{{short description|Courtship behavior of humans}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=November 2025}}[[Image:YoungCoupleEmbracing-20070508.jpg|thumb|A human couple|260x260px]]
{{Evolutionary biology}}
'''Human mating strategies''' are a topic of interest in ([[Evolutionary psychology|evolutionary]]) [[psychology]], [[behavioral ecology]], and [[anthropology]]. Strategies used to seek and retain [[Mating|mates]] often overlap with [[Human reproduction|reproductive]] strategies.

Humans may seek out individuals with the intention of forming a long-term [[intimate relationship]], marriage, [[casual relationship]], or ([[Romantic friendship|romantic]]) [[friendship]]. The desire for [[companionship]] and [[sex drive]] are some of the strongest motivations of human behavior. Relative to those of other animals, human mating strategies are unique in their relationship with cultural variables such as the institution of marriage.<ref>Low, B.S. (2007). [https://books.google.com/books?id=8K7Hc09xcQAC&dq=%22Ecological+and+socio-cultural+impacts+on+mating+and+marriage%22&pg=PA449 "Ecological and socio-cultural impacts on mating and marriage"]. ''Oxford Handbook of Evolutionary Psychology'', 449.</ref> Except in cases of arranged marriages, the human mating process involves one person meeting another to assess his or her suitability, [[courtship]], and the formation of an [[interpersonal relationship]]. Commonalities, however, can be found between humans and nonhuman animals in mating behavior, as in the case of [[animal sexual behavior]] in general and [[#Assortative mating|assortative mating]] in particular.

Human mating strategies also affect and are affected by [[Human sexuality|sexuality]], [[religion]], [[culture]], [[politics]], and [[economics]].

== Theoretical background ==
=== Parental investment ===
Research on human mating strategies is guided by the theory of [[Sexual selection in humans|sexual selection]], and in particular, [[Robert Trivers]]' concept of [[parental investment]]. Trivers defined parental investment as "any investment by the parent in an individual offspring that increases the offspring's chance of surviving (and hence [[reproductive success]]) at the cost of the parent's ability to invest in other offspring."<ref name="Trivers-1972">{{Cite book|chapter=Parental Investment and Sexual Selection|date=1972|title=Sexual Selection and the Descent of Man|pages=136–179|editor-last=Trivers|editor-first=Robert L.|publisher=Routledge|doi=10.4324/9781315129266-7|isbn=978-1-315-12926-6}}</ref> The support given to each offspring typically differs between the father and mother. Trivers posited that it is the differential parental investment between males and females that drives the process of sexual selection. In turn, sexual selection leads to the evolution of [[sexual dimorphism]] in [[mate choice]], competitive ability, and [[courtship display]]s (see [[secondary sex characteristics]]).[[File:William-Adolphe Bouguereau (1825-1905) - Maternal Admiration (1869)FXD.jpg|left|thumb|''Maternal Admiration'' (1869) by [[William-Adolphe Bouguereau]]. Females have a higher minimal investment in offspring than males.]]Minimum parental investment is the least required care for successful reproduction. In humans, females have a higher minimum parental investment. They have to invest in internal fertilization, placentation, and [[gestation]], followed by childbirth and [[lactation]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Lovejoy |first=C. O. |date=23 January 1981 |title=The Origin of Man |journal=Science |volume=211 |issue=4480 |pages=341–350 |doi=10.1126/science.211.4480.341 |pmid=17748254 |bibcode=1981Sci...211..341L |issn=0036-8075}}</ref> While human males can invest heavily in their offspring as well, their minimum parental investment is still lower than that of females.<ref name="Low-2000">{{Cite book|last=Low|first=B.S.|title=[[Why Sex Matters]]|publisher=Princeton University Press|year=2000|isbn=0-691-08975-2|location=Princeton, NJ}}</ref>

This same concept can be looked at from an economic perspective regarding the costs of engaging in sexual relations. Females incur the higher costs, as they carry the possibility of becoming pregnant among other costs.<ref name="Buss-1993">Buss, D.M., & Schmitt, D.P. (1993). [https://labs.la.utexas.edu/buss/files/2015/09/SexualStrategiesTheory.pdf "Sexual strategies theory: an evolutionary perspective on human mating"]. ''Psychological Review'', 100(2), 204.</ref> Conversely, males have comparatively minimal costs of having a sexual encounter. Therefore, evolutionary psychologists have predicted a number of sex differences in human mating psychologies.

Women tend to appreciate men who are chivalrous despite their sexist attitudes towards them. Because such men are more likely to invest in these women and their children, it makes evolutionary sense for women to be drawn towards them. They are likely to be more dependent on such men, to limit their own ambitions, and to submit to them.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gul |first1=Pelin |last2=Kupfer |first2=Tom R. |title=Benevolent Sexism and Mate Preferences: Why Do Women Prefer Benevolent Men Despite Recognizing That They Can Be Undermining? |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |date=1 January 2019 |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=146–161 |doi=10.1177/0146167218781000 |url=https://kar.kent.ac.uk/72765/1/Manuscript_BSMatePref_PSPB_Revision_02May2018%20(1).pdf |access-date=24 March 2026 |language=EN |issn=0146-1672}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last1=Gül|first1=Pelin|last2=Kupfer|first2=Tom R.|date=19 September 2018|title=Why women – including feminists – are still attracted to 'benevolently sexist' men|work=The Conversation|url=https://theconversation.com/why-women-including-feminists-are-still-attracted-to-benevolently-sexist-men-101067|access-date=9 September 2021}}</ref>

=== Life history strategies ===
{{See also|Life history theory#Human life history}}
Life history theory helps to explain differences in timing of sexual relationships, the quantity of sexual partners, and parental investment.<ref>{{Citation|last1=Kaplan|first1=Hillard S.|title=Life History Theory and Evolutionary Psychology|date=2015|work=The Handbook of Evolutionary Psychology|pages=68–95|publisher=John Wiley & Sons, Inc.|isbn=978-0-470-93937-6|last2=Gangestad|first2=Steven W.|doi=10.1002/9780470939376.ch2|hdl=2318/1852935 |hdl-access=free}}</ref> According to this theory, since organisms have only a [[Scarcity|limited supply]] of energy, which they use to develop their bodies, they must prioritize energy use and they do so on a spectrum. At one end of the spectrum, the organism chooses rapid physical development and reaches sexual maturation quickly, which is deemed a "fast" strategy.<ref name="Rushton-1985">{{Cite journal|last=Rushton|first=J.Philippe|date=1985|title=Differential K theory: The sociobiology of individual and group differences|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=6|issue=4|pages=441–452|doi=10.1016/0191-8869(85)90137-0|issn=0191-8869}}</ref> Organisms implementing a "fast" strategy seek to have sexual relationships earlier, multiple mates, and to invest less in their offspring. On the other end of the spectrum is the "slow" strategy, in which organisms have sexual relationships later, fewer mates, and invest more heavily in their offspring.

Generally, fast strategies are pursued in populations that are ''[[R/K selection theory|r]]''[[R/K selection theory|-selected]] (where ''r'' is the maximal intrinsic value of natural increase), and slow strategies are developed in populations that are ''K''-selected (''K'' being the [[carrying capacity]] of the local environment, or how many individuals within a population that their environment can support). Species that are ''r''-selected tend to reproduce faster, be specialists, and to be smaller. ''K''-selected organisms have fewer offspring over the course of their lifetimes and tend to live longer; they are more likely to be larger and to be generalists. Species exist along an ''r-K'' continuum, rather than being one or the other. Humans are considered a ''K''-selected species, meaning that on the whole, they pursue "slow" strategies relative to other species.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Pianka |first=Eric R. |date=November 1970 |title=On r- and K-Selection |url=https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/282697 |journal=The American Naturalist |language=en |volume=104 |issue=940 |pages=592–597 |doi=10.1086/282697 |bibcode=1970ANat..104..592P |issn=0003-0147|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Life-history characteristics include age at sexual maturity, [[gestation]] period, birth weight, litter size, postnatal growth rates, [[breastfeeding]] duration, [[birth spacing]], length of juvenile dependence, level of [[parental investment]], adult body size, and longevity. Variation in these traits between individuals, according to life-history theory, is due to [[homeostasis]], reproduction, and growth. For example, if more of one species' resources are going towards reproduction than physical growth, then the age at which they reach sexual maturity will be earlier than a species that devotes more energy to physical growth.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ellis |first1=Bruce J. |last2=Figueredo |first2=Aurelio José |last3=Brumbach |first3=Barbara H. |last4=Schlomer |first4=Gabriel L. |date=1 June 2009 |title=Fundamental Dimensions of Environmental Risk |journal=Human Nature |language=en |volume=20 |issue=2 |pages=204–268 |doi=10.1007/s12110-009-9063-7 |pmid=25526958 |s2cid=20904498 |issn=1936-4776}}</ref>

These strategies are unconscious and help increase the organism's reproductive success in a given environment. Early childhood environments may play a part in which strategy a person unconsciously pursues.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Chisholm|first=James|date=1993|title=Death, Hope, and Sex: Life-History Theory and the Development of Reproductive Strategies|journal=Current Anthropology|volume=34|issue=1|pages=1–24|doi=10.1086/204131|jstor=2743728|s2cid=144401423}}</ref> In a hostile environment, risk and unpredictability is increased and therefore survival is a higher priority. A "fast" strategy is more likely to be pursued by populations living in hostile environments in order to reach maturity and reproduce quickly.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Griskevicius|first1=Vladas|last2=Tybur|first2=Joshua M.|last3=Delton|first3=Andrew W.|last4=Robertson|first4=Theresa E.|date=2011|title=The influence of mortality and socioeconomic status on risk and delayed rewards: A life history theory approach.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=100|issue=6|pages=1015–1026|doi=10.1037/a0022403|pmid=21299312|pmc=3298774|bibcode=2011JPSP..100.1015G |issn=1939-1315}}</ref> In less risky environments, populations are more likely to pursue a "slow" strategy to physically develop first and then reproduce. This concept has been applied to humans as well, though there are differences in life history strategy application both between and within species.<ref name="Rushton-1985" />

==== Challenges with applying life history strategies to humans ====
Human life history theories in psychology focus on behavioral choices like mate choice and parenting effort (see [[evolutionary anthropology]]), while in evolutionary ecology, they focus on allocation of energy to maximize success and reproduction.<ref name="Sear2020">{{Cite journal |last=Sear |first=Rebecca |date=1 November 2020 |title=Do human 'life history strategies' exist? |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1090513820301197 |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |series=Current debates in human life history research |volume=41 |issue=6 |pages=513–526 |bibcode=2020EHumB..41..513S |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2020.09.004 |issn=1090-5138 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> Evolutionary psychologists use three metrics to describe environments that predict which life history strategy people will choose: resource availability, harshness, and unpredictability.<ref>Frankenhuis, Willem E; Gergely, Gyorgy; Watson, John S. "Infants May Use Contingency Analysis to EstimateEnvironmental States: An Evolutionary, Life-HistoryPerspective". ''Child Development Perspectives''. '''7'''(2): 115–120.</ref> For example, [[Food security|food insecurity]] is an example of low resource availability, and frequent relocation or switching schools are signs of unpredictability. Harshness and unpredictability come into play when resource availability is satisfied, because without resources, individuals have few opportunities to mature and reproduce. Smoking, weak health, and poor personal care are all traits that are correlated with earlier sexual experiences, earlier births, and having more short-term sex partners.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mell |first1=Hugo |last2=Safra |first2=Lou |last3=Algan |first3=Yann |last4=Baumard |first4=Nicolas |last5=Chevallier |first5=Coralie |date=1 January 2018 |title=Childhood environmental harshness predicts coordinated health and reproductive strategies: A cross-sectional study of a nationally representative sample from France |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1090513817300338 |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=39 |issue=1 |pages=1–8 |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2017.08.006 |bibcode=2018EHumB..39....1M |issn=1090-5138}}</ref> However, sexual behaviors can vary across cultures and classes, and might not have a simple relationship with childhood experiences.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Richardson |first1=George B. |last2=Placek |first2=Caitlyn |last3=Srinivas |first3=Vijaya |last4=Jayakrishna |first4=Poornima |last5=Quinlan |first5=Robert |last6=Madhivanan |first6=Purnima |date=1 May 2020 |title=Environmental stress and human life history strategy development in rural and peri-urban South India |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1090513820300441 |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |language=en-US |volume=41 |issue=3 |pages=244–252 |bibcode=2020EHumB..41..244R |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2020.03.003 |issn=1090-5138 |s2cid=216175091 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> [[Extrinsic mortality]] (the harshness of an individual's environment) does not directly affect whether people adopt a fast or slow strategy.<ref name="André-2020">{{Cite journal |last1=André |first1=Jean-Baptiste |last2=Rousset |first2=François |date=1 November 2020 |title=Does extrinsic mortality accelerate the pace of life? A bare-bones approach |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109051382030043X |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |series=Current debates in human life history research |volume=41 |issue=6 |pages=486–492 |bibcode=2020EHumB..41..486A |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2020.03.002 |issn=1090-5138}}</ref> Pace-of-life studies indicate that the link between individuals experiencing difficult environments growing up and their later sexual behavior may be tenuous, or else too muddied with confounding variables to track.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Royauté |first1=Raphaël |last2=Berdal |first2=Monica Anderson |last3=Garrison |first3=Courtney R. |last4=Dochtermann |first4=Ned A. |date=23 March 2018 |title=Paceless life? A meta-analysis of the pace-of-life syndrome hypothesis |journal=Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology |language=en |volume=72 |issue=3 |page=64 |doi=10.1007/s00265-018-2472-z |bibcode=2018BEcoS..72...64R |s2cid=253820435 |issn=1432-0762 |url=https://hal.inrae.fr/hal-03955414 }}</ref>

Behavior sciences might not, in general, be a good framework in which to consider life-history theory. Biological life-history theory is based on tradeoffs between energy expenditure and the benefits of reproduction, and these tradeoffs are difficult to measure in humans due to the inability to ascertain tradeoffs among phenotypically different individuals, poor models for tradeoffs, and a reliance on [[Alloparenting|allo-parental investment]].<ref name="Jones-2021">{{Cite journal |last1=Jones |first1=James Holland |last2=Promislow |first2=Daniel E. L. |date=1 May 2021 |title=Commentary on the life history special issue: The behavioral sciences need to engage more deeply in life history theory |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1090513821000192 |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=42 |issue=3 |pages=284–286 |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2021.03.001 |bibcode=2021EHumB..42..284J |s2cid=233842007 |issn=1090-5138|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Life-history theory could be made more applicable in humans by incorporating the recognition that individuals tend to [[Time preference|discount the future]], meaning they see their assets as more valuable in the present than in the future.<ref name="Jones-2021" /> Individuals who place a higher "discount rate" on their reproductive abilities, or see it as much more valuable now than later, are more likely to mate earlier and pursue fast strategies.<ref name="Jones-2021" />

== Mating strategies ==
There is vast variety in mating strategies across genders, cultures and environments.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Frederick |first1=David A. |url=https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordhb/9780197536438.013.25 |title=The Oxford Handbook of Human Mating |last2=Alley |first2=Jenna C. |last3=Semenyna |first3=Scott W. |last4=Garcia |first4=Justin R. |date=2023 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-753643-8 |edition=1 |language=en |chapter=Mating Strategy Variation by Sexual Orientation |access-date=27 March 2026}}</ref> Similar mating strategies are used by both men and women. They have also evolved to use broadly distinct strategies.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lawson |first1=Jamie F. |last2=James |first2=Christine |last3=Jannson |first3=Anna-Ulla C. |last4=Koyama |first4=Nicola F. |last5=Hill |first5=Russell A. |date=1 September 2014 |title=A comparison of heterosexual and homosexual mating preferences in personal advertisements |url=https://researchonline.ljmu.ac.uk/id/eprint/8508/1/A%20comparison%20of%20heterosexual%20and%20homosexual%20mating%20preferences%20in%20personal%20advertisements.pdf |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=35 |issue=5 |pages=408–414 |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2014.05.006 |issn=1090-5138 |access-date=27 March 2026}}</ref>

=== Sex similarities ===

==== Assortative mating ====
{{Main|Assortative mating}}

Human mating is inherently non-random.<ref name="Buss-1989" /> Despite the stereotype that "opposite" people attract one another, humans generally prefer mates who share the same or similar traits,<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Buss|first=David|date=January–February 1985|title=Human Mate Selection|url=|journal=American Scientist|publisher=Sigma Xi, The Scientific Research Honor Society|volume=73|issue=1|pages=47–51|jstor=27853061|bibcode=1985AmSci..73...47B}}</ref> such as genetics,<ref>{{Cite news|last=University of Colorado at Boulder|date=19 May 2014|title=I like your genes: People more likely to choose a spouse with similar DNA|work=Science Daily|url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2014/05/140519160716.htm|access-date=5 March 2021}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Fields|first=R. Douglas|date=14 February 2019|title=Humans Are Still Mating with Neandertals|url=https://blogs.scientificamerican.com/observations/humans-are-still-mating-with-neandertals/|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210203180500/https://blogs.scientificamerican.com/observations/humans-are-still-mating-with-neandertals/|archive-date=3 February 2021|access-date=5 March 2021|website=Scientific American}}</ref> quantitative phenotypes like height or [[Body mass index|body-mass index]],<ref>{{Cite journal|last=|first=|date=January 1903|title=Assortative Mating in Man: A Cooperative Study|url=|journal=Biometrika|volume=2|issue=4|pages=481–498|doi=10.2307/2331510|jstor=2331510}}</ref><ref name="Robinson-2017">{{Cite journal|last1=Robinson|first1=Matthew R.|last2=Kleinman|first2=Aaron|last3=Graff|first3=Mariaelisa|display-authors=2|date=9 January 2017|title=Genetic evidence of assortative mating in humans|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41562-016-0016|journal=Nature Human Behaviour|volume=1|issue=16|article-number=0016 |doi=10.1038/s41562-016-0016|s2cid=9084485|via=|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=CORDIS|date=13 January 2017|title=New evidence that humans choose their partners through assortative mating|work=Phys.org|department=Biology|url=https://phys.org/news/2017-01-evidence-humans-partners-assortative.html|access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref> skin pigmentation,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Chick|first1=Garry|last2=Proyer|first2=René|last3=Purrington|first3=Andrew|last4=Yarnal|first4=Careen|date=2020|title=Do Birds of a Playful Feather Flock Together? Playfulness and Assortative Mating|url=https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ1255234|journal=American Journal of Play|volume=12|issue=2|issn=1938-0399}}</ref> the level of physical attractiveness,<ref name="Hutson-2015">{{Cite news|last=Hutson|first=Matthew|date=January–February 2015|title=You're Just Like Me!|work=The Atlantic|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2015/01/youre-just-like-me/383502/|url-status=live|access-date=14 February 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201128165845/https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2015/01/youre-just-like-me/383502/|archive-date=28 November 2020}}</ref> disease risk (including cancers and mental disorders),<ref name="Robinson-2017" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Nordsletten|first1=Ashley E.|last2=Larsson|first2=Henrik|last3=Crowley|first3=James J.|last4=Almqvist|first4=Catarina|date=2016|title=Patterns of Nonrandom Mating Within and Across 11 Major Psychiatric Disorders|url=|journal=JAMA Psychiatry|publisher=American Medical Association|volume=73|issue=4|pages=354–361|doi=10.1001/jamapsychiatry.2015.3192|pmc=5082975|pmid=26913486}}</ref> race or ethnicity,<ref name="Mare-1991">{{Cite journal|last=Mare|first=Robert D.|date=February 1991|title=Five Decades of Educational Assortative Mating|journal=American Sociological Review|volume=56|issue=1|pages=15–32|doi=10.2307/2095670|jstor=2095670}}</ref><ref name="Norris-2019">{{Cite journal|last1=Norris|first1=Emily|last2=Rishishwar|first2=Lavanya|last3=Wang|first3=Lu|display-authors=2|date=29 April 2019|title=Assortative Mating on Ancestry-Variant Traits in Admixed Latin American Populations|journal=Frontiers in Genetics|volume=10|article-number=359|doi=10.3389/fgene.2019.00359|pmid=31105740|pmc=6491930|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Vandenberg-1972">{{Cite journal|last=Vandenberg|first=Steven G.|date=June 1972|title=Assortative mating, or who marries whom?|url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF01065686|journal=Behavior Genetics|volume=2|issue=2–3|pages=127–157|doi=10.1007/BF01065686|pmid=4664207|s2cid=145461385|via=|url-access=subscription}}</ref> facial features,<ref name="Hutson-2015" /> socioeconomic factors (such as (potential) income level and occupational prestige),<ref name="Greenwood-2014">{{Cite journal|last1=Greenwood|first1=Jeremy|last2=Nezih|first2=Guner|last3=Kocharkov|first3=Georgi|last4=Santos|first4=Cezar|date=January 2014|title=Marry Your Like: Assortative Mating and Income Inequality|url=http://www.nber.org/papers/w19829|journal=Working Paper Series|publisher=National Bureau of Economic Research|volume=|issue=19829|pages=|doi=10.3386/w19829|via=|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="The Economist-2017">{{Cite news|date=21 September 2017|title=Marital choices are exacerbating household income inequality|newspaper=The Economist|url=https://www.economist.com/finance-and-economics/2017/09/21/marital-choices-are-exacerbating-household-income-inequality|access-date=20 December 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201207080341/https://www.economist.com/finance-and-economics/2017/09/21/marital-choices-are-exacerbating-household-income-inequality |archive-date=December 7, 2020}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Reeves|first=Richard|date=8 April 2016|title=The rich marrying the rich makes the income gap worse, but it's not our biggest problem|url=https://www.brookings.edu/blog/social-mobility-memos/2016/04/08/the-rich-marrying-the-rich-makes-the-income-gap-worse-but-its-not-our-biggest-problem/|access-date=9 January 2021|website=Brookings Institution}}</ref> cultural backgrounds,<ref name="Vandenberg-1972" /><ref name="Kalmijn-1994">{{Cite journal|last=Kalmijn|first=Matthijs|date=September 1994|title=Assortative Mating by Cultural and Economic Occupational Status|url=https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/230542|journal=American Journal of Sociology|publisher=University of Chicago Press|volume=100|issue=2|pages=422–52|doi=10.1086/230542|s2cid=131770740|url-access=subscription|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220221141443/https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/230542 |archive-date=February 21, 2022 |access-date=March 3, 2021}}</ref> moral values,<ref name="Kalmijn-1994" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Miller|first=Geoffrey|author-link=Geoffrey Miller (psychologist)|date=June 2007|title=Sexual Selection for Moral Virtues|url=https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/abs/10.1086/517857|journal=The Quarterly Review of Biology|publisher=University of Chicago Press|volume=82|issue=2|pages=97–125|doi=10.1086/517857|pmid=17583267|s2cid=1021904|url-access=subscription}}</ref> religious beliefs,<ref name="Hutson-2015" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Schwartz|first=Christine R.|year=2013|title=Trends and Variation in Assortative Mating: Causes and Consequences|journal=Annual Review of Sociology|language=en|volume=39|issue=1|pages=451–470|doi=10.1146/annurev-soc-071312-145544}}</ref> political orientation,<ref name="Hutson-2015" /><ref name="Gibbons-2017">{{Cite web|last=Gibbons|first=Ann|date=9 January 2017|title=Your choice of a life partner is no accident|url=https://www.science.org/content/article/your-choice-life-partner-no-accident|access-date=10 January 2021|website=Science Magazine}}</ref> (perceived) [[Big Five personality traits|personality traits]] (such as conscientiousness or extraversion),<ref name="Robinson-2017" /><ref name="Mare-1991" /><ref name="Little-2006">{{Cite journal|last1=Little|first1=Anthony C.|last2=Burt|first2=D. Michael|last3=Perrett|first3=David I.|date=April 2006|title=Assortative mating for perceived facial personality traits|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886905003624|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=40|issue=5|pages=973–984|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2005.09.016|via=|url-access=subscription}}</ref> behavioral characteristics<ref name="Gibbons-2017" /> (such as the level of generosity or the propensity for alcoholism),<ref name="Hutson-2015" /> educational attainment,<ref name="Mare-1991" /><ref name="Gibbons-2017" /><ref name="TheEconomist2014">{{Cite news|date=8 February 2014|title=Sex, brains and inequality|newspaper=The Economist|url=https://www.economist.com/united-states/2014/02/08/sex-brains-and-inequality|access-date=20 December 2020|url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201201132501/https://www.economist.com/united-states/2014/02/08/sex-brains-and-inequality |archive-date=December 1, 2020 }}</ref> and IQ or [[G factor (psychometrics)|general intelligence]].<ref name="The Economist-2017" /><ref name="Miller-2016">{{Cite news|last1=Miller|first1=Claire Cain|last2=Bui|first2=Quoctrung|date=27 February 2016|title=Equality in Marriages Grows, and So Does Class Divide|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/02/23/upshot/rise-in-marriages-of-equals-and-in-division-by-class.html|url-status=live|access-date=1 January 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201224010231/https://www.nytimes.com/2016/02/23/upshot/rise-in-marriages-of-equals-and-in-division-by-class.html|archive-date=24 December 2020}}</ref><ref name="Plomin-2014">{{Cite journal|last1=Plomin|first1=R.|last2=Deary|first2=I. J.|date=16 September 2014|title=Genetics and intelligence differences: five special findings|journal=Molecular Psychiatry|publisher=Nature|volume=20|issue=1|pages=98–108|doi=10.1038/mp.2014.105|pmid=25224258|pmc=4270739|doi-access=free}}</ref> Furthermore, in the past, marriage across status lines was more common. Women typically looked for a man of high status ([[hypergamy]]), a sign of access to resources. Men, for their part, were usually willing to marry down the socioeconomic ladder (hypogamy) if the woman possessed domestic skills, or was young and good looking (proxies of fertility).<ref name="Mare-1991" /><ref name="Norris-2019" /> In the modern world, people tend to desire well-educated and [[Heritability of IQ|intelligent children]]; this goal is better achieved by marrying bright people with high incomes, resulting in the intensification of economic assortative mating.<ref name="The Economist-2017" /><ref name="Miller-2016" /> Indeed, better educated parents tend to have children who are not only well-educated but also healthy and successful.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kaushal |first=Neeraj |date=2014 |title=Intergenerational payoffs of education |journal=The Future of Children |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=61–78 |doi=10.1353/foc.2014.0005|pmid=25518703 |s2cid=10209820 }}</ref> For this reason, when judging the value of a potential mate, people commonly consider the other person's grasp of grammar (a proxy of socioeconomic status of educational level), teeth quality (indicators of health and age), and self-confidence (psychological stability).<ref name="Fisher-2023" /> Furthermore, the age gap between two partners has also declined.<ref name="Vandenberg-1972" /> In other words, men and women became more symmetrical in the socioeconomic traits they desire in a mate.<ref name="Mare-1991" /> Among the aforementioned traits, the correlations in age, race or ethnicity, religion, educational attainment, and intelligence between spouses are the most pronounced,<ref name="Plomin-2014" /><ref name="Li-2017">{{Cite journal|last1=Li|first1=Xiaoyin|last2=Redline|first2=Susan|last3=Zhang|first3=Xiang|last4=Williams|first4=Scott|last5=Zhu|first5=Xiaofeng|date=16 November 2017|title=Height associated variants demonstrate assortative mating in human populations|journal=Scientific Reports|publisher=Nature|volume=7|issue=15689|page=15689|doi=10.1038/s41598-017-15864-x|pmid=29146993|pmc=5691191|bibcode=2017NatSR...715689L}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Fry |first=Richard |date=7 September 2022 |title=College grads in U.S. tend to partner with each other – especially if their parents also graduated from college |work=Pew Research Center |url=https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2022/09/07/college-grads-in-u-s-tend-to-partner-with-each-other-especially-if-their-parents-also-graduated-from-college/ |access-date=8 September 2022}}</ref> while height is one of the most heritable,<ref name="Li-2017" /><ref name="BioMed Central-2016">{{Cite news|last=BioMed Central|date=19 January 2016|title=Physical attraction linked to genes that control height|work=Science Daily|url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2016/01/160119141424.htm|access-date=14 February 2021}}</ref> with mating partners sharing 89% of the genetic variations affecting the preference for height.<ref name="BioMed Central-2016" />

It is not unusual for couples to look alike (as if they were related). Besides assortative mating, some people are unconsciously attracted by their own faces or prefer familiar-looking ones for ease of cognitive processing. People who are emotionally close to their opposite-sex parents may be prone to unknowingly selecting mates bearing resemblance to said parents, who served as role models for what a desirable mate should be like, a phenomenon called [[Imprinting (psychology)|sexual imprinting]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Wu |first=Karen |date=June 2022 |title=Why So Many Couples Look Alike? |journal=Psychology Today |pages=18–19}}</ref>

Public secondary school is the last time people of various backgrounds are lumped together in the same setting. After that, they begin sorting themselves out by various measures of social screening.<ref name="McAndrew-2016">{{Cite news|last=McAndrew|first=Frank T.|date=1 June 2016|title=Why high school stays with us forever|work=The Conversation|url=https://theconversation.com/why-high-school-stays-with-us-forever-56538|access-date=16 June 2021}}</ref> Among those marrying late (relative to the time when they left school), socioeconomic status is especially important. In societies where the numbers of highly educated and career-minded women are increasing, the role of socioeconomic status is likely to be even more important in the future.<ref name="Kalmijn-1994" /> These women generally do not choose to mate with men who are less occupationally and educationally accomplished than they are. For this reason, in societies where they outnumber men, the competition for those desirable males has been intensifying. This trend first emerged in Europe and North America but has been spreading to other places as well.<ref name="Buss-2016">{{Cite magazine|last=Buss|first=David|date=2016|title=The Mating Crisis Among Educated Women|url=https://www.edge.org/response-detail/26747|access-date=16 June 2021|magazine=Edge}}</ref> However, a growing number of women are avoiding relationships with men altogether.<ref name="Burn-Murdoch-2024a" />
Positive assortative mating raises the chances of a given trait being passed on to the couple's offspring,<ref name="Gibbons-2017" /> strengthens the bond between the parents,<ref name="Little-2006" /><ref name="Thiessen-1980">{{Cite journal|last1=Thiessen|first1=Del|last2=Gregg|first2=Barbara|date=April 1980|title=Human assortative mating and genetic equilibrium: An evolutionary perspective|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0162309580900035|journal=Ethology and Sociobiology|volume=1|issue=2|pages=111–140|doi=10.1016/0162-3095(80)90003-5|via=|url-access=subscription}}</ref> and increases genetic similarity between family members, whereupon in-group altruism and [[Inclusive fitness in humans|inclusive fitness]] are enhanced.<ref name="Thiessen-1980" /> That the two partners are culturally compatible reduces uncertainty in lifestyle choices and ensures social support.<ref name="Kalmijn-1994" /> In some cases, homogamy can also increase the couple's fertility<ref name="Vandenberg-1972" /><ref name="Thiessen-1980" /> and the number of offspring surviving till adulthood.<ref name="O'Neil-2014">{{Cite web|last=O'Neil|first=Dennis|date=2014|title=Modern Theories of Evolution: Non-Random Mating|url=https://www2.palomar.edu/anthro/synthetic/synth_8.htm|access-date=14 February 2021|website=Palomar Community College}}</ref> [[File:Global prevalence of consanguinity.svg|left|thumb|Prevalence of consanguineous marriages (second-degree cousins or closer) by country.]]
On the other hand, there is evolutionary pressure against mating with people too genetically similar to oneself, such as members of the same nuclear family.<ref name="Thiessen-1980" /> In addition, children born into [[Cousin marriage#Biological aspects|parents who are cousins]] have an increased risk of [[autosomal recessive]] [[genetic disorder]]s, and this risk is higher in populations that are already highly ethnically homogeneous.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Hamamy|first=Hanan|date=July 2012|title=Consanguineous marriages|journal=Journal of Community Genetics|volume=3|issue=3|pages=185–192|doi=10.1007/s12687-011-0072-y|issn=1868-310X|pmc=3419292|pmid=22109912}}</ref> Children of more distantly related cousins have less risk of these disorders, though still higher than the average population. Therefore, humans tend to maximize the genetic similarity of their mates while avoiding excessive [[inbreeding]] or [[incest]].<ref name="Thiessen-1980" /> First-cousin marriages nowadays are rare<ref name="O'Neil-2014" /> and are in fact prohibited in a number of jurisdictions worldwide.<ref name="truth">{{cite web|date=14 February 2014|title=The Surprising Truth About Cousins and Marriage|url=http://www.todayifoundout.com/index.php/2014/02/people-stop-thinking-appropriate-cousins-marry/}}</ref><ref name="plos">{{cite journal|last1=Paul|first1=Diane B.|last2=Spencer|first2=Hamish G.|date=23 December 2008|title="It's Ok, We're Not Cousins by Blood": The Cousin Marriage Controversy in Historical Perspective|journal=PLOS Biology|volume=6|issue=12|pages=2627–30|doi=10.1371/journal.pbio.0060320|pmc=2605922|pmid=19108607 |doi-access=free }}</ref> In general, humans seem to prefer mates who are (the equivalent of) second or higher-parity cousins.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Zou|first1=James Y.|last2=Park|first2=Danny S.|last3=Burchard|first3=Esteban G.|last4=Torgerson|first4=Dara G.|last5=Pino-Yanes|first5=Maria|last6=Song|first6=Yun S.|last7=Sankararaman|first7=Sriram|last8=Halperin|first8=Eran|last9=Zaitlen|first9=Noah|date=3 November 2015|title=Genetic and socioeconomic study of mate choice in Latinos reveals novel assortment patterns|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America|volume=112|issue=44|pages=13621–13626|doi=10.1073/pnas.1501741112|pmid=26483472|pmc=4640764|bibcode=2015PNAS..11213621Z|doi-access=free}}</ref> Genetic analyses of the United States suggest that the genomic correlation between spouses is comparable to that between second cousins.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Guo|first1=Guang|last2=Wang|first2=Lin|last3=Liu|first3=Hexuan|last4=Randall|first4=Thomas|date=10 November 2014|editor-last=DeAngelis|editor-first=Margaret M.|title=Genomic Assortative Mating in Marriages in the United States|journal=PLOS ONE|volume=9|issue=11|article-number=e112322|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0112322|pmid=25384046|pmc=4226554|bibcode=2014PLoSO...9k2322G|doi-access=free}}</ref> Another study indicated that between 1800 and 1965 in Iceland, more children and grandchildren were produced from marriages between third or fourth cousins (people with common great-great- or great-great-great-grandparents) than from other degrees of [[consanguinity]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Swaminathan |first=Nikhil |date=8 February 2008 |title=When Incest Is Best: Kissing Cousins Have More Kin |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/when-incest-is-best-kissi/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210314185120/https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/when-incest-is-best-kissi/ |archive-date=14 March 2021 |access-date=5 September 2021 |work=Scientific American}}</ref> In the past, there was indeed some awareness of the dangers of inbreeding, as can be seen in legal prohibitions in some societies, while in the current era, better transportation infrastructure makes it less likely to occur.<ref name="Vandenberg-1972" /> Moreover, modern transportation has diminished residential propinquity as a factor in assortative mating.<ref name="Vandenberg-1972" /> But cultural anthropologists have noted that avoidance of inbreeding cannot be the sole basis for the incest taboo because the boundaries of the incest prohibition vary widely between cultures and time periods, and not necessarily in ways that maximize the avoidance of inbreeding.<ref name="WolfDurham2005">{{cite book|author1-link=Arthur P. Wolf|last1=Wolf|first1=Arthur P.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OW1nuQxcIQgC&pg=PA3|title=Inbreeding, Incest, and the Incest Taboo: The State of Knowledge at the Turn of the Century|last2=Durham|first2=William H.|publisher=Stanford University Press|year=2004|isbn=978-0-8047-5141-4|page=3}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Schneider|first1=D. M.|year=1976|title=The meaning of incest|journal=The Journal of the Polynesian Society|volume=85|issue=2|pages=149–169}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=White|first1=L. A.|year=1948|title=The definition and prohibition of incest|journal=American Anthropologist|volume=50|issue=3|pages=416–435|doi=10.1525/aa.1948.50.3.02a00020|pmid=18874938|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Schechner|first1=R|year=1971|title=Incest and culture: A reflection on Claude Lévi-Strauss|journal=Psychoanalytic Review|volume=58|issue=4|pages=563–72|pmid=4948055}}</ref> In [[Roman Egypt]], marriage between (biological) siblings was accepted.<ref name=":22">{{Cite news |last=Murphy |first=Clare |date=March 12, 2007 |title=Incest: an age-old taboo |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/6424337.stm |access-date=December 6, 2025 |work=BBC News}}</ref> France dropped incest from its legal code during the [[Napoleonic era]], though it remains illegal for siblings to marry.<ref name=":22" /> In the Middle East, marriage between cousins is common.<ref>{{Cite news |date=September 5, 2006 |title=When Cousins Get Married |url=https://abcnews.go.com/2020/Stossel/story?id=2395516 |access-date=December 6, 2025 |work=ABC News}}</ref> On the other hand, many parts of the United States have proscribed first-cousin marriage since the nineteenth century.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Kershaw |first=Sarah |date=November 25, 2009 |title=Shaking Off the Shame |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/11/26/garden/26cousins.html |archive-url=https://archive.today/20240719165534/https://www.nytimes.com/2009/11/26/garden/26cousins.html |archive-date=July 19, 2024 |access-date=December 6, 2025 |work=The New York Times}}</ref> There is no evidence that the actual prevalence of incest is affected by its legal status.<ref name=":22" />

While human assortative mating is usually positive,<ref name="Robinson-2017" /> in the case of the [[major histocompatibility complex]] (MHC) on chromosome 6, humans tend to be more attracted to those who are genetically different in this region, judging from their odors.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Wedekind |first=Claus |author-link=Claus Wedekind |last2=Seebeck |first2=Thomas |last3=Bettens |first3=Florence |last4=Paepke |first4=Alexander J. |date=June 22, 1995 |title=MHC-dependent mate preferences in humans |url=https://royalsocietypublishing.org/rspb/article-abstract/260/1359/245/69089/MHC-dependent-mate-preferences-in-humans |journal=[[Proceedings of the Royal Society of London]] |volume=260 |issue=1359 |pages=245–249 |doi=10.1098/rspb.1995.0087}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Wedekind |first=Claus |last2=Füri |first2=Sandra |date=October 22, 1997 |title=Body odour preferences in men and women: do they aim for specific MHC combinations or simply heterozygosity? |url=https://royalsocietypublishing.org/rspb/article-abstract/264/1387/1471/70022/Body-odour-preferences-in-men-and-women-do-they |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London |volume=264 |issue=1387 |pages=1471–1479 |doi=10.1098/rspb.1997.0204}}</ref> This promotes MHC heterogeneity in their offspring, making them more resistant to pathogens.<ref name="moral">{{Cite journal|last1=Lieberman|first1=D.|last2=Tooby|first2=J.|last3=Cosmides|first3=L.|year=2003|title=Does morality have a biological basis? An empirical test of the factors governing moral sentiments relating to incest|journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|volume=270|issue=1517|pages=819–826|doi=10.1098/rspb.2002.2290|pmc=1691313|pmid=12737660}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Chaix|first1=Raphaëlle|last2=Cao|first2=Chen|last3=Donnelly|first3=Peter|date=12 September 2008|title=Is Mate Choice in Humans MHC-Dependent?|journal=PLOS Genetics|volume=4|issue=9|article-number=e1000184|doi=10.1371/journal.pgen.1000184|issn=1553-7404|pmc=2519788|pmid=18787687 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Another example of negative assortative mating is among people with traits linked to high testosterone (such as analytical thinking and [[Spatial ability|spatial reasoning]]) and those traits due to high estrogen ([[empathy]] and social skills). They generally find each other appealing.<ref name="Fisher-2023">{{Cite journal |last=Fisher |first=Helen |author-link=Helen Fisher (anthropologist) |date=August 2023 |title=The Science of Love at First Sight |journal=Psychology Today |page=30}}</ref>

Assortative mating is partly due to social effects. For instance, religious people are more likely to meet their potential mates in their places of worship while highly educated people typically meet their future spouses in institutions of higher learning.<ref name="Hutson-2015" /> Nevertheless, it can have a quantitatively discernible impact upon the human genome and as such has implications for [[human evolution]] even in the presence of population stratification.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Yengo|first1=Loic|last2=Robinson|first2=Matthew R.|last3=Keller|first3=Matthew C.|display-authors=2|date=2018|title=Imprint of assortative mating on the human genome|journal=Nature Human Behaviour|volume=2|issue=12|pages=948–954|doi=10.1038/s41562-018-0476-3|pmid=30988446|pmc=6705135}}</ref> [[Pleiotropy]], or the phenomenon in which a single gene can influence multiple traits, and assortative mating are responsible for the correlations between some sexually selected traits in humans, such as height and IQ, which are weakly positively correlated.<ref>{{Cite news|last=University of Colorado at Boulder|date=27 August 2013|title=Study relies on twins and their parents to understand height-IQ connection|work=Science Daily|url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2013/08/130827135020.htm|access-date=14 February 2021}}</ref> In a knowledge-based economy, educational and socioeconomic assortative mating contributes to the growth in household income inequality, as parents with higher incomes and levels of education tend to invest more in their offspring, giving them an edge later in life.<ref name="Buss-2016" /><ref name="Greenwood-2014" /><ref name="The Economist-2017" /><ref name="Miller-2016" /><ref>{{Cite news|last=Muller|first=Jerry|date=11 April 2013|title=The Hard Truth About Economic Inequality That Both the Left and Right Ignore|work=PBS Newshour|url=https://www.pbs.org/newshour/economy/the-hard-nut-of-economic-inequality-what-the-left-and-right-both-ignore|access-date=5 March 2021}}</ref>

==== Dating ====
{{main|Dating}}

[[File:Arthur Hughes - The Sailing Signal Gun.jpg|thumb|''The Sailing Signal Gun'' (1880) by [[Arthur Hughes (artist)|Arthur Hughes]]. Humans engage in dating or courtship in order to assess their suitability as potential mates.]]
People date to assess each other's suitability as a partner in an [[intimate relationship]] or as a spouse. Dating rules may vary across different cultures, and some societies may even replace the dating process by a [[courtship]] instead.

==== Double standards and infidelity ====
{{see also|Hypocrisy}}

Both men and women apply one set of standards for themselves and another for their partners. They disapprove of their partners examining other options, but may very well do so themselves. Some even get worried if their partners are not [[Jealousy|jealous]] enough.<ref name="Bainbridge-2009" />{{Reference page|page=238}} In particular, what counts as sexual contact is different depending on the person engaging in the act, oneself or one's partner. If the person in question is the one to do it, they are unlikely to consider it infidelity compared to when their partner does it. Nevertheless, women are more likely than men to be judged harshly for their [[promiscuity]], even in the most gender-egalitarian of modern societies like Norway. In fact, women are the most aggressive in shaming other women for being promiscuous.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Buss |first=David |date=29 June 2021 |title='Cheating's OK for me, but not for thee' – inside the messy psychology of sexual double standards |work=The Conversation |url=https://theconversation.com/cheatings-ok-for-me-but-not-for-thee-inside-the-messy-psychology-of-sexual-double-standards-161642 |access-date=27 August 2021}}</ref> This sexual double standard can be explained by the biological sex difference between men and women; no matter how he acts, a man can never become pregnant.<ref name=":14" />{{Reference page|page=16}}

==== Flirting ====
{{main|Flirting}}

Although sexual intercourse is a motivation for flirting,<ref name="Motives to Remain Platonic, Equity">{{cite journal |last1=Messman |first1=Susan J |last2=Canary |first2=Daniel J |last3=Hause |first3=Kimberly |date=2000 |title=Motives to Remain Platonic, Equity, and the Use of Maintenance Strategies in Opposite-Sex Friendships |journal=Journal of Social and Personal Relationships |volume=16 |issue=67–94 |pages=67–94 |doi=10.1177/0265407500171004 |s2cid=145745343}}</ref> humans sometimes flirt just for fun.<ref name="Flirting with meaning: an examinati" /> They may also flirt in order to consolidate or maintain a romantic relationship with their partner.<ref name="Flirting with meaning: an examinati" /> Sociologist David Henningsen identified six main motivations for flirting: sex, relational development, exploration, fun, self-esteem, and as a means to an end. Henningsen further noted that many instances of flirtation may have multiple motivations.<ref name="Flirting with meaning: an examinati">{{cite journal |last1=Henningsen |first1=David |date=2004 |title=Flirting with meaning: an examination of miscommunication in flirting interactions |journal=Sex Roles |volume=50 |issue=7–8 |pages=481–489 |doi=10.1023/B:SERS.0000023068.49352.4b |s2cid=143077407}}</ref>

==== Kissing ====
[[File:Romantic kiss map.png|thumb|Prevalence of romantic or sexual kissing among indigenous cultures worldwide]]
[[Kiss]]ing is an activity found in many different [[Animal|species of animals]]. Although romantic or sexual kissing confers no known benefits for survival and reproduction, it is hypothesized that, like other primates, humans kiss to determine the health and suitability of a potential mate, for example by olfactory signals.<ref name=":15">{{Cite journal |last1=Brindle |first1=Matilda |last2=Talbot |first2=Catherine F. |last3=West |first3=Stuart |date=2025 |title=A comparative approach to the evolution of kissing |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1090513825001370 |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |issue=106788 |article-number=106788 |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2025.106788|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=":16">{{Cite news |last=Gill |first=Victoria |date=November 18, 2025 |title=First kiss dates back 21 million years, say scientists |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cr43gq61g2qo |access-date=November 20, 2025 |work=BBC News}}</ref> It is possible that there was kissing between anatomically modern humans (''Homo sapiens'') and the now extinct [[Neanderthal]]s,<ref name=":15" /><ref name=":16" /> with whom they [[Interbreeding between archaic and modern humans|interbred]],<ref>{{Cite news |last=Brindle |first=Matilda |date=November 19, 2025 |title=When did kissing evolve and did humans and Neanderthals get off with each other? New research |url=https://theconversation.com/when-did-kissing-evolve-and-did-humans-and-neanderthals-get-off-with-each-other-new-research-269210 |access-date=November 21, 2025 |work=The Conversation}}</ref> perhaps as early as 100,000 years ago, as suggested by studying the spread of the orally transmitted microorganism ''[[Methanobrevibacter oralis]]''.<ref name=":19" />

But while friendly or familial kissing was common in different places throughout human history, romantic or sexual kissing was by no means universal.<ref name=":18">{{Cite news |last=Banfield-Nwachi |first=Mabel |date=May 18, 2023 |title=First records of human kissing may date back 1,000 years earlier than estimated |url=https://www.theguardian.com/education/2023/may/18/first-records-of-human-kissing-may-date-back-1000-years-earlier-than-estimated |access-date=November 21, 2025 |work=The Guardian}}</ref> Written historical evidence suggests that this practice arose independently in different complex or stratified societies, such as India, [[Mesopotamia]], and Egypt during the early [[Bronze Age]], some centuries after the [[History of writing|invention of writing]], but did not necessarily spread to other places. Kissing is also more common in colder climates.<ref name=":19">{{Cite journal |last1=Arbøll |first1=Troels Pank |last2=Rasmussen |first2=Sophie Lund |date=18 May 2023 |title=The Ancient History of Kissing |department=Archeology |journal=Science |volume=380 |issue=6646 |pages=688–690 |bibcode=2023Sci...380..688A |doi=10.1126/science.adf0512 |pmid=37200431 |s2cid=258765170 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Page |first=Michael Le |date=18 May 2023 |title=Humans were kissing at least 4500 years ago, reveal ancient texts |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/2374337-humans-were-kissing-at-least-4500-years-ago-reveal-ancient-texts/ |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230519192321/https://www.newscientist.com/article/2374337-humans-were-kissing-at-least-4500-years-ago-reveal-ancient-texts/ |archive-date=19 May 2023 |access-date=22 May 2023 |work=New Scientist}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Pappas |first=Stephanie |date=18 May 2023 |title=The First Kiss in Recorded History Dates Back Nearly 5,000 Years |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/the-first-kiss-in-recorded-history-dates-back-nearly-5-000-years/ |access-date=22 May 2023 |work=Scientific American |department=Archeology}}</ref> As of 2024, the oldest known textual reference to romantic or sexual kissing is a [[clay tablet]] from the [[Sumer]]ian city of [[Nippur]] (in modern-day Iraq), dated 2,400 B.C., depicting the copulation of [[Sumerian religion|deities]]—[[Enlil]], king of the gods, and the mother goddess [[Ninhursag]].<ref name=":17">{{Cite news |last=Lidz |first=Franz |date=February 14, 2024 |title=Who Kissed First? Archaeology Has an Answer. |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/02/13/science/archaeology-sumeria-kissing.html |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250214131252/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/02/13/science/archaeology-sumeria-kissing.html |archive-date=February 14, 2025 |access-date=November 21, 2025 |work=The New York Times}}</ref> Another early description of kissing in the mating context comes from India in 1,500 B.C. when the oral tradition of the [[Vedic Sanskrit]] was being transcribed.<ref name=":17" /> By the end of the fourth century B.C., kissing had spread across the Mediterranean, following the return of [[Ancient Macedonian army|Greek and Macedonian soldiers]] from the campaign of [[Alexander the Great|Alexander III of Macedon]] in [[Indian campaign of Alexander the Great|Northern India]].<ref name=":17" /> In the [[Roman Republic]], different types of kissing were recognized, one of which was open-mouth kissing with tongue-to-tongue contact, now known as [[French kiss]]ing.<ref name=":17" /> However, the depiction of romantic or sexual kissing in some known prehistoric sculptures indicates that this kind of kissing may in fact predate writing itself.<ref name=":19" />

Kissing on the mouth is often a prelude to copulation ([[foreplay]]) or itself a sexual activity.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Paramon's Editorial Team |title=Atlas of the Human Body |publisher=Barron's Educational Series |year=2008 |isbn=978-0-7641-6091-2 |location=New York |language=Spanish |translator-last=Geiger |translator-first=Gerald L.}}</ref>{{Reference page|page=169}} In the aforementioned Sumerian clay tablet, however, kissing is done post-coitus.<ref name=":17" /> Various cultures have different standards of social acceptability towards romantic kissing. In ancient Mesopotamia, romantic kissing might be something lovers yearn for,<ref name=":18" /> but could not do outside of marriage, because doing so was deemed a crime as serious as adultery.<ref name=":17" /> In the [[Roman Empire]], two lovers kissing in public was considered indecent. This was partly because of a health risk posed by kissing, which was known to spread [[cold sore]]s, caused by the [[herpes simplex virus]].<ref name=":17" /> In medieval Europe, kissing was done on both the cheeks and the mouth, with a certain level of ambiguity as to whether it was a simple greeting or an expression of romantic interest. But kissing on the mouth as a greeting gesture disappeared following the [[Black Death]].<ref name=":20">{{Cite news |last=Hansen |first=Liane |date=February 11, 2007 |title=The (Mostly) Blissful History of 'Kissing' |url=https://www.npr.org/2007/02/11/7348582/the-mostly-blissful-history-of-kissing?t=1651502324027 |access-date=November 23, 2025 |work=National Public Radio}}</ref> Indeed, in many cultures today, such as Sudan, kissing on the mouth is thought to be rude or unsanitary.<ref name=":20" /> It is hypothesized that despite the risk of pathogen transmission, romantic and sexual kissing evolved from parental care-giving ([[Premastication|premasticating]] and oral feeding) to something that could help strengthen social ties and facilitate pair-bonding.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Andrei |first=Mihai |date=November 19, 2025 |title=Scientists Say Kissing Is Over 20 Million Years Old and It's Not Just a Human Thing |url=https://www.zmescience.com/science/news-science/kissing-is-old-humans-and-animals/ |access-date=November 25, 2025 |work=ZME Science}}</ref>

==== Matchmaking ====
{{see also|Marry-your-rapist law}}
[[File:Egyptian harvest.jpg|thumb|Since the [[Neolithic Revolution|dawn of agriculture]], marital norms have been centered on the accumulation and preservation of the [[Generational wealth|family fortune]].<ref name=":27" /> ]]
Historically, one of the roles of the family was to select (potential) spouses of the opposite sex but from the same race or ethnicity and religion for the children.<ref name="Rosenfeld-2012">{{Cite journal |last1=Rosenfeld |first1=Michael J. |last2=Thomas |first2=Reuben J. |date=June 13, 2012 |title=Searching for a Mate: The Rise of the Internet as a Social Intermediary |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0003122412448050 |journal=American Sociological Review |volume=77 |issue=4 |doi=10.1177/0003122412448050|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="Ferdman-2016b">{{Cite news |last=Ferdman |first=Roberto A. |date=March 8, 2016 |title=There are only three ways to meet anyone anymore |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2016/03/08/how-much-life-has-changed-in-one-incredible-chart-about-dating/ |archive-url=https://archive.today/20160414133418/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2016/03/08/how-much-life-has-changed-in-one-incredible-chart-about-dating/ |archive-date=14 April 2016 |access-date=October 12, 2024 |newspaper=The Washington Post}}</ref> It was also common for families to [[Arranged marriage|arrange a marriage]] as a transaction for the sake of political or economic expediency.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Baddeley |first=Anna |date=July 21, 2021 |title=I don't: my very modern marriage |url=https://www.economist.com/1843/2021/07/21/i-dont-my-very-modern-marriage |archive-url=https://archive.today/20211005074108/https://www.economist.com/1843/2021/07/21/i-dont-my-very-modern-marriage |archive-date=October 5, 2021 |access-date=November 6, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref> In many cultural traditions, a date may be arranged by a third party, who may be a family member, acquaintance, or (professional) [[Matchmaking|matchmaker]].<ref name="Batabyal-2018" />

In China, for instance, single women in their late 20s and up—pejoratively called "leftover women" ([[Sheng nu]])—tend to use matchmaking services. Women who prefer not to ask potentially embarrassing questions—such as whether both spouses will handle household finances, whether or not they will live with his parents, or how many children he wants to have, if any—will get a matchmaker to do it for them. Both sexes prefer matchmakers who are women.<ref name="Whitehead-20173">{{Cite news |last=Whitehead |first=Kate |date=September 24, 2017 |title=Chinese online dating giant Zhenai.com shows money still driver in politically incorrect matchmaking world |url=https://www.scmp.com/news/world/article/1426600/researchers-claim-have-method-improves-online-dating-odds |access-date=December 19, 2019 |work=South China Morning Post |department=Lifestyle}}</ref> Such a matchmaker could be also a religious leader in a community where religious attendance is common.<ref name="Ferdman-2016b" /> In some cultures, such as India, a marriage may be arranged by the couple's parents or an outside party.<ref name="Batabyal-2018" /> Yet even here, couples in the early twenty-first century are increasingly making their own decisions about marriage.<ref name=":8" /> According to a 2014 report from the [[UNICEF|United Nations Children's Fund]], a quarter of a billion females were [[Forced marriage|forced]] into [[Child marriage|marriage before the age of 15]], especially in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa.<ref name="Lamothe-2018">{{Cite web |last=Lamothe |first=Cindy |date=June 12, 2018 |title=The health risks of maturing early |url=https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20180611-the-health-risks-of-girls-maturing-early |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210125030648/https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20180611-the-health-risks-of-girls-maturing-early |archive-date=January 25, 2021 |access-date=January 9, 2021 |website=BBC Future}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=United Nations Children's Fund |year=2014 |title=Ending Child Marriage: Progress and Prospects |url=https://www.unicef.org/media/files/Child_Marriage_Report_7_17_LR..pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190727024931/https://www.unicef.org/media/files/Child_Marriage_Report_7_17_LR..pdf |archive-date=July 27, 2019 |access-date=January 9, 2021 |website=UNICEF}}</ref> In these cultures, the onset of [[puberty]] remains a marker of readiness for marriage. According to their traditional viewpoints, marrying off a young teenage girl is a way to prevent her from having premarital sexual intercourse and to "protect" her from sexual assault.<ref name="Lamothe-2018" />

From the 2000s onward, [[internet dating]]—a new form of matchmaking—has become increasingly popular.<ref name="Rosenfeld-2012" /><ref name="Ferdman-2016b" /><ref name="Batabyal-2018">{{Cite news|last=Batabyal|first=Amitrajeet A.|date=4 April 2018|title=What meeting your spouse online has in common with arranged marriage|work=The Conversation|url=https://theconversation.com/what-meeting-your-spouse-online-has-in-common-with-arranged-marriage-93839|access-date=16 June 2021}}</ref> This service feeds data shared by users into [[Machine learning|machine-learning]] algorithms to find the [[Optimal stopping|optimal match]].<ref name=":1" />

=== Sex differences ===

==== Short-term and long-term mating ====
Evolutionarily speaking, due to differential parental investment, the less investing sex displays more intrasexual competitiveness.<ref name="Trivers-1972" /> This is because they can invest less in each offspring and therefore can reproduce at a higher frequency, which allows them to compete for more mates. Additionally, the higher investing sex is more choosy in their mate.<ref name="Trivers-1972" /> Since they have a higher minimum parental investment, they carry greater costs with each sexual encounter. These costs lead them to have higher selection standards and therefore they are more choosy.

In humans, females have the higher obligatory biological parental investment. In short-term mating, females are choosier as they have the bigger parental investment. In long-term mating, males and females are equally choosy as they have the same amount of parental investment. Therefore, female and male intrasexual competition and female and male choosiness is equally high in long-term mating but not in short-term mating.
[[File:Tiziano - Amor Sacro y Amor Profano (Galería Borghese, Roma, 1514).jpg|thumb|''Sacred and Profane Love'' (1514) by [[Titian]]. Men pursue different types of women depending on whether they desire a long-term or short-term mate.]]
Since males have the lower obligatory parental investment, they should pursue a short-term mating strategy more often than females.<ref name="Buss-1993" /> Short term mating is characterized by casual, low commitment sexual relationships with many partners that do not last a long time. Additionally, males benefit more from short-term mating than females do.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Buss|first=David M.|date=1998|title=Sexual strategies theory: Historical origins and current status|journal=Journal of Sex Research|volume=35|issue=1|pages=19–31|doi=10.1080/00224499809551914|issn=0022-4499}}</ref> Because males generally pursue short-term mating strategies, their overall reproductive success is higher than that of females, however it is also more variable. This means that males are able to have more offspring on average, however only relatively few males are able to have a very large number of offspring. Due to this short-mating strategy, males have a greater desire for sexual variety, need less time to consent to intercourse, and seek short-term mates more than females.<ref name="Buss-1993" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Lippa|first=R. A.|date=2009|title=Sex Differences in Sex Drive, Sociosexuality, and Height across 53 Nations: Testing Evolutionary and Social Structural Theories|journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior|volume=38|issue=5|pages=631–651|doi=10.1007/s10508-007-9242-8|pmid=17975724|s2cid=15349303|issn=0004-0002}}</ref>

However, females also pursue short-term mates, but the motivations differ from males. Females can benefit from short-term mating in numerous ways.<ref name="Schmitt-2014">{{Citation|last=Schmitt|first=David P.|chapter=Evaluating Evidence of Mate Preference Adaptations: How do We Really Know What Homo sapiens sapiens Really Want? |title=Evolutionary Perspectives on Human Sexual Psychology and Behavior|date=2014|series=Evolutionary Psychology|pages=3–39|publisher=Springer: New York|doi=10.1007/978-1-4939-0314-6_1|isbn=978-1-4939-0313-9}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Lancaster|first=J.B.|title=Human sexuality, life histories, and evolutionary ecology|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=1994|editor-last=Rossi|editor-first=A. S.|location=Chicago, IL}}</ref> First, it allows for a quick extraction of resources. Women in a stressed situation may benefit from protection from a male and short term mating is a way to achieve this as is seen in contemporary asylum seeker anthropological studies.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://deeply.thenewhumanitarian.org/refugees/community/2017/10/31/why-so-many-migrant-mothers-arrive-in-europe-pregnant|title=Why So Many Migrant Mothers Arrive in Europe Pregnant|website=Refugees|date=31 October 2017 }}</ref>

One prominent hypothesis is that ancestral women selectively engaged in short-term mating with men capable of transmitting genetic benefits to their offspring such as health, disease resistance, or attractiveness (see [[Sexy son hypothesis#Good genes theory|good genes theory]] and [[sexy son hypothesis]]). Since women cannot inspect men's genes directly, they may have evolved to infer genetic quality from certain observable characteristics (see [[Mate choice#Indicator traits|indicator traits]]). One prominent candidate for a "good genes" indicator includes absence of [[fluctuating asymmetry]], or the degree to which men have perfect bodily symmetry. Other candidates include masculine facial features,<ref>Gangestad, S.W., & Thornhill, R. (2003). "Facial masculinity and fluctuating asymmetry". ''Evolution and Human Behavior'', 24(4), 231–241.</ref> behavioral dominance,<ref>Simpson, J.A., Gangestad, S.W., Christensen, P.N., & Leck, K. (1999). [http://www.yourpersonality.net/psych437/fall2011/fa.pdf "Fluctuating asymmetry, sociosexuality, and intrasexual competitive tactics"]. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 76(1), 159.</ref> and low vocal pitch.<ref>Puts, D.A., Gaulin, S.J., & Verdolini, K. (2006). [https://web.archive.org/web/20171211053315/https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/b511/063a8576c3fdbffe0bc3bc5d8084bd773495.pdf "Dominance and the evolution of sexual dimorphism in human voice pitch"]. ''Evolution and Human Behavior'', 27(4), 283–296.</ref> Evolutionary psychologists have therefore indicated that women pursuing a short-term mating strategy have higher preferences for these good gene indicators, and men who possess good genes indicators are more successful in pursuing short-term mating strategies than men who do not. Indeed, research indicates that self-perceived physical attractiveness,<ref>Clark, A.P. (2006). [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0191886906002078#:~:text=They%20found%20that%20although%20men,between%20individuals%20within%20both%20sexes. "Are the correlates of sociosexuality different for men and women?"]. ''Personality and Individual Differences'', 41(7), 1321–1327.</ref> absence of fluctuating asymmetry,<ref>Thornhill, R., & Gangestad, S.W. (1994). "Human fluctuating asymmetry and sexual behavior". ''Psychological Science'', 5(5), 297–302.</ref> and low vocal pitch<ref>{{cite journal | pmc=2391230 | year=2007 | last1=Apicella | first1=C. L. | last2=Feinberg | first2=D. R. | last3=Marlowe | first3=F. W. | title=Voice pitch predicts reproductive success in male hunter-gatherers | journal=Biology Letters | volume=3 | issue=6 | pages=682–684 | doi=10.1098/rsbl.2007.0410 | pmid=17895219 | bibcode=2007BiLet...3..682A }}</ref> are positively related to short-term mating success in men but not in women.

Conversely, long-term mating is marked by serious committed sexual relationships with relatively few partners. While males generally pursue a short-term mating strategy when possible, females typically pursue a long-term mating strategy.<ref name="Buss-1993" /> Long-term strategies are characterized by extended courtships, high investment, and few sexual partners. While pursuing a long-term strategy, females are able to get resources from males over the course of the relationship. Female mating psychology is generally more focused on finding high quality mates rather than increasing the quantity of their mates, which is reflected in their pursuit of a long-term strategy.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Thornhill|first1=R.|title=The evolutionary biology of human female sexuality|last2=Gangestad|first2=S.W.|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2008|location=New York, NY}}</ref> Additionally, they also benefit from higher parental investment by males. Women are thought to seek long-term partners with resources (such as shelter and food) that provide aid and support survival of offspring.<ref>{{Cite journal|author1=Rodríguez-Gironés, M.A.|author2=Enquist, M.|date=2001|title=The evolution of female sexuality|journal=Animal Behaviour|volume=61|issue=4|pages=695–704|doi=10.1006/anbe.2000.1630|bibcode=2001AnBeh..61..695R |s2cid=16503736}}</ref> To achieve this, women are thought to have evolved [[Extended female sexuality|extended sexuality]]. The key benefit for males pursuing a long-term strategy is higher parental certainty.<ref name="Buss-1993" /> However, both sexes pursue both strategies and get benefits from both strategies.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Schmitt|first=David P.|date=2005a|title=Is Short-Term Mating the Maladaptive Result of Insecure Attachment? A Test of Competing Evolutionary Perspectives|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|volume=31|issue=6|pages=747–768|doi=10.1177/0146167204271843|pmid=15833903|s2cid=7769880|issn=0146-1672}}</ref> Additionally, humans typically do not pursue the extremes of either short or long-term mating strategies.

It is possible that females are more prone to [[Depression (mood)|psychological depression]] than males if they are subject to [[R/K selection theory|K-selection]].<ref name="Suarez1991">{{cite journal|author=Suarez, S.D.|author2=Gallup Jr, G.G.|year=1991|title=Depression as a response to reproductive failure|journal=Journal of Social and Biological Structures|volume=8|issue=3|pages=279–287|doi=10.1016/0140-1750(85)90071-5}}</ref> Because women's reproductive decisions are made with more risks then men's, [[postpartum depression]] could be a signal to women that they faced a bad investment opportunity, would be evolutionarily adaptive.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kaplan|first=Hillard|name-list-style=vanc|date=1996|title=A theory of fertility and parental investment in traditional and modern human societies|journal=American Journal of Physical Anthropology|volume=101|issue=S23 |pages=91–135|doi=10.1002/(sici)1096-8644(1996)23+<91::aid-ajpa4>3.0.co;2-c}}</ref> By the same token, some researchers hypothesized that postpartum depression is more likely to occur in mothers who are suffering a [[Fitness (biology)|fitness]] cost, in order to inform them that they should reduce or [[Infanticide|withdraw investment in their infants]].<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Hagen|first1=Edward H|name-list-style=vanc|date=September 1999|title=The Functions of Postpartum Depression|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=20|issue=5|pages=325–359|citeseerx=10.1.1.335.7173|doi=10.1016/S1090-5138(99)00016-1 |bibcode=1999EHumB..20..325H }}</ref><ref name="Lehrman 1998 p. ">{{cite book | last=Lehrman | first=Daniel S. | title=Advances in the Study of Behavior | publisher=Academic Press | publication-place=San Diego, California | date=1998 | isbn=978-0-12-004527-3 | page=}}</ref> Moreover, there is some evidence that postpartum depression could function as a [[bargaining]] strategy, in which parents who were not receiving adequate support from their partners withdrew their investment in order to elicit additional support. In support of this, Hagen found that postpartum depression in one spouse was related to increased levels of child investment in the other spouse.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Hagen|first=Edward H|name-list-style=vanc|date=1 September 2002|title=Depression as bargaining: The case postpartum|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=23|issue=5|pages=323–336|doi=10.1016/S1090-5138(01)00102-7|bibcode=2002EHumB..23..323H |issn=1090-5138}}</ref>

==== Determination of mate value ====
[[File:Edmund Blair Leighton - The Wedding Register.jpg|left|thumb|''The Wedding Register'' (1920) by [[Edmund Leighton|Edmund Blair Leighton]]. Male mate value depends on his access to resources while female mate value lies in her youth and fertility.<ref name="Buss-1989" />]]
An individual's [[Mate value in humans|mate value]] corresponds to the likelihood of his or her future reproductive success,<ref name="Symons-1979">{{Cite book|last=Symons |first=Donald|title=The evolution of human sexuality|date=1979|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=0-19-502535-0|oclc=4494283}}</ref> determined by ability of said individual to produce healthy offspring in the future, based on his or her age and sex.<ref name="Symons-1979" /> Mate value is determined by [[sexual selection]], that is, what each sex considers to be desirable in a mate.<ref name="Buss-1993" /> Over evolutionary time, the individuals with higher mate values generally have greater reproductive success. These qualities that comprise mate value evolved into what is considered physically attractive,<ref name="Sugiyama-2015">{{Cite book|last=Sugiyama|first=Lawrence|chapter=Physical Attractiveness: An Adaptationist Perspective|date=2015|title=The Handbook of Evolutionary Psychology|volume=2|pages=1–68|editor-last=Buss|editor-first=David|publisher=John Wiley & Sons, Inc.|doi=10.1002/9781119125563.evpsych112|isbn=978-1-119-12556-3}}</ref> meaning individuals with a high mate value are perceived to be more attractive by the opposite sex than those with low mate value.<ref name="Sugiyama-2015" /> Furthermore, individuals with a high mate value can afford to be stricter in mate selection and may reproduce more often than those with a low mate value. Biological sex differences suggest that there are differences between the sexes in mate value as well.<ref name="Buss-1993" />

Mate value is perceived through signals and cues. Signals are characteristics that have been selected for because they offer reliable changes in receiver behavior that lead to higher reproductive success for the receiver.<ref name="Smith-2003">{{Cite book|last1=Smith|first1=J. M.|title=Animal Signals|last2=David|first2=H.|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2003|location=Oxford, England}}</ref> Conversely, cues have not been selected for to carry meaning, but instead are byproducts.<ref name="Smith-2003" /> But with sexual selection, cues can become signals over time.<ref name="Sugiyama-2015" /> Costly [[Signalling theory|signals]] are ones that require intense effort for the signaler to send. Because they require high investment, costly signals are typically honest signals of underlying genetic qualities.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Zahavi|first1=A.|title=The handicap principle: A missing piece of Darwin's puzzle|last2=Zahavi|first2=A.|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=1997|location=Oxford, England}}</ref> One the other hand, signals that are not costly enough can be faked and therefore are not associated with the underlying traits.

Philosopher [[Jean de La Bruyère]] argued that it was better to be "a beautiful girl from the age of thirteen to the age of twenty-two and then after that to be a man."<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=9}} Indeed, evolutionary psychologists have evidence that men generally place a greater value on youth and [[physical attractiveness]] in a mate than do women. Youth is associated with greater [[Reproductive value (population genetics)|reproductive value]] in women, because their ability to have offspring decreases dramatically over time compared to men. Therefore, males typically prefer to mate with females who are younger than themselves, except when they are maturing in their teens.<ref name="Kenrick-1992">{{Cite journal|last1=Kenrick|first1=Douglas|last2=Keefe|first2=Richard|date=1992|title=Age Preferences in Mates Reflect Sex Differences in Human Reproductive Strategies|url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/behavioral-and-brain-sciences/article/age-preferences-in-mates-reflect-sex-differences-in-human-reproductive-strategies/BE5CDF43F525E472B4B0C00DBE5EC74F|journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences|volume=15|pages=75–133|doi=10.1017/S0140525X00067595|s2cid=145276497 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> The features that men find physically attractive in women are thought to signal health and [[fertility]],<ref name="Buss-1993" /><ref name="Salam-2018">{{Cite news |last=Salam |first=Maya |date=August 15, 2018 |title=For Online Daters, Women Peak at 18 While Men Peak at 50, Study Finds. Oy. |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/08/15/style/dating-apps-online-men-women-age.html |access-date=November 30, 2019 |work=The New York Times}}</ref> as well as high estrogen and low testosterone.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=192}} Examples of the determinants of female attractiveness include [[wikt:curvaceousness|curvaceousness]],<ref name="Fisher2006">{{cite journal |last1=Fisher |first1=M. L. |last2=Voracek |first2=M. |date=June 2006 |title=The shape of beauty: Determinants of female physical attractiveness |journal=Journal of Cosmetic Dermatology |volume=5 |issue=2 |pages=190–194 |doi=10.1111/j.1473-2165.2006.00249.x |pmid=17173598 |s2cid=25660426}}</ref> the fact that her breasts do not yet sag,<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=58-9}} and having a low [[Waist–hip ratio|waist-to-hip ratio]] (WHR).<ref>{{cite journal |last=Singh |first=Devendra |date=August 1993 |title=Adaptive significance of female physical attractiveness: role of waist-to-hip ratio |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=65 |issue=2 |pages=293–307 |citeseerx=10.1.1.492.9539 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.65.2.293 |pmid=8366421}}</ref>  In general, the female [[body shape]] is largely due to her bodily fat distribution, spread across much of her body. On average, about a quarter of a woman's body consists of fat, twice as much as a man's. This body fat was a valuable asset for childcare in ancestral times when humans spread across the globe and had to confront novel environments as well as frequent famines.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=16-7}}
