In fact, woman's lower-body or [[gynoid fat distribution]], where the energy is stored for pregnancy for early infant care, including [[breastfeeding]], is a determinant of her WHR.<ref>Plowman, Sharon & Smith, Denise (2007). ''Exercise Physiology for Health, Fitness, and Performance'', p. 218. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins. {{ISBN|0781784069}}</ref> A mother's WHR before pregnancy is correlated with her child's [[Cognitive skill|cognitive ability]], as hip fat, which contains long chain [[Polyunsaturated fat|polyunsaturated fatty acids]], critical for the development of the fetus's brain.<ref name="Lassek">{{cite journal |last=Lassek|first=W.|author2=Gaulin S.|date=January 2008|title=Waist–hip ratio and cognitive ability: is gluteofemoral fat a privileged store of neurodevelopmental resources?|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=29|issue=1|pages=26–34|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2007.07.005|bibcode=2008EHumB..29...26L}}</ref> Another reason is the fact that women, not men, give birth, and they do so between their legs. As a result, women's [[Hip bone|pelvic girdles]]—evolved to be a compromise between the ease of childbearing and mobility—are wider on average than men's.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=17-8}} A woman's wider [[hip]]s are universally recognized as a sign of her sex, even if they did not originally evolve as a sexual signal.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=25}} A female human's WHR is at its optimal minimum during times of peak fertility—late adolescence and early adulthood—before increasing later in life.<ref>Thornhill, Randy (2008). ''The Evolutionary Biology of Human Female Sexuality'', p. 117. Oxford University Press, USA. {{ISBN|0199712484}}</ref> For men, women with the [[hourglass figure]], that is, low WHRs, are the most attractive.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=56-7}}

{{multiple image
| total_width       = 360
| image1            = Venus de Milo Louvre Ma399 n4.jpg
| alt1              = 
| caption1          = ''[[Venus de Milo]]'' at the [[Louvre]] has a waist-hip ratio of 0.76.<ref name="Bovet-and-Raymond"/><ref name="Heyman"/>
| image2            = 'David' by Michelangelo JBU05.JPG
| alt2              = 
| caption2          = [[Michelangelo]]'s ''[[David (Michelangelo)|David]]'' is considered a symbol of young male beauty and strength.
}}

But while the association between perceived beauty and the WHR is found across cultures,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Singh|first1=Devendra|last2=Young|first2=Robert K.|date=27 June 2001|title=Body Weight, Waist-to-Hip Ratio, Breasts, and Hips: Role in Judgments of Female Attractiveness and Desirability for Relationships|url=http://web.missouri.edu/~rouderj/3010/readings/Singh.pdf|journal=Ethology and Sociobiology|volume=16|issue=6|pages=483–507|doi=10.1016/0162-3095(95)00074-7|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081209135606/http://web.missouri.edu/~rouderj/3010/readings/Singh.pdf|archive-date=9 December 2008}}</ref> there are differences with regards to what the ideal WHR is, ranging from 0.6 in China, South America, and some of Africa<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dixson|first1=Barnaby J.|last2=Dixson|first2=Alan F.|last3=Li|first3=B|last4=Anderson|first4=M. J.|date=January 2007|title=Studies of human physique and sexual attractiveness: Sexual preferences of men and women in China|journal=American Journal of Human Biology|volume=19|issue=1|pages=88–95|doi=10.1002/ajhb.20584|pmid=17160976|s2cid=8868828}}</ref> to 0.8 in Cameroon and among the Hadza tribe of Tanzania.<ref name="MarloweApicellaReed2005">{{cite journal |last1=Marlowe|first1=F.|last2=Apicella|first2=C.|last3=Reed|first3=D.|date=2005|title=Men's preferences for women's profile waist-to-hip ratio in two societies|url=http://www.fas.harvard.edu/~hbe-lab/acrobatfiles/profilewhr.pdf|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=26|issue=6|pages=458–68|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2005.07.005|bibcode=2005EHumB..26..458M}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Marlowe|first1=F.|last2=Wetsman|first2=A.|date=2001|title=Preferred waist-to-hip ratio and ecology|url=http://www.fas.harvard.edu/%7Ehbe-lab/acrobatfiles/preferred%20waist.pdf|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=30|issue=3|pages=481–489|citeseerx=10.1.1.489.4169|doi=10.1016/S0191-8869(00)00039-8}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dixson|first1=Barnaby J.|last2=Dixson|first2=Alan F.|last3=Morgan|first3=B|last4=Anderson|first4=M. J.|date=June 2007|title=Human physique and sexual attractiveness: Sexual preferences of men and women in Bakossiland, Cameroon|journal=[[Archives of Sexual Behavior]]|volume=36|issue=3|pages=369–375|doi=10.1007/s10508-006-9093-8|pmid=17136587|s2cid=40115821}}</ref> In the United States, divergent preferences of African- and European-Americans have been observed.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Freedman |first1=R. E. |last2=Carter |first2=M. M. |last3=Sbrocco |first3=T. |last4=Gray |first4=J. J. |date=July 2004 |title=Ethnic differences in preferences for female weight and waist-to-hip ratio: A comparison of African-American and White American college and community samples |url=https://figshare.com/articles/thesis/Ethnic_differences_in_preferences_for_female_weight_and_waist-to-hip_ratio_A_comparison_of_African_American_and_White_American_college_and_community_samples/23879001 |journal=Eating Behaviors |volume=5 |issue=3 |pages=191–198 |doi=10.1016/j.eatbeh.2004.01.002 |pmid=15135331}}</ref> However, this gap has narrowed in the early twenty-first century, suggesting a degree of flexibility.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Freedman |first1=R. E. |last2=Carter |first2=M. M. |last3=Sbrocco |first3=T. |last4=Gray |first4=J. J. |date=August 2007 |title=Do men hold African-American and Caucasian women to different standards of beauty? |journal=Eating Behaviors |volume=8 |issue=3 |pages=319–333 |doi=10.1016/j.eatbeh.2006.11.008 |pmc=3033406 |pmid=17606230}}</ref> Indeed, there is evidence of variation across time, even within a single culture or civilization. In the Western world, the ideal WHR, as seen in artworks, was approximately constant at around 0.74 in ancient times (500 B.C. to A.D. 400) but declined between the [[early modern period]] to the early twentieth century. (Women are rarely [[Depictions of nudity|depicted in the nude]] in medieval European art due to a ban by the Church.) From around 1960 to present, this decline has slowed or even reversed, judging from the WHRs of ''[[Playboy]]'' models and ''[[Miss America]]'' contestants. Nevertheless, they have never exceeded 0.85, which is what the World Health Organization currently recommends for women.<ref name="Bovet-and-Raymond">{{Cite journal|title=Preferred Women's Waist-to-Hip Ratio Variation over the Last 2,500 Years|last1=Bovet|first1=Jeanne|date=April 17, 2015|journal=PLOS ONE|last2=Raymond|first2=Michel|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0123284|issue=4|volume=10|article-number=e0123284 |pmid=25886537 |pmc=4401783 |bibcode=2015PLoSO..1023284B |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Heyman">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/05/28/arts/international/gleaning-new-perspectives-by-measuring-body-proportions-in-art.html|title=Gleaning New Perspectives by Measuring Body Proportions in Art|last=Heyman|first=Stephen|date=May 27, 2015|archive-url=https://archive.today/20230325150546/https://www.nytimes.com/2015/05/28/arts/international/gleaning-new-perspectives-by-measuring-body-proportions-in-art.html|archive-date=March 25, 2023|access-date=December 1, 2025|work=The New York Times}}</ref> Medical research indicates that the WHR is a more accurate measure of bodily fat and predictor of mortality than the [[body mass index]].<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Surrogate Adiposity Markers and Mortality|last1=Khan|first1=Irfan|journal=JAMA Network Open|issue=9|volume=6|pages=e2334836|last2=Chong|first2=Michael|display-authors=1|date=2023 |doi=10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.34836 |pmid=37728925 |pmc=10512100}}</ref> In general, however, perceived physical beauty is a matter of biology, rather than simple proportions or ratios of integers ([[rational number]]s).<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=18}}

Only one third of the tissues comprising the [[breast]]s of adult humans are involved in [[lactation]]; the rest, mostly [[Adipose tissue|fatty tissues]], help give the breasts their round shape. While other female mammals mostly have flat chests when they not breastfeeding their young, human females of reproductive age typically maintain their protruding breasts during their entire period of fertility.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=26}} Breasts are also noticeable among teenage girls, many of whom are not yet fertile.<ref name="Bainbridge-2009" />{{Reference page|page=52}} This suggests that human female breasts have also been a result of sexual selection, that is, they are sexual signals.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=26}} Nevertheless, it is not clear why men would find breasts attractive or sexy, nor is there an "optimal" breast size that men prefer.<ref name="Bainbridge-2009" />{{Reference page|page=|pages=53-5}}

Besides health and fertility, men also desire younger women because they tend to have less experience, lower expectations of prospective mates, and are more psychologically pliant.<ref name="Jagannathan-20182">{{Cite news |last=Jagannathan |first=Meera |date=August 17, 2018 |title=Women online daters peak at age 18. Men peak at 50. |url=https://www.marketwatch.com/story/women-online-daters-peak-at-age-18-men-peak-at-50-2018-08-17 |access-date=November 30, 2019 |work=MarketWatch}}</ref>
On the other hand, male mate value depends on social status and access to resources, which come with age,<ref name="Buss-1989">Buss, D.M. (1989). [http://philipperushton.net/wp-content/uploads/2015/02/iq-race-sex-gender-survey-mate-buss-nyborg-rushton-buss-behavioral-brain-sciences-19891.pdf "Sex differences in human mate preferences: Evolutionary hypotheses tested in 37 cultures"]. ''Behavioral and Brain Sciences'', 12(1), 1–49.</ref><ref name="Jagannathan-20182" /> because one of the costs of pregnancy is the limited ability to get resources for oneself.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kaplan|first1=Hillard|last2=Hill|first2=Kim|last3=Lancaster|first3=Jane|last4=Hurtado|first4=A. Magdalena|date=2000|title=A theory of human life history evolution: Diet, intelligence, and longevity|journal=Evolutionary Anthropology|volume=9|issue=4|pages=156–185|doi=10.1002/1520-6505(2000)9:4<156::aid-evan5>3.0.co;2-7|s2cid=2363289|issn=1060-1538}}</ref> Indeed, a man's mate value generally increases until later in middle age.<ref name="Salam-2018" /> A high-status and resource-rich male is more likely to be willing and able to invest in a female and her offspring,<ref name="Buss-1989" /> enhancing the likelihood that the offspring will survive until reproductive age.<ref name="Kenrick-1992" /> Evolutionary psychologists have speculated that women tend to prefer men of ambition and social status because they associate these characteristics with men's access to resources.<ref name="Buss-1989" /><ref name="Shackelford-2005">Shackelford, T.K., Schmitt, D.P., & Buss, D.M. (2005). [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222557486_Universal_Dimensions_of_Human_Mate_Preferences#:~:text=We%20identified%20four%20universal%20dimensions,%2FChildren%3B%20and%20Sociability%20vs. "Universal dimensions of human mate preferences"]. ''Personality and Individual Differences'', 39(2), 447–458.</ref> Ancestral women who opted to mate with such men would have left more descendants than those who did not.<ref name="Buss-1993" /> Male mate value is also determined by his physical and social dominance, which are signals to high quality genes.<ref name="Buss-1993" /> Women typically feel attracted to men who are taller than they are and who display a high degree of facial symmetry, [[Masculinity|masculine]] facial [[Sexual dimorphism|dimorphism]], [[Physical strength|upper-body strength]], broad shoulders, a relatively narrow waist, and a V-shaped torso.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Sell A, Lukazsweski AW, Townsley M |date=December 2017 |title=Cues of upper body strength account for most of the variance in men's bodily attractiveness |journal=Proceedings. Biological Sciences |volume=284 |issue=1869 |article-number=20171819 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2017.1819 |pmc=5745404 |pmid=29237852}}</ref><ref name=":23">{{cite journal |vauthors=Mautz BS, Wong BB, Peters RA, Jennions MD |date=April 2013 |title=Penis size interacts with body shape and height to influence male attractiveness |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |volume=110 |issue=17 |pages=6925–30 |bibcode=2013PNAS..110.6925M |doi=10.1073/pnas.1219361110 |pmc=3637716 |pmid=23569234 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Indeed, on average, a man has greater muscle mass than a woman,<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=16}} and his broader shoulders evolved to accommodate greater upper-body strength.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=25}} A key sexual signal for men is their [[beard]]s, universally recognized as signs of masculinity. Boys do not have them before reaching puberty.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=26}}[[File:Cerne-abbas-giant-2008.jpg|left|thumb|Whatever its origins, the [[Cerne Abbas Giant]] in Southern England became associated with fertility during the Victorian age.<ref name="Koch396">{{Cite book|last=Koch|first=John T.|title=Celtic Culture: A Historical Encyclopedia|publisher=ABC-CLIO|year=2006|pages=|isbn=978-1-851-09440-0}}</ref>{{Reference page|pages=395–6}}]]Unlike in other animals, human [[Human genitals|genitals]] or [[Hypogastrium|pubic regions]] have not evolved to be (visual) sexual signals. Their sizes and shapes are merely what is necessary for copulation, and, in the case of women, childbearing.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=25}} However, in the case of the male penis, [[Human penis size|size]], when combined with other characteristics of body size and shape (such as the shoulder-to-hips ratio and height), is a determinant of male attractiveness.<ref name=":23" />

[[Body odour and sexual attraction|Body odor]] is another crucial criterion in assessing the suitability of a mate. In humans, some olfactory receptors are directly connected to the parts of the brain controlling reproductive behavior. Men are able to detect women's sexual arousal by the sense of smell, and a woman's smell may increase a man's level of arousal.<ref name="Fields-2022">{{Cite journal |last=Fields |first=R. Douglas |date=June 2022 |title="Covid Nose" Can Be A Deal Breaker |journal=Psychology Today |pages=20–21}}</ref> Among women not taking the hormone contraceptive pill, their olfactory and visual senses convergently guide them towards men exhibiting the least amount of [[fluctuating asymmetry]] during the most fertile periods of their menstrual cycles.<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|page=79}} However, the existence of [[Human sex pheromones|pheromones in humans]] has never been reliably demonstrated.<ref name="Wysocki 2004">{{cite journal |last1=Wysocki |first1=Charles J. |last2=Preti |first2=George |date=7 October 2004 |title=Facts, fallacies, fears, and frustrations with human pheromones |journal=The Anatomical Record |volume=281A |issue=1 |pages=1201–1211 |doi=10.1002/ar.a.20125 |pmid=15470677 |quote=It is emphasized that no bioassay-guided study has led to the isolation of true human pheromones, a step that will elucidate specific functions to human chemical signals. |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Yuhas |first=Daisy |date=May 2014 |title=Are Human Pheromones Real? |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/are-human-pheromones-real/ |journal=Scientific American}}</ref>

Whereas in the past, women usually had shorter lives because many of them died during childbirth, this is no longer the case thanks to modern medicine, which has made the sexes more equal by causing [[Maternal death|maternal mortality]] rates to plummet.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=164}} Evolutionary theory predicts that males, as the sex having to take risks in order to acquire resources and status to impress potential mates, tend to have a shorter [[life expectancy]]. This is in fact what happens.<ref name="Bainbridge-2009">{{Cite book |last=Bainbridge |first=David |author-link=David Bainbridge (scientist) |title=Teenagers: A Natural History |publisher=Greystone Books |year=2009 |isbn=978-1-55365-437-7}}</ref>{{Reference page|page=308}} From an evolutionary point of view, women are far more valuable as reproductive units than men. For this reason, although men tend to be physically stronger, women generally have better immune systems and are less likely to suffer from (acute) depression and to commit suicide.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=16}}

==== Relevance of the sex ratio ====
[[File:Hylas and the Water Nymphs.jpg|thumb|''Hylas and the Water Nymphs'' (1909) by [[Henrietta Rae]]. Human mating is dependent on the operational sex ratio.]]
The local operational sex ratio has been shown to have an impact on mating strategies.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Guttentag |first1=M. |title=Too Many Women? The sex ratio question |last2=Secord |first2=P. F. |publisher=Sage |year=1983 |location=Beverly Hills, CA}}</ref> This is defined as the ratio of marriage-age males to marriage-age females, with a high ratio representing more males and a low ratio representing more females in the local area. When there is an imbalance of sexes, the rare sex typically has more choice, while the plentiful sex has to compete more strategically for the rare sex. This leads to the plentiful sex competing on specific areas that the rare sex finds attractive.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Pedersen |first=F. A. |date=1991 |title=Secular Trends in Human Sex Ratios |journal=Human Nature |volume=2 |issue=3 |pages=271–291 |doi=10.1007/bf02692189 |issn=1045-6767 |pmid=24222281 |s2cid=824054}}</ref> Additionally, the plentiful sex will adopt more of the rare sex's mating strategy. In a population with a low sex ratio, females will adopt a more short-term mating strategy and will compete more intensely on things like physical attractiveness. On the other hand, in a society with a high sex ratio, males will adopt a more long-term strategy to attractive females. (See [[going steady]].) For example, in the major metropolitan areas of China, females are generally in short supply and as such are more likely to be fulfilled should they find a mate while many men are simply left out of the dating market. On the other hand, on the Island of Manhattan and in some Western university campuses, females are in excess and as such they compete intensely for male attention, giving rise to [[hookup culture]] and short-term mating websites such as [[Tinder (app)|Tinder]].<ref name="Buss-2016" />

In 2005, the evolutionary psychologist David Schmitt conducted a multinational survey of sexual attitudes and behaviors involving 48 countries called the International Sexual Description Project (ISSR).<ref name="sociosexualityaz">Schmitt, D.P. (2005). [http://psy.swan.ac.uk/staff/stewart-williams/publications/Schmitt_2005.pdf "Sociosexuality from Argentina to Zimbabwe: A 48-nation study of sex, culture, and strategies of human mating"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170809050800/http://psy.swan.ac.uk/staff/stewart-williams/publications/schmitt_2005.pdf|date=9 August 2017}}. ''Behavioral and Brain Sciences'', 28(2), 247–274.</ref> Schmitt assessed relationships between several societal-level variables and average scores on the SOI. One variable that was shown to significantly predict a nation's average SOI score was the Operational Sex Ratio (OSR). This prediction was confirmed; OSR was significantly positively correlated with national SOI scores.<ref name="sociosexualityaz" /> Another variable that Schmitt predicted would influence SOI scores was the need for biparental care. In societies where extensive care from both parents is needed to ensure offspring survival, the costs of having sex with an uncommitted partner are much higher. Schmitt found significant negative correlations between several indices of need for biparental care (e.g. infant mortality, child malnutrition, and low birth-weight infants) and national SOI scores.

==== Sexual desire ====
[[File:Lagedor W-A Bouguereau.JPG|thumb|''The Golden Age'' (1863) by [[William-Adolphe Bouguereau]]. Extended sexuality facilitates and reinforces pair-bonding. The woman shown here may also be taking advantage of [[lactational amenorrhea]].]]
Sexual selection theory states that because of their lower minimum parental investment, men can achieve greater [[reproductive success]] by mating with multiple women than women can achieve by mating with multiple men.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pellegrini |first1=Anthony D. |last2=Long |first2=Jeffrey D. |date=July 2003 |title=A sexual selection theory longitudinal analysis of sexual segregation and integration in early adolescence |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0022096503000602 |journal=Journal of Experimental Child Psychology |volume=85 |issue=3 |pages=257–278 |doi=10.1016/S0022-0965(03)00060-2|pmid=12810038 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Evolutionary psychologists therefore argue that ancestral men who possessed a desire for multiple short-term sex partners, to the extent that they were capable of attracting them, would have left more descendants than men without such a desire. Ancestral women, by contrast, would have maximized reproductive success not by mating with as many men as possible, but by selectively mating with those men who were most able and willing to invest resources in their offspring. Gradually in a bid to compete to get resources from potential men, women have evolved to show [[Extended female sexuality|extended sexuality]], that is, the ability and willingness to copulate despite being infertile. Like a number of other primates, humans do engage in non-procreative sexual intercourse in different ways and can do so multiple times per day without conceiving offspring. According to anthropologists and psychologists, the reasons why people have sex other than reproduction are to signal affection, to provide pain relief and comfort, to [[Consummation|authenticate a romantic relationship]], to derive [[Prostitution|economic]] or political benefits, to engage in athletic activity, to offer and receive pleasure, or to cure boredom. Unlike other species, it is even possible for a human couple to [[Sexual activity during pregnancy|copulate while the woman is pregnant]].<ref name="Bainbridge-2009" />{{Reference page|pages=255-6}} Indeed, (anonymized) Internet search data shows that people around the world are interested in or curious about having sexual intercourse during pregnancy.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|pages=188-90}}

Men as a group have a stronger desire for [[casual sex]] than women.<ref name="Clark1989">Clark, R.D., & Hatfield, E. (1989). [http://www2.hawaii.edu/~elaineh/79.pdf "Gender differences in receptivity to sexual offers"]. ''Journal of Psychology & Human Sexuality'', 2(1), 39–55.</ref> It makes evolutionary sense for men to want to copulate with as many female partners as possible in order to spread their genetic materials a widely as possible, thereby maximizing their reproductive success,<ref name="Flanagan">{{cite book|last1=Flanagan|first1=Cara|title=A2 student book for AQA A psychology|date=2012|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-912984-3|edition=3rd|location=Oxford}}</ref> regardless of whether or not they are interested in committing to a new family.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=91-2}} Indeed, across cultures, men exhibit a greater openness to non-committal and uncomplicated sex;<ref name="sociosexualityaz" /> a stronger desire for numerous sexual partners,<ref name="universal">Schmitt, D.P. (2003). [http://repositorio.ulima.edu.pe/xmlui/bitstream/handle/ulima/2086/Echegaray_Marcela.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y "Universal sex differences in the desire for sexual variety: tests from 52 nations, 6 continents, and 13 islands"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171211053401/http://repositorio.ulima.edu.pe/xmlui/bitstream/handle/ulima/2086/Echegaray_Marcela.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y|date=11 December 2017}}. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 85(1), 85.</ref> and an interest in earlier [[fornication]] in a relationship.<ref name="universal" /> These sex differences have been shown to be reliable across various studies and methodologies.<ref>Baumeister, R.F., Catanese, K.R., & Vohs, K.D. (2001). [https://carlsonschool.umn.edu/sites/carlsonschool.umn.edu/files/faculty/publications/71520.pdf "Is there a gender difference in strength of sex drive? Theoretical views, conceptual distinctions, and a review of relevant evidence"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170912115725/https://carlsonschool.umn.edu/sites/carlsonschool.umn.edu/files/faculty/publications/71520.pdf|date=12 September 2017}}. ''Personality and Social Psychology Review'', 5(3), 242–273.</ref><ref>Oliver, M.B., & Hyde, J.S. (1993). [http://www.secretintelligenceservice.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/02/d413531b566ab3b9eed0fd4ced1e80ef3663.pdf "Gender differences in sexuality: a meta-analysis"]. ''Psychological Bulletin'', 114(1), 29.</ref> Evolutionary mechanisms for short-term mating can be observed today. Mate-guarding behaviors and sexual jealousy point to an evolutionary history in which sexual relations with multiple partners was frequent.<ref>Buss, D.M., & Shackelford, T.K. (1997). [https://labs.la.utexas.edu/buss/files/2015/09/Buss-Shackelford-JPSP-1997.pdf "From vigilance to violence: Mate retention tactics in married couples"]. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 72, 346–361.</ref> Meanwhile, the general willingness of modern-day men to have sex with attractive strangers,<ref name="Clark1989" /> and the prevalence of extramarital affairs in similar frequencies across different cultures, are evidence of an ancestral past in which [[polygamy|polygamous]] mating strategies were adopted.<ref>Buss, D.M. (1994). "Individual differences in mating strategies". ''Behavioral and Brain Sciences'', 17, 581–582.</ref>

Despite having to bear the costs of pregnancy, childbirth, and nurturing offspring, and to face the danger of single parenthood, females do not always resist the sexual advances of males.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=91}} Except in cases of coercion, males could not pursue polygamy without willing female partners. Every time a man has a new sexual partner, so does a woman. It is plausible, therefore, that casual sex and numerous sexual partners may also confer some benefit to females. That is, they would produce more genetically diverse offspring as a result, which would increase their chances of successfully rearing children to adolescence, or independence.<ref name=Flanagan /> Provided that her adultery is not caught, an ancestral woman who mates with men other than other main partner could benefit from having a protector and a provider, as well as a younger and healthier source of genetic materials for her offspring. But if her partner were a man of high status and good health, she would likely remain faithful.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=92}} Compared with other primates, human males do not have very large testicles, suggesting only a moderate level sperm competition during the course of human evolution. In other words, women tend to be fairly, but not entirely, faithful, which is what is observed in human societies.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=24-5}}

[[Error management theory]] states that psychological processes should be biased to minimize costs of making incorrect judgments and decisions. Since males lean towards short-term mating, the costs of not having sexual intercourse is higher than doing so. Conversely, since females generally pursue a long-term strategy, the costs of having sexual intercourse is higher than not. Due to these different costs and benefits, males have the proclivities for overestimating female interest or desire for sex whereas females tend to underrate male commitment. However, males accurately perceive female commitment and females accurately perceive male sexual interests.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Haselton|first1=M. G.|last2=Buss|first2=D. M.|date=2000|title=Error Management Theory: A New Perspective on Biases in Cross-Sex Mind Reading|url=https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/3daf/64a4d0a66e19f77b3704cef7fd18d796c719.pdf|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=78|issue=1|pages=81–91|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.78.1.81|pmid=10653507|bibcode=2000JPSP...78...81H |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028031312/https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/3daf/64a4d0a66e19f77b3704cef7fd18d796c719.pdf|archive-date=28 October 2020|s2cid=328380}}</ref>

By comparing the actual numbers of condoms sold each year, the percentage of women of child-bearing age using contraception, and the number of new pregnancies in the United States, data scientist Seth Stephens-Davidowitz came to the conclusion that self-reports tend to overestimate how often people were engaging in sexual intercourse.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|page=122}} He also found that according to Google search data, it may be more frequent for boyfriends or husbands to withhold sex from their girlfriends or wives than commonly realized.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|page=123}} Indeed, one of the top complaints about marriages or romantic relationships is the lack or low frequency of sex.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|pages=5-6}}

==== Mate retention ====
{{See also|Theory of attachment}}
[[File:Etty Mars abandoning Venus.jpg|thumb|''Mars abandoning Venus'' (circa 1820) by [[William Etty]]. Mate retention is a recurring adaptive problem.]]
After attracting and acquiring mates, humans need to retain their mates over a certain period of time, especially in long-term monogamous relationships.<ref name="Campbell-2015">{{Citation|last1=Campbell|first1=Lorne|title=Love and Commitment in Romantic Relationships|date=18 November 2015|work=The Handbook of Evolutionary Psychology|pages=1–17|editor-last=Buss|editor-first=David|publisher=John Wiley & Sons, Inc.|isbn=978-1-119-12556-3|last2=Loving|first2=Timothy J.|doi=10.1002/9781119125563.evpsych118}}</ref> Feelings of love motivate individuals to stay with their current partners and forego from alternatives.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Gonzaga|first1=Gian C.|last2=Haselton|first2=Martie G.|last3=Smurda|first3=Julie|last4=Davies|first4=Mari sian|last5=Poore|first5=Joshua C.|date=2008|title=Love, desire, and the suppression of thoughts of romantic alternatives☆|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=29|issue=2|pages=119–126|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2007.11.003|bibcode=2008EHumB..29..119G |issn=1090-5138}}</ref> Proclamations of love and commitment increase the level of [[oxytocin]], a hormone associated with social bonding and love, in the bloodstream.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Gonzaga|first1=Gian C.|last2=Turner|first2=Rebecca A.|last3=Keltner|first3=Dacher|last4=Campos|first4=Belinda|last5=Altemus|first5=Margaret|date=2006|title=Romantic love and sexual desire in close relationships.|journal=Emotion|volume=6|issue=2|pages=163–179|doi=10.1037/1528-3542.6.2.163|pmid=16768550|issn=1931-1516}}</ref>

Nevertheless, remaining a bonded pair can be difficult, especially in the presence of alternative mates. In the presence of desirable alternatives, humans tend to view their current relationships less favorably.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kenrick|first1=Douglas T.|last2=Neuberg|first2=Steven L.|last3=Zierk|first3=Kristin L.|last4=Krones|first4=Jacquelyn M.|date=1994|title=Evolution and Social Cognition: Contrast Effects as a Function of Sex, Dominance, and Physical Attractiveness|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|volume=20|issue=2|pages=210–217|doi=10.1177/0146167294202008|s2cid=146625806|issn=0146-1672}}</ref> Yet there are psychological counter-measures to these processes. First, individuals in a committed relationship tend to devalue alternative mate options, thus finding them less attractive.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Johnson|first1=Dennis J.|last2=Rusbult|first2=Caryl E.|date=1989|title=Resisting temptation: Devaluation of alternative partners as a means of maintaining commitment in close relationships.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=57|issue=6|pages=967–980|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.57.6.967|s2cid=197655762 |issn=0022-3514}}</ref> Second, humans do not always consider potential alternatives. Instead they pay less attention to alternative mates and therefore do not undergo the devaluation process.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Miller|first=Rowland S.|date=1997|title=Inattentive and contented: Relationship commitment and attention to alternatives.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=73|issue=4|pages=758–766|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.73.4.758|issn=1939-1315}}</ref> These mechanisms tend to happen unconsciously and help them maintain their current relationships.

There are several strategies that an individual can do to retain their mate, especially if their mate is of high value.<ref name="Campbell-2015" /> Males undertake resource displays and females enhance their physical appearance.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Buss|first=David M.|date=1988|title=From vigilance to violence|journal=Ethology and Sociobiology|volume=9|issue=5|pages=291–317|doi=10.1016/0162-3095(88)90010-6|hdl=2027.42/27156|issn=0162-3095|hdl-access=free}}</ref> From an evolutionary perspective, jealousy is a cognitive adaptation to deal with the possibility of mate loss, motivating an individual to take actions in order keep their current mate.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Salovey|first1=Peter|last2=Rodin|first2=Judith|date=1991|title=Provoking Jealousy and Envy: Domain Relevance and Self-Esteem Threat|journal=Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology|volume=10|issue=4|pages=395–413|doi=10.1521/jscp.1991.10.4.395|issn=0736-7236}}</ref> But there are sex differences on what make humans jealous.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Buss|first=D. M.|title=The dangerous passion: Why jealousy is as necessary as love and sex|publisher=Free Press|year=2000|location=New York, NY}}</ref> Since males have issues confirming parental certainty, something that does not happen to females, they become more jealous than females for sexual cheating. Indeed, the majority of adult women who fall victim to [[Murder|felonious homicide]] were murdered by their husbands or boyfriends, with the most frequent reason being real or suspected infidelity.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book |last=Saad |first=Gad |author-link=Gad Saad |title=The Consuming Instinct: What Juicy Burgers, Ferraris, Pornography, and Gift Giving Reveal about Human Nature |publisher=Prometheus Books |year=2011 |isbn=978-1-61614-429-6 |location=New York}}</ref>{{Reference page|page=16}} Females, on the other hand, have historically needed male resources for offspring investment. Therefore, females are more concerned with emotional cheating, as it threatens the devotion of resources to them and their offspring. As previous mentioned, sexual intercourse can facilitate pair-bonding.<ref name="Bainbridge-2009" />{{Reference page|pages=255-6}} However, females tend to be more sexually generous than males. For example, Internet search data reveals that women are twice more likely to look up how to please their partners with their mouths than men. By contrast, men are equally interested in performing oral sex on their partners and on themselves.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|pages=127-8}}

In feudal China, a highly patriarchal society, men (and their parents) bound the feet of their wives to prevent them from fleeing. Even songs and poems in [[Nüshu]] (literally "women's script"), used by Yao and Miao women in Hunan Province, taught women to endure, obey, be loyal to their father-in-laws and their husbands.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Lofthouse |first=Andrew |date=October 1, 2020 |title=Nüshu: China's secret female-only language |url=https://www.bbc.com/travel/article/20200930-nshu-chinas-secret-female-only-language |access-date=January 4, 2026 |work=BBC News}}</ref>

As previously mentioned, human genitals have not evolved to be sexual displays or signals, unlike in other animals. In the case of a male penis, however, while its length helps it deposit sperm further up the vagina, its width increases the friction with the vaginal walls, sexually arousing and pleasing the woman. In consequence, it enhances the coital experience and strengthens the emotional bond between man and woman.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=24}}

In a typical romantic relationship, the man is more dependent on his partner for emotional support than the woman, whose friends and family can provide what she needs, a consequence of societal norms of [[masculinity]], which dictate that man should limit the public display of his emotions. Women may focus on fulfilling the emotional needs of their [[Social isolation|socially isolated]] male partners in order to maintain household harmony, though at the cost of jeopardizing their own mental health, social relationships, and personal autonomy.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ferrara |first1=Angelica Puzio |last2=Vergara |first2=Dylan |date=May 2025 |title=The Hidden Costs of Men's Social Isolation |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/the-hidden-costs-of-mens-social-isolation/ |journal=Scientific American |volume=332 |issue=5 |pages=82–3 |doi=10.1038/scientificamerican052025-3CPGG7AmkbVqTJRr8d9IST |pmid=40232977 }}</ref>

Small but thoughtful gestures of affection may be employed by both sexes to demonstrate commitment and understanding.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Mumphrey |first=Cheyanne |date=February 8, 2026 |title=Relationship experts and couples say romance doesn't need grand gestures to thrive |url=https://apnews.com/article/valentines-day-love-romance-relationships-437cd9b31d20aa039f2f42bec7321dd4 |access-date=February 8, 2026 |work=Associated Press}}</ref> Empirical data indicates that relationships with a relatively large age gap, despite popular conception, can be just as long-lasting and happy as those with smaller ones.<ref name=":26" /> As of the mid-2020s, the average age gap was 2.2 years in North America, with the men being older, 2.7 in Europe, 4 in the Asia-Pacific region, and 8.6 in sub-Saharan Africa, the highest in the world. The global average age gap was 4.2.<ref name=":26" />

==== Intrasexual competition ====
For both sexes, high social status and ample access to resources are important for evolutionary success. But each sex has its own strategies for competing against members of the same sex. To safeguard their genetic interests, girls and women tend to form alliances with kin, affines (in-laws), and a few select female friends. Instead of direct competition, females tend to disguise their efforts to outclass their competitors in order to avoid physical harm and violence unless they are already of high status, in which case they can rely on greater protection and greater access to resources. Other strategies include enforcing equality within a social clique in order to minimize competition and excluding other females—that is, potential competitors—from one's social circles.<ref name="Benenson-2013">{{Cite journal|last=Benenson|first=Joyce F.|date=5 December 2013|title=The development of human female competition: allies and adversaries|journal=Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London B: Biological Sciences|volume=368|issue=1631|doi=10.1098/rstb.2013.0079|pmid=24167309 |pmc=3826208}}</ref>

==== Abuse and violence ====
As a society becomes more urbanized, that is, as population density increases, incidents of internecine violence become more common. Within a household or a romantic relationship, men are much more likely to physically assault or even murder women than the other way around. [[Domestic violence and pregnancy|Pregnant women are even more likely to be beaten]] by their husbands or boyfriends. [[Battery (crime)|Battery]] is in fact a leading cause of injuries for women. Cases of [[domestic violence]] are often the result of (long-term) revenge, including [[redirected aggression]]. Men who beat their wives or girlfriends were likely beaten as children. Battered women, in turns, may beat their own children.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=194-5}} The risk of homicide is markedly higher if the men is at least 16 years older than the woman, or if the women is at least 10 years older than the man, though cases of such large age gaps are rare.<ref name=":26">{{Cite news |date=January 29, 2026 |title=Age gaps in relationships are not as bad as you think |url=https://www.economist.com/culture/2026/01/29/age-gaps-in-relationships-are-not-as-bad-as-you-think |access-date=March 9, 2026 |work=The Economist}}</ref> Despite the rising economic independence of women in modern countries, men's attitudes have not changed. Men who earn less than their wives or girlfriends are more inclined to verbally or physically abuse them, and such relationships are more likely to dissolve, though not necessarily among younger generations, who tend to hold more egalitarian views.<ref name=":24" />

As previously mentioned, this sex difference in proclivities towards violence is, according to evolutionary psychologists, a consequence of paternal uncertainty.<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|page=16}} Furthermore, whenever the males of a human species monopolize the females of another, their sexual interactions are likely coercive, as was the case between [[Neanderthal]] males and ''[[Human|Homo sapiens]]'' females, who, according to genetic evidence, preferentially mated with another.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Alex |first=Brigid |date=February 26, 2026 |title=Surprising partner preference found in matings between Neanderthals and modern humans |url=https://www.science.org/content/article/surprising-partner-preference-found-matings-between-neanderthals-and-modern-humans |journal=Science |volume=391 |issue=6788}}</ref>

=== Individual differences ===

==== Sociosexual Orientation Inventory ====
Just as there are differences between the sexes in mating strategies, there are differences within the sexes and such within-sex variation is substantial.<ref name="Gangestad, S. W. 2000">Gangestad, S.W., & Simpson, J.A. (2000). [http://www2.psych.ubc.ca/~schaller/Psyc591Readings/GangestadSimpson2000.pdf "The evolution of human mating: Trade-offs and strategic pluralism"]. ''Behavioral and Brain Sciences'', 23(04), 573–587.</ref> Individual differences in mating strategies are commonly measured using the Sociosexual Orientation Inventory (SOI), a questionnaire that includes items assessing past sexual behavior, anticipated future sexual behavior, and openness to casual sex.<ref>Simpson, J.A., & Gangestad, S.W. (1991). [https://psycnet.apa.org/record/1991-26250-001 "Individual differences in sociosexuality: evidence for convergent and discriminant validity"]. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 60(6), 870.</ref> Higher scores on the SOI indicate a ''sexually unrestricted'' mating strategy, which indicates an openness to casual sex and more partners. Conversely, lower scores on the SOI indicate a ''sexually restricted'' mating strategy, which a focus on higher commitment and fewer partners.

Several studies have found that scores on the SOI are related to mate preferences, with more sexually restricted individuals preferring personal/parenting qualities in a mate (e.g. responsibility and loyalty), and with less sexual restricted individual preferring qualities related to physical attractiveness and social visibility.<ref>Simpson, J.A. & Gangestad, S.W. (1992). [http://ablongman.com/partners_in_psych/PDFs/Kenrick/kenrick_CH10.pdf "Sociosexuality and romantic partner choice"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170808164114/http://www.ablongman.com/partners_in_psych/PDFs/Kenrick/kenrick_CH10.pdf |date=8 August 2017 }}. ''Journal of Personality'', 60(1), 31–51.</ref> Other studies have shown that SOI scores are related to personality traits (i.e. [[extraversion]], [[erotophilia]], and low [[agreeableness]]),<ref>Wright, T.M., & Reise, S.P. (1997). "Personality and unrestricted sexual behavior: Correlations of sociosexuality in Caucasian and Asian college students". ''Journal of Research in Personality'', 31(2), 166–192.</ref> [[conspicuous consumption]] in men as a means to attract women,<ref name="Sundie-2011">Sundie, J.M., Kenrick, D.T., Griskevicius, V., Tybur, J.M., Vohs, K.D., & Beal, D.J. (2011). [https://web.archive.org/web/20171211053313/https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/b944/dad3b7501b97c9f181eb35aca4df32cb30b7.pdf "Peacocks, Porsches, and Thorstein Veblen: conspicuous consumption as a sexual signaling system"]. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 100(4), 664.</ref> and increased allocation of visual attention to attractive opposite-sex faces.<ref>Duncan, L.A., Park, J.H., Faulkner, J., Schaller, M., Neuberg, S.L., & Kenrick, D.T. (2007). [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2034358/ "Adaptive allocation of attention: Effects of sex and sociosexuality on visual attention to attractive opposite-sex faces"]. ''Evolution and Human Behavior'', 28(5), 359–364.</ref>

==== Short-term vs. long-term mating ====
Evolutionary psychologists have proposed that individuals adopt conditional mating strategies in which they adjust their mating tactics to relevant environmental or internal conditions, which is called strategic pluralism.<ref name="Gangestad, S. W. 2000"/> The concept of sexual pluralism states that humans do not pursue the same mating strategy all of the time. There are different motivations and environmental influences that determine the mating strategy which a person will adopt. The long-term and short-term mating behaviors are triggered in the individual by the current strategy being pursued.<ref name="Schmitt-2014" /> Therefore, not only are there differences between the sexes in long-term and short-term mating, but there are also differences within the sexes. To the extent that ancestral men were capable of pursuing short-term mating strategies with multiple women, they tend to do so. However, not every male is able to pursue this option. Additionally, even though most women pursue a long term mating strategy, some females pursue a short-term strategy.

==== Differences among males ====
When possible, males will typically pursue a short-term mating strategy.<ref name="Buss-1993" /> The ability to do this depends upon their mate value, so males with a high mate value are more likely to pursue a short-term mating strategy.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Pedersen|first=F. A.|date=1991|title=Secular trends in human sex ratios: Their influence on individual and family behavior|journal=Human Nature|volume=2|issue=3|pages=271–291|doi=10.1007/BF02692189|pmid=24222281|s2cid=824054}}</ref> High mate value males have been shown to have sexual intercourse earlier and more often than low mate value males. Self-esteem and physical attractiveness have been shown to be related to male pursuing a short term mating strategy.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kirkpatrick|first1=Lee A.|last2=Waugh|first2=Christian E.|last3=Valencia|first3=Alelhie|last4=Webster|first4=Gregory D.|date=2002|title=The functional domain specificity of self-esteem and the differential prediction of aggression.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=82|issue=5|pages=756–767|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.82.5.756|pmid=12003475|issn=1939-1315}}</ref> Additionally, males with more testosterone have been shown to pursue more short-term strategies.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Manning|first=J. T.|title=Digit ratio: A pointer to fertility, behavior, and health.|publisher=Rutgers University Press|year=2002|location=New Brunswick, NJ}}</ref>

However, not all males pursue a short-term mating strategy. There are several reasons for this. First, long-term mating has its own advantages that have already been discussed. Second, while males of higher mate value and status have opportunities to pursue short-term mates, low mate value males typically do not have the same opportunities. Since females generally prefer long-term mating strategies, the few who would mate in the short-term are already paired with the high mate value males. Additionally, the benefits of short-term mating for females are only obtained through high mate value males. Therefore, low status males are more likely to pursue long-term mating strategy.

==== Differences among females ====
While more attractive males tend to pursue a short-term mating strategy, more attractive females tend to pursue a more long-term mating strategy. Additionally, younger females are more likely to pursue a short-term mating strategy, as well as those who are not satisfied with their current partner.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Greiling|first1=Heidi|last2=Buss|first2=David M|date=2000|title=Women's sexual strategies: the hidden dimension of extra-pair mating|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=28|issue=5|pages=929–963|doi=10.1016/s0191-8869(99)00151-8|issn=0191-8869}}</ref>

The ovulatory cycle has been shown to influence a female's mating strategy. In the late follicular phase, women are the most fertile in the ovulatory cycle.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Regan|first=P. C.|date=1996|title=Rhythms of desire: The association between menstrual cycle phases and female sexual desire|url=https://psycnet.apa.org/record/1997-02985-001|journal=The Canadian Journal of Human Sexuality|volume=5|pages=145–156}}</ref> During this time, there is evidence that females tend to pursue a short-term oriented mating strategy over a long-term one.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cantú|first1=Stephanie M.|last2=Simpson|first2=Jeffry A.|last3=Griskevicius|first3=Vladas|last4=Weisberg|first4=Yanna J.|last5=Durante|first5=Kristina M.|last6=Beal|first6=Daniel J.|date=2014|title=Fertile and Selectively Flirty: Women's Behavior Toward Men Changes Across the Ovulatory Cycle|journal=Psychological Science|language=en|volume=25|issue=2|pages=431–438|doi=10.1177/0956797613508413|pmid=24335600|s2cid=15136174|issn=0956-7976}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Grammer|first1=Karl|last2=Renninger|first2=LeeAnn|last3=Fischer|first3=Bettina|date=2004|title=Disco Clothing, Female Sexual Motivation, and Relationship Status: Is She Dressed to Impress?|journal=The Journal of Sex Research|volume=41|issue=1|pages=66–74|doi=10.1080/00224490409552214|jstor=3813404|pmid=15216425|s2cid=16965002|issn=0022-4499}}</ref> Additionally, female sexual desires increase as well as their attraction towards more masculine males.<ref name="Buss-1993" />

Additionally, female mating strategies can change across their lifetime. In their early thirties, females experience a peak in sexual desire.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Baker|first1=R. R.|title=Human sperm competition|last2=Bellis|first2=M. A.|publisher=Chapman & Hall|year=1995|location=London, England}}</ref> In turn, this increase influences females to pursue a more long or short term oriented strategy depending on the mate value of their current partner.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Barr|first1=Alicia|last2=Bryan|first2=Angela|last3=Kenrick|first3=Douglas T.|date=2002|title=Sexual Peak: Socially Shared Cognitions About Desire, Frequency, and Satisfaction in Men and Women|journal=Personal Relationships|volume=9|issue=3|pages=287–299|doi=10.1111/1475-6811.09305|issn=1350-4126|doi-access=free}}</ref>

==== Mating plasticity ====
Research on the conditional nature of mating strategies has revealed that long-term and short-term mating preferences can be fairly plastic. Following exposure to cues that would have been affected mating in the ancestral past, both men and women appear to adjust their mating preferences in ways that would have historically enhanced their fitness. Such cues include the need to care for young, danger from animals and other humans, and resource availability.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Thomas|first1=Andrew G.|last2=Stewart-Williams|first2=Steve|title=Mating strategy flexibility in the laboratory: Preferences for long- and short-term mating change in response to evolutionarily relevant variables|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=39|issue=1|pages=82–93|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2017.10.004|date=January 2018|bibcode=2018EHumB..39...82T |url=https://cronfa.swan.ac.uk/Record/cronfa36154}}</ref> Additionally, there is evidence that the female sex drive is more plastic than male sex drive, because they are the selecting sex.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Baumeister|first=Roy F.|date=2000|title=Gender differences in erotic plasticity: The female sex drive as socially flexible and responsive.|journal=Psychological Bulletin|language=en|volume=126|issue=3|pages=347–374|doi=10.1037/0033-2909.126.3.347|pmid=10825779|s2cid=35777544 |issn=1939-1455}}</ref> Since females typically chose when and with whom to engage in sex, this sex drive plasticity could be an effect of female [[mate choice]].

=== Alternatives to opposite-sex pair-bonding ===

==== Asexuality, aromanticism, and celibacy ====
While the general lack of sexual attraction—[[asexuality]]—has traditionally been viewed as a problem to be rectified, research since the 2010s has cast doubt upon this view. Research on how asexual individuals forge social relationships, including romantic ones, is ongoing.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Marshall |first=Allison |date=January 2024 |title=Life Beyond Sex |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/asexuality-is-finally-breaking-free-from-medical-stigma/ |journal=Scientific American |volume=330 |issue=1 |pages=68–73 |doi=10.1038/scientificamerican0124-68 |pmid=39017380 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The Catholic Church has traditionally demanded a vow of [[celibacy]] from nuns and priests. Nuns may be formally married to Jesus Christ by ritual and may engage in various nurturing activities as they would in an ordinary household. Priests, on the other hand, may not marry and must [[Sexual abstinence|abstain]] from sexual intercourse. However, many priests have left the Church in order to get married and many of those who remain fail to maintain their vows. Some have even been caught in scandals involving sexual misconduct, including pedophilia.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=117-20}}

==== Homosexuality ====
[[File:Anh&Cung wedding.jpg|thumb|A (Vietnamese) gay couple at their wedding ceremony in Canada (2006)]]
In the medieval Europe, [[homosexuality]] was deemed unacceptable, even a crime punishable by death.<ref name=":13" /> However, by the late eighteen and nineteenth centuries, in the Great Britain, the severity of penalties for homosexuality was dramatically reduced.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=121}}  In Western countries in the twenty-first century, homosexuality is no longer considered a crime, partly as a consequence of growing acceptance of the notion of the [[right to privacy]].<ref name=":13">{{Cite news |date=June 19, 2025 |title=The right to privacy has been hard-earned. It is imperilled again |url=https://www.economist.com/culture/2025/06/19/the-right-to-privacy-has-been-hard-earned-it-is-imperilled-again |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250624145240/https://www.economist.com/culture/2025/06/19/the-right-to-privacy-has-been-hard-earned-it-is-imperilled-again |archive-date=June 24, 2025 |access-date=November 19, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref> In many jurisdictions today, a homosexual couple may choose to get [[Same-sex marriage|married]], in which case they would receive the same benefits as their heterosexual counterparts, including social security, pensions, discounted membership fees, medical decision-making, among others.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=120}}

==== Platonic relationships and friendships ====
{{See also|Boston marriage|romantic friendship|queerplatonic relationship|work spouse}}

In a given society, whether it is feasible for men and women to be in a purely [[Platonic relationship]] or friendship depends upon the level of gender equality in said society, as measured in terms of female workforce participation, how common it is for girls to be married off to older men, how the law treats women in the context of inheritance and private properties, and whether rape, of which most victims are girls and women, is considered a property crime against men, among other factors.<ref name=":12">{{Cite news |date=June 19, 2025 |title=Can men and women be just friends? |url=https://www.economist.com/international/2025/06/19/can-men-and-women-be-just-friends |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250622130541/https://www.economist.com/international/2025/06/19/can-men-and-women-be-just-friends |archive-date=June 22, 2025 |access-date=November 12, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref> As sociologist Alice Evans explains, "Where men's honor depends on women's seclusion, [[Cross-sex friendship|cross-gender friendships]] are rare."<ref name=":12" /> In Turkey, due to the influence of conservative Muslims, opposite-sex friendships are deemed immoral because they are viewed as a [[slippery slope]] to adultery. In Nigeria, on the other hand, where women's participation in the labor force is almost on par with men, such friendships are ubiquitous, at least on Facebook.<ref name=":12" />

==== Polygamy ====
{{multiple image
| align             = right
| direction         = vertical
| width             = 300
| header            = 
| image1            = A Zulu and his wives, South Africa LCCN2017657661.tif
| caption1          = A polygamous [[Zulu people|Zulu]] warrior and his wives, 1890.
| image2            = Legality of polygamy.svg
| caption2          = World map of the legality of polygamy (as of 2019).
{{legend striped|#009e73|#009e73|Polygamy is only legal for Muslims}}
{{legend striped|#56b4e9|#56b4e9|Polygamy is legal}}
{{legend striped|#d55e00|#d55e00|Polygamy is illegal, but practice is not criminalised}}
{{legend striped|#000000|#000000|Polygamy is illegal and practice criminalised}}
{{legend striped|#e0e0e0|#e0e0e0|Legal status unknown}}
}}

Culture also has a link to mating strategies in the form of marriage systems in the society. Specifically, pathogens have been linked to whether a society is more likely to have [[Polygyny|polygynous]] or monogamous marriage systems. Cultures with high pathogen stress are more likely to have polygynous marriage systems, especially exogamous polygamy systems.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Low|first=B. S.|date=1990|title=Marriage Systems and Pathogen Stress in Human Societies|url=https://academic.oup.com/icb/article/30/2/325/164387|journal=American Zoologist|volume=30|issue=2|pages=325–340|doi=10.1093/icb/30.2.325|doi-access=free}}</ref> This is helpful for both males and females, as males obtain greater genetic diversity for their offspring and females have access to healthy males, which are typically lacking in high pathogen societies.<ref name="Low-2000" /> Conversely, monogamy is often absent from high pathogen environments, but common in low pathogen environments.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dow|first1=M. M.|last2=Eff|first2=E. A.|date=2013|title=When One Wife is Enough: A Cross-Cultural Study of the Determinants of Monogamy|journal=Journal of Social, Evolutionary, and Cultural Psychology|volume=7|issue=3|pages=211–238|bibcode=2013IJECP...7..211D|doi=10.1037/h0099200|doi-access=free}}</ref>

Polygamy takes two forms, [[polygyny]] (a husband having multiple wives) and [[polyandry]] (a wife having multiple husbands).<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=103}} In some historical and current societies, it is permissible to have more than one mate at any one time, and this may be justified by religion. In the Old Testament, men with the highest status had the largest numbers of mates.<ref name=":3" /> Islam permits polygyny—a Muslim may marry up to four women—partly because Islamic societies historically faced high mortality rates among males due to frequent interpersonal violence, including and up to warfare. This serves as a form of social welfare for women, because, according to Islamic tradition, they may not pursue their own careers or to be without a male guardian.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=111-2}} The [[Topkapı Palace|Topkapi Palace]], located in what is now Istanbul, Turkey, was home to a [[harem]] during the time of the [[Ottoman Empire]]. Women of the harem were isolated from the outside world but enjoyed a life of luxury. Court intrigue, sometimes with deadly consequences, were common, however.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=104}} Indeed, [[Polygamy|polygamous]] societies tend to be more unstable than monogamous ones, as men of high status in such a society tend to have more children, who may well aspire for positions of power and influence even though their sociopolitical structure may not be able to accommodate them, a phenomenon known as [[elite overproduction]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Reeves|first=Richard V.|date=June 2023|title=No Room at the Top|url=https://literaryreview.co.uk/no-room-at-the-top|access-date=October 21, 2024|work=Literary Review}}</ref>

While polygyny can be found in many different societies, polyandry is much rarer. One polyandrous society is the [[Toda people]] in Southern India. However, the co-husbands of a woman are always brothers. In such a union, all brothers have sexual access to their shared wife and the paternity of the children born is decided by ritual.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=112-3}}

==== Prostitution ====
Regardless of its [[Legality of prostitution|legality]] and social acceptability, prostitution exists across different times and cultures. In ancient Greece and Rome, [[Prostitution in ancient Greece|prostitution]] was not only legal but also seen as a social good for preserving the family. It was thought to be an outlet for the sex drive of unmarried men, lest they seduce married women, and for married men, who might have felt unsatisfied in the bedroom despite being otherwise happy with family life. Of the former group, the Roman poet [[Horace]] wrote, "As soon as desire brings the blood in the veins of young men to a boiling point, it is right and just that they should go this way and not seduce respectable married women." In the ancient Mediterranean port city of [[Ephesus]] (now part of Turkey), there once existed a large brothel in its [[Downtown|central area]].<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=97-101}}

==== Solitary sex ====
[[Masturbation]] is a behavior found across the animal kingdom, including humans.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Brindle |first=Matilda |date=June 7, 2023 |title=Why we're searching for the evolutionary origins of masturbation – and the results so far |url=https://theconversation.com/why-were-searching-for-the-evolutionary-origins-of-masturbation-and-the-results-so-far-207127 |access-date=November 21, 2025 |work=The Conversation}}</ref> A person having sex in isolation can avoid commitment, relationship troubles, as well as venereal diseases. Due to rising demand, [[sex toy]]s of a variety of shapes and sizes have become common in the Western world since the late twentieth century. Masturbation can be done with the aid of pornographic videos and magazines. Today, people may also have sexual intercourse with one another remotely [[Cybersex|via the Internet]].<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=122-24}}

== Effects of modernity ==
During pre-industrial era, people most commonly married those living nearby, partly because meeting someone far away was not feasible given the transportation and communication technologies of the time. But the [[Industrial Revolution]] and various changes in politics and culture have led people to think more about their own personal choices. Women's growing workforce participation in modern industrial economies is one of the key factors responsible for how modern mating is different from the past.<ref name=":1">{{Cite news |last=O'Grady |first=Eileen |date=February 13, 2025 |title=Tech has changed. Dating? It's complicated. |url=https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2025/02/tech-has-changed-dating-its-complicated/ |access-date=October 2, 2025 |work=Harvard Gazette}}</ref> In Germany, for instance, teenage boys who have girls as friends tend to have more egalitarian views, possibly because they wish to one day have a romantic relationship with them. Girls, by contrast, do not generally change their expectation of equal treatment and are unlikely to want to give up on their career dreams just to please the boys.<ref name=":12" />

During times of economic distress, women would be highly reluctant to commit to low-status men in long-term relationships and men would be delaying marriage, if they ever get married at all, in order to accumulate enough resources to attract attention. Consequently, both marriage and birth rates would drop.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Seitz |first1=Benjamin M. |last2=Aktipis |first2=Athena |display-authors=1 |date=10 November 2020 |title=The pandemic exposes human nature: 10 evolutionary insights |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |volume=117 |issue=45 |pages=27767–27776 |bibcode=2020PNAS..11727767S |doi=10.1073/pnas.2009787117 |pmc=7668083 |pmid=33093198 |doi-access=free}}</ref> During the early twenty-first century, young adults around the world are much more likely to be romantically unattached, either by choice or circumstance, than older generations, and the decline is steepest among the poor.<ref name=":5">{{Cite news |last=Burn-Murdoch |first=John |date=January 11, 2025 |title=The relationship recession is going global |url=https://www.ft.com/content/43e2b4f6-5ab7-4c47-b9fd-d611c36dad74 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250403193529/https://www.ft.com/content/43e2b4f6-5ab7-4c47-b9fd-d611c36dad74 |archive-date=April 3, 2025 |access-date=May 26, 2025 |work=Financial Times}}</ref> At the regional level, East Asia, the Middle East, North Africa, and Latin America saw the greatest drops compared to the 2000s.<ref name=":5" />
[[File:A Girl Defending Herself against Eros, by William-Adolphe Bouguereau.jpg|left|thumb|''A Girl Defending Herself against Eros'' (1880) by William-Adolphe Bouguereau. People today are increasingly choosing to remain romantically unattached.]]
Historically, marriage was the best option to gain independence from one's parents, and people generally married early in life and after short periods of courtship. But this is no longer true in a modern society, where people are more independent from their parents and are willing to wait longer to find an ideal mate (a "[[soulmate]]").<ref name="Kerley-2016">{{Cite news |last=Kerley |first=Paul |date=February 13, 2016 |title=The graphs that show the search for love has changed |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/magazine-35535424 |access-date=October 12, 2024 |work=BBC Magazine}}</ref> Consequently, the average age at first marriage has increased,<ref name="Kerley-2016" /> while many individuals are [[Singleton (lifestyle)|choosing to remain single]].<ref name="Thomson-DeVeaux-2023" /> In the Scandinavian countries, single-person households have become commonplace.<ref name=":10" /> In East Asia, urban and highly educated women are wary of the high [[opportunity cost]] of marriage in their patriarchal societies; they may have to give up on their careers and incomes to take care of their in-laws, parents, children, and do housework.<ref name=":24" /> People value their independence more than in the past.<ref name="Bolick-2011">{{Cite news |last=Bolick |first=Kate |date=November 2011 |title=All the Single Ladies |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2011/11/all-the-single-ladies/308654/?single_page=true |archive-url=https://archive.today/20170823053616/https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2011/11/all-the-single-ladies/308654/?single_page=true |archive-date=August 23, 2017 |access-date=June 30, 2025 |work=The Atlantic}}</ref> Furthermore, in a country where few children are born out of wedlock like Japan, those who are [[Voluntary childlessness|uninterested in having children]] tend to not get married.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Rich |first=Motoko |date=August 3, 2019 |title=Craving Freedom, Japan's Women Opt Out of Marriage |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/08/03/world/asia/japan-single-women-marriage.html |archive-url=https://archive.today/20210702100731/https://www.nytimes.com/2019/08/03/world/asia/japan-single-women-marriage.html |archive-date=July 2, 2021 |access-date=October 14, 2024 |work=The New York Times}}</ref>

Some sex differences in mate preferences may be attenuated by national levels of gender equity and [[gender empowerment]].<ref>Eagly, A.H., & Wood, W. (1999). [https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/behavioral-and-brain-sciences/article/origins-of-aggression-sex-differences-evolved-dispositions-versus-social-roles/06737D63DBC6B517201549DE5D7AA617 "The origins of sex differences in human behavior: Evolved dispositions versus social roles"]. ''American Psychologist'', 54(6), 408.</ref><ref>Zentner, M., & Mitura, K. (2012). "Stepping Out of the Caveman's Shadow Nations' Gender Gap Predicts Degree of Sex Differentiation in Mate Preferences". ''Psychological Science'', 23(10), 1176–1185.</ref> For example, as women gain more access to resources their mate preferences change.<ref>Gangestad, S.W., Haselton, M.G., & Buss, D.M. (2006). [https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David_Buss/publication/233345027_AUTHORS'_RESPONSE_Toward_an_Integrative_Understanding_of_Evoked_and_Transmitted_Culture_The_Importance_of_Specialized_Psychological_Design/links/0912f5100137862343000000.pdf#page=4 Evolutionary foundations of cultural variation: "Evoked culture and mate preferences"]. ''Psychological Inquiry'', 17(2), 75–95.</ref> While in the past, women typically need to get married in order to ensure their own financial security, modern women are more likely to be able to achieve this on their own and as such are in a position to set high standards for potential mates.<ref name="Thomson-DeVeaux-2023">{{Cite news |last=Thomson-DeVeaux |first=Amelia |date=February 14, 2023 |title=Americans Are Increasingly Single And OK With It |url=https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/more-americans-are-choosing-to-stay-single/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230215014342/https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/more-americans-are-choosing-to-stay-single/ |archive-date=February 15, 2023 |access-date=September 29, 2024 |work=Five Thirty Eight}}</ref><ref name="Burn-Murdoch-2024">{{Cite news |last=Burn-Murdoch |first=John |date=March 29, 2024 |title=Why family-friendly policies don't boost birth rates |url=https://www.ft.com/content/838eeb4e-3bff-4693-990f-ff3446cac9b2 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20240329160813/https://www.ft.com/content/838eeb4e-3bff-4693-990f-ff3446cac9b2 |archive-date=March 29, 2024 |access-date=March 29, 2024 |work=Financial Times}}</ref> Finding a mate with access to material resources becomes less of a priority compared to finding someone with domestic skills and who can provide emotional support.<ref name="Gangestad-2006">Gangestad, S.W., Haselton, M.G., & Buss, D.M. (2006). [https://www.jstor.org/stable/pdf/20447315.pdf "Evolutionary foundations of cultural variation: Evoked culture and mate preferences"]. ''Psychological Inquiry'', 17(2), 75–95.</ref><ref name="Thomson-DeVeaux-2023" /> For this reason, female physical attractiveness and male access to resources can be labeled as "necessities" while other qualities, as such as humor, can be categorized as "luxuries" in finding a mate.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Norman P. |last2=Bailey |first2=J. Michael |last3=Kenrick |first3=Douglas T. |last4=Linsenmeier |first4=Joan A. W. |date=2002 |title=The necessities and luxuries of mate preferences: Testing the tradeoffs. |url=https://ink.library.smu.edu.sg/soss_research/718 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=82 |issue=6 |pages=947–955 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.82.6.947 |issn=1939-1315 |pmid=12051582}}</ref> Nevertheless, instincts that evolved during the [[Pleistocene]] persist, and humans will continue to look for traits associated with good health and fertility in a mate, regardless of the era of history they live in or whether they wish to have children.<ref name="Etcoff-1999">{{Cite book |last=Etcoff |first=Nancy |author-link=Nancy Etcoff |title=Survival of the Prettiest |publisher=Doubleday |year=1999 |isbn=0-385-47854-2 |location=New York |pages= |chapter=}}</ref>{{Reference page|page=242}}

Since the late twentieth century, marriages across the developed world have become unstable. Divorces have become much more common while people increasingly choose to remain single.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Matsuda |first1=Shigeki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JNLHDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA30 |title=Low Fertility in Advanced Asian Economies: Focusing on Families, Education, and Labor Markets |date=January 3, 2020 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-0710-6 |pages=29–30 |language=en}} Table 3, pages 29-30. "It is notable that the proportion of males who had ever married/cohabited in the three Asian countries were lower than of males in the same category in the European countries -- in other words, Asian men are more likely to be single."</ref><ref name="Hobsbawn-1996b">{{Cite book |last=Hobsbawm |first=Eric |url=https://archive.org/details/ageofextremeshis0000hobs/ |title=The Age of Extremes: The Short Twentieth Century 1914-1991 |publisher=Abacus |year=1996 |isbn=978-0-349-10671-7 |chapter=Chapter Eleven: Cultural Revolution}}</ref> In addition, as a culture becomes more individualistic, public support for traditional gender roles declines.<ref name="Twenge-2023b">{{Cite book |last=Twenge |first=Jean |author-link=Jean Twenge |title=Generations: The Real Differences Between Gen Z, Millennials, Gen X, Boomers, and Silents—and What The Mean for America's Future |publisher=Atria Books |year=2023 |isbn=978-1-9821-8161-1 |location=New York |chapter=Chapter 3: Boomers}}</ref> Marriage becomes increasingly viewed as an option, rather than an obligation,<ref name="Twenge-2023b" /> and people are more open about the challenges of remaining married.<ref>{{Cite news |date=January 13, 2026 |title=How well do you know the person lying next to you in bed? |url=https://www.economist.com/culture/2026/01/13/how-well-do-you-know-the-person-lying-next-to-you-in-bed |work=The Economist |pages=|access-date=March 7, 2026}}</ref> In fact, since the 1960s, marriage has stopped being primarily focused on having and raising children but instead, the fulfillment of adults.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Brooks |first=David |date=March 2020 |title=The Nuclear Family Was A Mistake |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2020/03/the-nuclear-family-was-a-mistake/605536/ |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231119143819/https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2020/03/the-nuclear-family-was-a-mistake/605536/ |archive-date=November 19, 2023 |access-date=April 12, 2024 |work=The Atlantic}}</ref> Unmarried women were no longer considered "sick" or "immoral" the way they were in the past. In addition, neither working mothers nor [[single parent]]hood (what used to be called [[Legitimacy (family law)|illegitimacy]]) was socially ostracized the way they used to be, at least in the Western world.<ref name="Twenge-2023b" /> For instance, almost three fifths of children born in France in 2015 did not have married parents.<ref name=":8">{{Cite news |last=Budd |first=Joel |date=November 23, 2017 |title=The state of marriage as an institution |url=https://www.economist.com/special-report/2017/11/23/the-state-of-marriage-as-an-institution |archive-url=https://archive.today/20191225140335/https://www.economist.com/special-report/2017/11/23/the-state-of-marriage-as-an-institution |archive-date=December 25, 2019 |access-date=November 6, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref> But while marriage rates have declined, the prevalence of cohabitation (formerly referred to as "living in sin") has gone up.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=159}} [[Cohabitation]] may help determine suitability of a mate before marriage.<ref name="Kalmijn-1994" /> At the same time, significant numbers [[Criticism of marriage|deem marriage to be an outdated institution]] that reinforces the subjugation of women<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=159}} and deem it unnecessary for a fulfilling or happy life, though they may remain open to that option.<ref name="Nazzaro-2023">{{Cite news |last=Nazzaro |first=Miranda |date=July 20, 2023 |title=2 in 5 young adults surveyed say marriage an outdated tradition |url=https://thehill.com/changing-america/respect/equality/4107946-2-in-5-young-adults-surveyed-say-marriage-an-outdated-tradition/ |access-date=September 29, 2024 |work=The Hill}}</ref> But among those who choose to get married in the modern era, it is common to ignore various customs that signify the subordinate role of the woman. For example, in contemporary North America, wedding ceremonies tend to emphasize gender equality; the bride is no longer "given away" by her father.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=158-9}} Modern Western couples typically treat their wedding ceremonies as special and romantic occasions, a celebration of love and commitment between two individuals.<ref name=":8" /> Western couples with higher levels of education and wealth tend to marry later, after choosing their mates carefully, and have more durable marriages; divorces are more common among the lower classes of society.<ref name=":8" />[[File:The Ladies' home journal (1948) (14745699296).jpg|thumb|Technological change has rendered traditional domestic skills less important in a potential mate.]]Meanwhile, married men have become noticeably less willing to disrupt the careers of their wives.<ref name="Hobsbawn-1996b" /> Whereas in the past women had typically looked for men of high social status while men had not, by the late twentieth-century, men also looked for women of high earning potential, resulting in an even more pronounced educational and economic assortative mating. More generally, higher rates of university attendance and [[Women in the workforce|workforce participation by women]] affects the marital expectations of both sexes in that both men and women became more symmetrical in what they desired in a mate. The share of marriages in which both spouses were of the same educational level steadily rose.<ref name="Mare-1991" /> Moreover, it was no longer possible for a couple with one spouse having no more than a high-school diploma to earn about the national average; on the other hand, couples both of whom have at least a bachelor's degree could expect to make a significant amount above the national average. People thus have a clear economic incentive to seek out a mate with at least as high a level of education in order to maximize their potential income. A societal outcome of this was that as household gender equality improved because women had more choices, income inequality widened.<ref name="TheEconomist2014" /> Part of the reason why people increasingly married their socioeconomic and educational peers was technological change. Innovations that became commercially available in the twentieth century, such as the [[refrigerator]] or the [[washing machine]], reduced the amount of time people needed to spend on housework, which diminished the importance of [[Home economics|domestic skills]].<ref name="The Economist-2017" />
