By the twenty-first century, young women in industrialized nations are more likely than young men to have tertiary education.<ref name="Bolick-2011" /> In some countries, such as the United Kingdom, they even have higher employment rates and higher incomes;<ref name="Burn-Murdoch-2024a">{{Cite news |last=Burn-Murdoch |first=John |date=September 20, 2024 |title=Young women are starting to leave men behind |url=https://www.ft.com/content/17606f25-1d03-4f37-b7f4-f39989af9bde |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250404013953/https://www.ft.com/content/17606f25-1d03-4f37-b7f4-f39989af9bde |archive-date=April 4, 2025 |access-date=May 23, 2025 |work=Financial Times}}</ref><ref name=":2" /> a growing number do not see a need for pairing up.<ref name="Nazzaro-2023" /><ref name="Burn-Murdoch-2024a" /> As sociologist [[Philip N. Cohen|Philip Cohen]] explains, "It's an advantage to not need to be married, in terms of economics or social pressure. People can improve their career status and happiness on their own terms, and they can set the terms for potential mates in the future."<ref name="Thomson-DeVeaux-2023" /> A number of young adults are looking at [[AI companion|artificially intelligent companions]] as alternatives to real-life people,<ref name=":10">{{Cite news |date=November 6, 2025 |title=The rise of singlehood is reshaping the world |url=https://www.economist.com/leaders/2025/11/06/the-rise-of-singlehood-is-reshaping-the-world |archive-url=https://archive.today/20251106110846/https://www.economist.com/leaders/2025/11/06/the-rise-of-singlehood-is-reshaping-the-world |archive-date=November 6, 2025 |access-date=November 6, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref> a form of [[Parasocial interaction|parasocial]] bonding.<ref name=":21">{{Cite news |last1=Prickett |first1=Katy |last2=Bakshi |first2=Mousumi |date=November 17, 2025 |title=Parasocial is Cambridge Dictionary Word of the Year |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cvgmv877746o |access-date=November 23, 2025 |work=BBC News}}</ref>
[[File:Sexual scene at a red-figure tondo of a kylix at the Museo nazionale (Tarquinia).jpg|left|thumb|Classic sexual positions, such as the missionary, shown here in an ancient Greek artwork (circa 470 B.C.), remain popular in the modern era.]]
In the modern era, the availability of reliable [[Birth control|contraception]], [[Contragestion|contragestation]] (colloquially "Plan B" or the "[[Emergency contraception|morning after pill]]"), and the legalization of [[abortion]] have severed the tie between sexual intercourse and reproduction, effectively making women equal to men, at least in the context of dealing with the biological consequences of a sexual encounter.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Brenot |first1=Philippe |title=The Story of Sex: A Graphic History through the Ages |last2=Coryn |first2=Laetitia |publisher=Black Dog & Leventhal |year=2017 |isbn=978-0-316-47222-7 |location=New York |pages= |translator-last=McMorran |translator-first=Will}}</ref><ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=206-8}} The modern concept of privacy has played a role in the legal arguments in favor of these measures.<ref name=":13" /> Since the late twentieth century, young adults have been seeking sterilization procedures ([[Vasectomy|vasectomies]] for men and [[tubal ligation]]s for women) in growing numbers; some even do so as soon as they reach the age of majority.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=189}} On the other hand, classic sexual positions—the [[Missionary position|missionary]], [[Doggy style|doggy]], and [[Woman on top|cowgirl]] styles—remain popular even among couples in the modern era.<ref>{{cite web |last=Laken |first=Howard |date=April 17, 2018 |title=4,000 People Ages 19 -36 Just Ranked This Sex Position As Their Favorite |url=https://www.bustle.com/p/doggy-style-is-millennials-favorite-sex-position-in-2018-8811324 |access-date=November 3, 2025 |website=Bustle |publisher= |quote=}}</ref>

Access to the [[combined oral contraceptive pill]] has been found to change a woman's taste in men. Women not on the pill tend to prefer men whose [[major histocompatibility complex]] (MHC) genes is different from their own, whereas those on the pill tend to find men with similar MHC genes more attractive.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Wenner |first=Melinda |date=December 1, 2008 |title=Birth Control Pills Affect Women's Taste in Men |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/birth-control-pills-affect-womens-taste/ |journal=Scientific American}}</ref>

By the early twenty-first century, trends first observed in the Western world are now also seen in developing countries, though the pace of social change tends to be slower. For example, Asian couples who normally obey their parents nevertheless insist on their future spouses being compatible with them.<ref name=":8" />

== Impact of and on culture ==

=== Adolescent behavior ===
[[File:Portrait of African American couple at Lincoln High School senior prom (6846385789).jpg|left|thumb|For females, late adolescence is a crucial period in determining their reproductive success.]]
From the neurological perspective, the well-known tendencies of teenagers to be emotional, impulsive, and to take high risks are due to the fact that the [[limbic system]] (responsible for emotional thought) is developing faster than the [[prefrontal cortex]] (logical reasoning).<ref name="Murdock-2020">{{Cite news|last=Murdock|first=Andy|date=25 March 2020|title=The evolutionary advantage of the teenage brain|work=University of California News|url=https://www.universityofcalifornia.edu/news/evolutionary-advantage-teenage-brain|access-date=16 June 2021}}</ref> From the evolutionary viewpoint, this mismatch is adaptive in that it helps young people connect with other people (by being emotional) and learn to negotiate the complexities of life (by taking risks yet being more sensitive to rewards).<ref name="Murdock-2020" /><ref name="Ellis-2012">{{Cite journal|last1=Ellis|first1=Bruce J.|last2=Del Giudice|first2=Marco|last3=Wilson|first3=David Sloan|display-authors=2|date=2012|title=The evolutionary basis of risky adolescent behavior: implications for science, policy, and practice.|journal=Developmental Psychology|publisher=American Psychological Association|volume=48|issue=3|pages=598–623|doi=10.1037/a0026220|pmid=22122473|s2cid=543391}}</ref><ref name="Allen-2014">{{Cite news|last=Allen|first=Arthur|date=1 September 2014|title=Risky behavior by teens can be explained in part by how their brains change|newspaper=The Washington Post|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/risky-behavior-by-teens-can-be-explained-in-part-by-how-their-brains-change/2014/08/29/28405df0-27d2-11e4-8593-da634b334390_story.html|access-date=9 September 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210909065409/https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/risky-behavior-by-teens-can-be-explained-in-part-by-how-their-brains-change/2014/08/29/28405df0-27d2-11e4-8593-da634b334390_story.html|archive-date=9 September 2021}}</ref> As a result, teenagers are more prone to feelings of fear, anxiety, and depression than adults.<ref name="Allen-2014" /> In order to attract potential mates, males are especially prone to take [[risk]]s and showcase their athleticism<ref name="Ellis-2012" /> whereas females tend to direct attention to their beauty.<ref name="McAndrew-2016" /> In the context of mating, this group of humans is especially prone to having unwanted pregnancies and [[Sexually transmitted infection|venereal diseases]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Giedd |first=Jay N. |date=May 1, 2016 |title=The Amazing Teen Brain |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/the-amazing-teen-brain/ |journal=Scientific American}}</ref>

Young males (who have the highest reproductive variance) take more risks than any other group in both experiments and observations.<ref name="Wilson-1985">{{Cite journal|last1=Wilson|first1=Margo|last2=Daly|first2=Martin|date=1 January 1985|title=Competitiveness, risk taking, and violence: the young male syndrome|journal=Ethology and Sociobiology|language=en|volume=6|issue=1|pages=59–73|doi=10.1016/0162-3095(85)90041-X|s2cid=9068200 |issn=0162-3095}}</ref><ref name="Baker-2009">{{Cite journal|last1=Baker|first1=Michael D.|last2=Maner|first2=Jon K.|date=2009|title=Male risk-taking as a context-sensitive signaling device|journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology|language=en|volume=45|issue=5|pages=1136–1139|doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2009.06.006}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kelly|first1=S.|last2=Dunbar|first2=R. I.|date=2001|title=Who dares, wins: Heroism versus altruism in women's mate choice|journal=Human Nature (Hawthorne, N.Y.)|volume=12|issue=2|pages=89–105|doi=10.1007/s12110-001-1018-6|issn=1045-6767|pmid=26192164|s2cid=25044517}}</ref> By undertaking risky endeavors, males are thought to signal the qualities which may be directly related to one's ability to provision and protect one's family,<ref name="Farthing-2005">{{Cite journal|last=Farthing|first=G. William|date=2005|title=Attitudes toward heroic and nonheroic physical risk takers as mates and as friends|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|language=en|volume=26|issue=2|pages=171–185|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2004.08.004 |bibcode=2005EHumB..26..171F }}</ref> namely, physical skill, good judgment, or bravery.<ref name="Wilson-1985" /><ref name="Farthing-2005" /> Social dominance, confidence, and ambition could help in competition among other males, while social dominance, ambition, and wealth might alleviate the costs of failure.<ref name="Baker-2009" /> In addition, traits like bravery and physical prowess may also be valued by cooperative partners due to their benefits in group-hunting and warfare, thereby increasing the potential audience for risk takers.<ref name="Farthing-2005" /> The tendency of adolescent and young-adult males to engage in risky and aggressive behaviors is known as the 'young male syndrome'. His self-worth is tied to being perceived as a 'real man'. His likelihood of committing or falling victim to a violent crime peaks between his late teens and late twenties.<ref>{{Cite news|last=McAndrew|first=Frank T.|date=4 December 2015|title=If you give a man a gun: the evolutionary psychology of mass shootings|work=The Conversation|url=https://theconversation.com/if-you-give-a-man-a-gun-the-evolutionary-psychology-of-mass-shootings-51782|access-date=26 August 2021}}</ref>

Young females, on the other hand, are under strong peer pressure to be physically attractive, potentially leading to problems with their [[body image]].<ref name="tamiu">{{cite journal|last1=Ferguson|first1=Christopher J.|last2=Winegard|first2=Benjamin|last3=Winegard|first3=Bo M.|date=March 2011|title=Who is The Fairest One of All? How Evolution Guides Peer and Media Influences on Female Body Dissatisfaction|journal=Review of General Psychology|volume=15|issue=1|pages=11–28|doi=10.1037/a0022607|s2cid=4848392}}</ref> A teenage girl or young woman's bond with her first sexual partner is often deep. In both sexes, intense adolescent intrasexual competition, [[Puppy love|amorous infatuations]], and sexual experimentation are common.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Weisfeld|first1=Glenn E.|last2=Woodward|first2=Laura|date=1 January 2004|title=Current Evolutionary Perspectives on Adolescent Romantic Relations and Sexuality|url=https://www.jaacap.org/article/S0890-8567(09)61123-0/fulltext|journal=Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry|volume=43|issue=1|pages=11–19|doi=10.1097/00004583-200401000-00010|pmid=14691356 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> Those who have been well-treated by parents of the opposite sex often (unconsciously) use said parents as model for what a desirable mate ought to be.<ref name="Bainbridge-2009" />{{Reference page|page=301}} (See [[sexual imprinting]].)

Psychological research indicates the existence of a "reminiscence bump" between the ages of 10 and 30, a period important in human development, when people receive a substantial amount of feedback on their social status and reproductive desirability.<ref name="McAndrew-2016" /> Due to sex differences in mating strategies, it is more difficult for a female to alter the course of her reproductive career than it is more a male. In fact, females not only mature more quickly but also were historically more likely than males to marry and bear their first children before the age of 20. As a consequence, by late adolescence, it is, from the perspective of evolution, crucial that a girl finds herself a high-quality mate.<ref name="Benenson-2013" />

Whereas ancestral humans lived in small bands of related people of all ages, modern secondary school students share the same social environment as people from the same age groups from diverse backgrounds, an evolutionary novelty. Back then, social competition during adolescence proved crucial to future social and reproductive success, hence the [[Popularity contest|strong desire to be popular]]. Today, it is possible for people to relocate to a different place or transfer to another school. Still, the curiosity about how the lives of others for the sake of comparison remains. Teenagers are also quite conformist with regards to their peers, for under ancestral condition, social ostracism was generally deadly.<ref name="McAndrew-2016" /> In 21st-century society, youths who rebel against the dominant culture or figures of authority tend become more homogeneous with respect to their own subculture, making their behavior the opposite to any claims of [[counterculture]]. This synchronization occurs even if more than two choices are available, such as multiple styles of beard rather than whether or not to have a beard. Mathematician Jonathan Touboul who studies how information propagation through society affects human behavior calls this the [[Hipster (contemporary subculture)|hipster]] effect.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Brandeis University|date=1 March 2019|title=The hipster effect: Why anti-conformists always end up looking the same|work=Phys.org|url=https://phys.org/news/2019-03-hipster-effect-anti-conformists.html|access-date=31 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last1=Feingold|first1=Lindsey|last2=Garcia-Navarro|first2=Lulu|date=10 March 2019|title=Man Inadvertently Proves That Hipsters Look Alike By Mistaking Photo As Himself|work=NPR|url=https://www.npr.org/2019/03/10/702063209/man-inadvertently-proves-that-hipsters-look-alike-by-mistaking-photo-as-himself|access-date=31 December 2020}}</ref>

Youngsters who have a stressful childhood environment, due to, for instance, parental abuse or having divorced parents, tend to [[Precocious puberty|reach puberty early]]. It has been hypothesized that such early experiences prompts the body to mature quickly for the sake of early reproduction.<ref name=":45">{{Cite news |last=Weil |first=Elizabeth |date=March 30, 2012 |title=Puberty Before Age 10: A New 'Normal'? |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/04/01/magazine/puberty-before-age-10-a-new-normal.html |archive-url=https://archive.today/20190504071506/https://www.nytimes.com/2012/04/01/magazine/puberty-before-age-10-a-new-normal.html |archive-date=May 4, 2019 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=The New York Times}}</ref> In the United States, for example, girls raised by single parents have higher than average rates of [[teenage pregnancy]].<ref name=":14">{{Cite book |last=Sowell |first=Thomas |title=Social Justice Fallacies |publisher=Basic Books |year=2023 |isbn=978-1-5416-0392-9 |location=New York}}</ref>{{Reference page|page=10}}

=== Cultural traditions and popular culture ===
[[File:Kamasutra1.jpg|thumb|An illustration from the ''Kama Sutra'', published in India in the 3rd century A.D.]]
It remains unclear how culture modulates sex differences in mate choice.<ref name="Gangestad-2006" /><ref>Schmitt, D.P. (2011). When the difference is in the details: a critique of Zentner and Mitura (2012)" Stepping out of the caveman's shadow: Nations' gender gap predicts degree of sex differentiation in mate preferences". Evolutionary psychology: an international journal of evolutionary approaches to psychology and behavior, 10(4), 720–726.</ref> Cultural variations in mate preference can be due to the evolved sex differences in a given culture. Since physical attractiveness is thought to signal health and disease resistance, evolutionary psychologists have predicted that, in societies high in pathogen prevalence, people value attractiveness more in a mate, and cross-cultural data substantiates this claim.<ref>Gangestad, S.W., & Buss, D.M. (1993). [https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/handle/2027.42/30928/0000598.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y "Pathogen prevalence and human mate preferences"]. ''Ethology and Sociobiology'', 14(2), 89–96.</ref> Women in nations with high pathogen prevalence also show greater preferences for facial masculinity.<ref>DeBruine, L.M., Jones, B.C., Crawford, J.R., Welling, L.L., & Little, A.C. (2010). [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2894896/ "The health of a nation predicts their mate preferences: cross-cultural variation in women's preferences for masculinized male faces"]. ''Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences'', 277(1692), 2405–2410.</ref> Furthermore, since sexual contact with multiple individuals increases the risk of disease transmission, thereby increasing the costs of pursuing a short-term mating strategy, higher pathogen prevalence is associated with lower national SOI scores.<ref>Schaller, M., & Murray, D.R. (2008). [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18605861/ "Pathogens, personality, and culture: disease prevalence predicts worldwide variability in sociosexuality, extraversion, and openness to experience"]. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 95(1), 212.</ref> Finally, several studies have found that experimentally manipulating disease salience has a causal influence on attractiveness preferences and SOI scores in predicted directions.<ref>Murray, D.R., Jones, D.N., & Schaller, M. (2012). [http://www2.psych.ubc.ca/~schaller/MurrayJonesSchaller2013.pdf "Perceived threat of infectious disease and its implications for sexual attitudes"]. ''Personality and Individual Differences''.</ref><ref>Lee, A.J., & Zietsch, B.P. (2011). [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3210674/ "Experimental evidence that women's mate preferences are directly influenced by cues of pathogen prevalence and resource scarcity"]. ''Biology Letters'', 7(6), 892–895.</ref><ref>Little, A.C., DeBruine, L.M., & Jones, B.C. (2011). [http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/early/2010/11/30/rspb.2010.1925.short "Exposure to visual cues of pathogen contagion changes preferences for masculinity and symmetry in opposite-sex faces"]. ''Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences'', 278(1714), 2032–2039.</ref>

For millennia, books have been written to share advice on mate acquisition Such books are generally products of their historical eras and societies. Most of them, however, have been aimed at men, who, as a group, are less socially adept than women.<ref>{{Cite news |date=December 18, 2025 |title=What The Economist discovered at dating bootcamp |url=https://www.economist.com/christmas-specials/2025/12/18/what-the-economist-discovered-at-dating-bootcamp |archive-url=https://archive.today/20251218145824/https://www.economist.com/christmas-specials/2025/12/18/what-the-economist-discovered-at-dating-bootcamp |archive-date=December 18, 2025 |access-date=February 6, 2026 |work=The Economist}}</ref> But boredom can pose a threat to pair bonding. In response, many cultures have produced sex manuals over the centuries to help their married couples remain happily together. Such manuals feature a great variety of novel [[Sex position|sexual positions]] that a couple may try together. One example of such a book is the ''[[Kama Sutra]]'', written in India in the third century A.D. Unusual for its time, the ''Kama Sutra'' acknowledges female pleasure and agency.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=93-6}}[[File:SLNSW 10798 Catholic Assurance Ball at David Jones nine debutantes.jpg|thumb|Upper-class families in the West traditionally arrange for their children to attend debutante balls—such as this one from New South Wales, Australia—to ensure that they meet potential matches from the same background.|left]]As children [[Coming of age|come of age]], their parents and communities may organize social events to commemorate this important benchmark in their lives. In Latin America and parts of the United States with sizeable Hispanic communities, such as Houston, Texas, girls traditionally participate in the [[Quinceañera]] when they reach 15 years of age; this Catholic tradition affirms that they have become women and a commitment to [[chastity]] before marriage.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=137-8}} In the West, the upper class may arrange for their children to attend exclusives dances, such as a [[debutante|debutante ball]], in order to ensure that they would meet potential mates who share the same background.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=85}} In the United States, a formal dance party for high-school students known as [[prom]] has its origins in the debutante ball. Prom may treated as a milestone event, similar to a wedding, in which the participants were taking an important step into a new stage in their lives.<ref>{{cite book |author=Ann Anderson |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WCLjW3bh9QUC&pg=PA7 |title=High School Prom: Marketing, Morals and the American Teen |publisher=McFarland |year=2012 |isbn=978-1-476-60007-9 |pages=}}</ref>{{Reference page|pages=7-10}}

Different cultures seek to emphasize a woman's physical beauty in different ways, so much so that in some cases, what is considered normal in one society may seem excessive in another. Among the Surma and Mursi tribes of Ethiopia, a young woman's attractiveness is determined by the size of the [[lip plate]] she can put on; the larger the plate, the higher the [[bride price]], measured in terms of cattle, her family can demand.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=41}} For the Padaung people of Myanmar, women who can wear a large number of brass [[neck ring]]s, up to 32, are beautiful.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=44}} In ancient China, the custom was to emphasize and exaggerate the smallness of female feet; the practice of [[foot binding]], banned since the early twentieth century, began in childhood and was quite painful. But the view then was that women with such small feet—dubbed the "Golden Lotus"—were not only sexually attractive but also of high status, for only peasants had larger feet, or "duck feet" as they were pejoratively known. (The story of [[Cinderella]] originally came from China.) A similar trend today is the [[High-heeled shoe|high-heel shoe]], a Western invention, which also limits the mobility of the wearer.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=45}} High heels also make a woman appear taller, to have a raised backside (reminiscent of [[mammalian lordosis]], a sign of sexual receptivity), and look perkier (a sign of youth).<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|page=82}}  [[Belly dance|Belly dancing]], a tradition from the Middle East, draws the viewers' attention to the width of the woman's hips.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=46-7}} [[Cheerleading]], first introduced in the United States, highlights the [[Sexual attraction|sexual appeal]] of teenage girls and young women with routines that demonstrate their youth and vigor, but are not sexually provocative like the movements of pole dancers, or physically strenuous like those of professional athletes.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=59}}

Nevertheless, in all cultures, parents usually uphold a stricter dress code for their daughters than their sons, a phenomenon that can be explained by the [[#Parental investment|parental investment]] theory.<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|pages=113-4}} Similarly, there is traditionally a greater emphasis on chastity and [[virginity]] for females than for males as a consequence of parental uncertainty, a recurring problem for the latter.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=77}}[[File:Cheerleaders Mexicanas.jpg|thumb|Cheerleaders—seen here at a soccer match in Monterrey, Mexico—direct attention to their physical beauty but without being too provocative.]]Since social status is of great importance in terms of mate value for males, different cultures around the world have developed various traditions for men and boys to cultivate and display their status. Individuals and societies of lower status typically copy those of higher status. In the ancient eastern Mediterranean, for instance, the Jews and Arabs copied the tradition of [[Circumcision|male circumcision]] from the more affluent Egyptians.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=58-9}}

As already mentioned, in humans, female breasts have partly been a consequence of sexual selection. Hence, concealing them—by clothing or [[Handbra|hand bra]], say—means attenuating a potent sexual signal.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=26}} Different cultures have vastly different views on female [[toplessness]]. It is considered normal or acceptable in certain tribal or traditional societies, such as the Aboriginals of Australia,<ref name="news">{{cite news |date=27 February 2004 |title=Aborigines' fury over topless ban |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/world/asia-pacific/3493408.stm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230523133709/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/asia-pacific/3493408.stm |archive-date=May 23, 2023 |publisher=[[BBC News]]}}</ref> deemed indecent in others, such as the former Soviet Union.<ref name="Allyn">David Smith Allyn, ''Make love, not war'', pages 23–29, Taylor & Francis, 2001, {{ISBN|0-415-92942-3}}</ref> In the modern Western world, attitudes towards public nudity, including of women, have relaxed, something seen in clothing-optional or [[nude beach]]es and other [[Nude recreation|recreational locations]].<ref>{{Cite news |date=July 5, 2014 |title=Nudists on cruises |url=http://www.economist.com/news/united-states/21606292-right-bare-arms-legs-and-other-body-parts-nudists-cruises |archive-url=https://archive.today/20140706035035/http://www.economist.com/news/united-states/21606292-right-bare-arms-legs-and-other-body-parts-nudists-cruises |archive-date=July 6, 2014 |access-date=November 3, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref> However, sexuality activities may be illegal, even in these places.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Howie |first=Cherie |date=February 4, 2022 |title=Sexual activity at popular nudist beach not a first - community leader |url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/ladies-bay-nudist-beach-sexual-activity-incident-not-a-first-auckland-community-leader/DPBJOWCU4FSG7ZK32NZOIG65LE/ |access-date=June 23, 2022 |work=NZ Herald}}</ref> For instance, at the [[Naked party|naked parties]] organized at certain institutions of higher learning in the United States, sexual contact of any kind is strictly prohibited, even though nudity is required.<ref name="nyt-070107">{{cite news |last=Aviv |first=Rachel |author-link=Rachel Aviv |date=January 7, 2007 |title=Black Tie Optional |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/01/07/education/edlife/07nakedparties.html?_r=1&pagewanted=all&oref=slogin |url-access=subscription |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080807063729/http://www.nytimes.com/2007/01/07/education/edlife/07nakedparties.html?ei=5090&en=4ff3d98d49f32172&ex=1325826000&partner=rssuserland&emc=rss&pagewanted=all |archive-date=August 7, 2008 |access-date=February 1, 2024 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]]}}</ref> On the other hand, there are circumstances where sexual expression is acceptable or even expected, such as the [[wet T-shirt contest]], originally from the United States.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=58-9}}

Evolutionary biologist [[Charles Darwin]] reported there was a universal "passion for ornament" in the peoples he had countered during his voyage aboard [[HMS Beagle|HMS ''Beagle'']]. While icons of beauty in popular culture are frequently imitated, beauty in fact thrives on variety. As Darwin noted, "If everyone were cast in the same mold, here would be no such thing as beauty."<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=5}} While physical beauty may be dismissed as shallow or trivial in certain contexts, it plays an important role in human decision making and motivation.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=7}} Sexual appeal aside, a romantically unattached person may wish to signal his or her availability. In Tahiti, it is traditional for single people to wear a flower above the right ear. Those who are already in a relationship put their flowers on the left—that is, above the heart. Married men looking for an affair might temporarily relocate the flower from the left to right. The analogous Western practice is removing the [[Wedding ring|wedding band]].<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=77}}

=== Consumer psychology ===
[[File:1970sgirls.jpg|left|thumb|Fashion choices, such as short or [[Miniskirt|minidresses]], are partly shaped by mating behavior.]]

According to psychologist [[Gad Saad]], consumer behavior can only be truly understood in light of evolutionary psychology because consumer behavior "is rooted in a shared biological heritage based around four key Darwinian factors: survival, reproduction, kin selection, and reciprocal altruism."<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Saad|first=Gad|date=28 March 2013|title=Evolutionary consumption|url=https://myscp.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1016/j.jcps.2013.03.002|journal=Journal of Consumer Psychology|volume=23|issue=3|pages=351–371|doi=10.1016/j.jcps.2013.03.002|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name="Concordia University-2013">{{Cite news|last=Concordia University|date=12 June 2013|title=Big movies and other cultural products have evolutionary roots|work=Science Daily|url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2013/06/130612133317.htm|access-date=16 June 2021}}</ref> Consequently, products that can manipulate or enhance a person's body odor (perfumes and deodorants) and looks (cosmetics and plastic surgeries) are profitable businesses.<ref name="Fields-2022" /> While trends may be ephemeral, certain evolved universal tendencies persist, and these are exploited by the fashion, cosmetics, and plastic surgery industries.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=4}} Sophisticated cosmetics have been used since antiquity, with some of the earliest known examples being found in Egypt.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=57}} In Brazil at the end of the twentieth century, for instance, there were more people selling [[Avon Products|Avon cosmetics]] than there were members of the armed forces. Similarly, in the United States, more money was spent on cosmetics and plastic surgeries than on education or social services.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=6}} By the mid-2020s, the global cosmetics industry was worth well over US$400 billion.<ref>{{Cite news |date=January 20, 2026|title=Why the beauty industry is booming|url=https://www.economist.com/business/2026/01/20/why-the-beauty-industry-is-booming|access-date=March 5, 2026|work=The Economist}}</ref> Even women with "natural beauty" actually spend considerable amounts of time and money on makeup.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=13}} Psychiatrist [[Peter D. Kramer|Peter Kramer]] calls cosmetic surgery a form of [[psychopharmacology]] because it can help people feel better about themselves.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=20}} [[File:Marilyn Monroe shoes (23193798402).jpg|thumb|As mentioned in the previous section, many women wear high-heel shoes in order to accentuate their beauty.]]One way to signal one's socioeconomic status is [[conspicuous consumption]], or when individuals purchase luxurious items which provide little to no utility over less costly versions,<ref name="Nelissen-2011">{{Cite journal |last1=Nelissen |first1=Rob M.A. |last2=Meijers |first2=Marijn H.C. |date=2011 |title=Social benefits of luxury brands as costly signals of wealth and status |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |language=en |volume=32 |issue=5 |pages=343–355 |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2010.12.002 |bibcode=2011EHumB..32..343N}}</ref> thereby prioritizing self-promotion over economic sense.<ref name="Sundie-2011" /> For instance, in France, the hoarding of flour for cosmetic purposes was a common practice, even during times of food shortages, up until the French Revolution.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=6}} It is a common behavior of class and often involves strategic planning to maximize the audience of the display and the strength of the signal.<ref name="Boone-1998">{{Cite journal|last=Boone|first=James L.|date=1998|title=The evolution of magnanimity: When is it better to give than to receive?|journal=Human Nature|language=en|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–21|doi=10.1007/s12110-998-1009-y|issn=1045-6767|pmid=26197355|s2cid=45198951}}</ref> Most signaling explanations of conspicuous consumption predict the targets of the signal will predominately be potential mates.<ref name="Sundie-2011" /><ref name="De Fraja-2009">{{Cite journal|last=De Fraja|first=Gianni|date=2009|title=The origin of utility: Sexual selection and conspicuous consumption|url=https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00703547/file/PEER_stage2_10.1016%252Fj.jebo.2009.05.019.pdf|journal=Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization|language=en|volume=72|issue=1|pages=51–69|doi=10.1016/j.jebo.2009.05.019|s2cid=17910319 }}</ref> Among males, the information signaled is thought to go beyond genetic quality and signal the potential for investment, which can be attractive to those seeking both long-term and short-term mating strategies.<ref name="Sundie-2011" /> Among females, a suggested to benefit from conspicuous consumption in mating contexts is its hypothesized ability to demonstrate the commitment of one's partner<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Wang|first1=Yajin|last2=Griskevicius|first2=Vladas|date=1 February 2014|title=Conspicuous Consumption, Relationships, and Rivals: Women's Luxury Products as Signals to Other Women|journal=Journal of Consumer Research|language=en|volume=40|issue=5|pages=834–854|doi=10.1086/673256|issn=0093-5301}}</ref> and signal one's mate quality to rivals,<ref name="Hudders-2014">{{Cite journal|last1=Hudders|first1=Liselot|last2=De Backer|first2=Charlotte|last3=Fisher|first3=Maryanne|last4=Vyncke|first4=Patrick|date=2014|title=The Rival Wears Prada: Luxury Consumption as a Female Competition Strategy|journal=Evolutionary Psychology|language=en-US|volume=12|issue=3|pages=570–87|doi=10.1177/147470491401200306|issn=1474-7049|pmid=25299993|doi-access=free|pmc=10480915 }}</ref> both of which may help in intrasexual competition and deter [[Infidelity|mate poaching]].<ref name="Hudders-2014" /> However, the cost of a gift is not the only determinant of commitment. Women often prefer courtship gifts that are unique and thoughtful.<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|page=78}}
[[File:Michele_Merkin_1.jpg|left|thumb|Models are thought to be sexy because they are taller, thinner, and curvier than the average woman, but still heavier than anorexics.]]
In cases where food shortages are frequent, weight is a sign of status. But in a society of abundance, dietary restraints, especially an among women, becomes prevalent, as upwardly mobile women, those who "marry up" the social ladder, tend to be thinner than average as they are willing and able to avoid [[junk food]].<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|pages=200-1}} However, while [[supermodel]]s and [[Glamour photography|glamour models]] are taller and thinner than average women, they are also heavier than [[Anorexia nervosa|anorexics]]. They also tend to be more curvaceous, as indicated by their waist-to-hip-ratios of around 0.7, which men consider attractive. Although women go on a diet in order to make their bodies more attractive, this strategy does not help because their body shapes are not as curvy as those of models.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Tovée |first=Martin J. |last2=Mason |first2=Suzanne M. |last3=Emery |first3=Joanne L. |last4=McCluskey |first4=Sara E. |last5=Cohen-Tovée |first5=Esther M. |date=November 15, 1997 |title=Supermodels: stick insects or hourglasses? |url=https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140673605642389/fulltext |journal=The Lancet |volume=350 |issue=9089 |pages=1474–5}}</ref>

Conspicuous consumption may also be useful for problems outside of acquiring mates. This can involve attempts at attracting other cooperative partners, who stand to gain from the signalers ability to confer benefits should they form an alliance.<ref name="Boone-1998" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Barclay|first=Pat|title=The Handbook of Evolutionary Psychology|date=2015|publisher=American Cancer Society|isbn=978-1-119-12556-3|pages=1–19|language=en|chapter=Reputation|doi=10.1002/9781119125563.evpsych233}}</ref> As in mating contexts, there may also be benefits to intimidating rivals, thereby decreasing the likelihood of direct competition for resources in the future.<ref name="Hudders-2014" /> Its prevalence across cultures and social classes suggests that humans may be well suited to balancing the costs and benefits of the signal.<ref name="Nelissen-2011" /><ref name="De Fraja-2009" />

The notion that "sex sells" is now commonly accepted and utilized by advertisers.<ref>Jessica Dawn Blair, et al., "Ethics in advertising: sex sells, but should it?." ''Journal of Legal, Ethical and Regulatory Issues'' 9.1/2 (2006): 109+.</ref><ref>{{cite journal|first=Tom|last=Reichert|year=2002|title=Sex in advertising research: A review of content, effects, and functions of sexual information in consumer advertising|journal=Annu. Rev. Sex Res.|volume=13|pages=241–73|doi=10.1080/10532528.2002.10559806 |pmid=12836733}}</ref> Nevertheless, some cultures (such as France) are more receptive to [[sex in advertising]] than others (such as South Korea).<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Biswas|year=1992|title=A comparison of print advertisements from the United States and France|journal=Journal of Advertising|volume=21|issue=4|pages=73–81|doi=10.1080/00913367.1992.10673387}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Internet Ads with Sexual Imagery at a Critical Level: Survey {{!}} Be Korea-savvy|url=http://koreabizwire.com/internet-ads-with-sexual-imagery-at-a-critical-level-survey/65968?platform=hootsuite|access-date=12 September 2016|website=koreabizwire.com| date=8 September 2016 }}</ref>

{{Clear}}

=== Sensational journalism and gossip ===
[[File:Eugen von Blaas - Sharing the News 1904.jpg|thumb|''Sharing the News'' (1904) by [[Eugene de Blaas|Eugene von Blaas]]. Sensational news may serve the same purpose as gossip.]]Despite common objections, sensational news stories continue to attract a large audience. A 2003 analysis of 736 stories from 1700 to 2001 by Hank Davis and S. Lyndsay McLeod reveals that these stories could be categorized according themes with reproductive value, such as cheater detection and treatment of offspring. Davis and McLeod therefore propose that [[Sensationalism|sensational journalism]] serves the same purpose as [[gossip]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Davis|first1=Hank|last2=McLeod|first2=S.Lyndsay|date=May 2003|title=Why humans value sensational news: An evolutionary perspective|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/257492054|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=24|issue=3|pages=208–216|doi=10.1016/S1090-5138(03)00012-6 |bibcode=2003EHumB..24..208D }}</ref> Furthermore, much gossip concerns social affairs.<ref name="Delistraty-2014">{{Cite news |last=Delistraty |first=Cody C. |date=2 November 2014 |title=The Psychological Comforts of Storytelling |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/health/archive/2014/11/the-psychological-comforts-of-storytelling/381964/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210808201857/https://www.theatlantic.com/health/archive/2014/11/the-psychological-comforts-of-storytelling/381964/ |archive-date=8 August 2021 |access-date=9 September 2021 |work=The Atlantic}}</ref> It is the sharing of both positive and negative information about a third person who may or may not be absent from the group, and as such is useful for acquiring potentially useful information about the social structure, rivals, as well as allies. It may also be used for the purposes of intrasexual competition, or the denigration of rivals in order to elevate oneself, with men gossiping about access to resources (wealth and achievement) and women about looks and reputations. However, women appear to be more likely to gossip than men and to think of it more positively than men.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Davis|first1=Adam C.|last2=Dufort|first2=Caroline|last3=Desrochers|first3=Jessica|last4=Vaillancourt|first4=Tracy|last5=Arnocky|first5=Steven|date=3 October 2017|title=Gossip as an Intrasexual Competition Strategy: Sex Differences in Gossip Frequency, Content, and Attitudes|url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40806-017-0121-9|journal=Evolutionary Psychological Science|publisher=Springer|volume=4|issue=2|pages=141–153|doi=10.1007/s40806-017-0121-9|s2cid=53624611|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Springer|date=4 October 2017|title=Women use gossip to compete for a man's attention|work=Science Daily|url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2017/10/171004095049.htm|access-date=16 June 2021}}</ref> Teenage girls are particularly vulnerable to malicious gossip or false [[rumor]]s as a form of intrasexual competition for mates.<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|page=165}}

According to [[Francis T. McAndrew|Frank T. McAndrew]], the same psychological reasons that underlie more traditional forms gossip carry over to gossip about "celebrities" in the modern world because, on the evolutionary timescale, the birth of celebrity culture is a recent phenomenon.<ref name="science">{{cite news|last=McAndrew|first=Frank T.|date=October 2008|title=The Science of Gossip: Why we can't stop ourselves|work=[[Scientific American]]|department=Mind|url=http://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=the-science-of-gossip|access-date=18 May 2021}}</ref> Nevertheless, as [[Bertrand Russell]] observed, "No one gossips about other people's secret virtues, but only about their secret vices." Some of main themes of gossip include sexual transgression (such as infidelity or promiscuity) and sexual dysfunction.<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|page=165}} Sociologists initially coined the term "[[Parasocial interaction|parasocial]]" in 1956 to describe (one-sided) relationships people form with fictional characters on television, treating them as close friends or confidants, family members, or even lovers. In the twenty-first century, this term has been generalized to celebrity culture in general, as well as [[social media]] and artificially intelligent [[chatbot]]s.<ref name=":21" />

=== Romantic novels, fan fiction, and pornography ===
{{See also|Literary Darwinism}}
As defined by the [[Romance Writers of America]], a [[Romance novel|romantic novel]] features "a central love story and an emotionally satisfying and optimistic ending."<ref name="Soloski-2021" /> Many also carry erotic undertones,<ref name="Soloski-2021">{{Cite news|last=Soloski|first=Alexis|date=6 February 2021|title=For Television and Romance Novels, Love at Last?|work=The New York Times|department=Arts|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/12/28/arts/television/bridgerton-outlander-romance-novels.html|access-date=16 June 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210318092512/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/12/28/arts/television/bridgerton-outlander-romance-novels.html|archive-date=18 March 2021}}</ref> or are themselves [[Erotic literature|erotica]].<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|page=68}} According to the same organization, only a minority of those who read this genre are male.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Barnes |first=Brooks |date=June 2, 2022 |title=Forget Twitter. This Musk Is Into 'Toe Curling Yumminess.' |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/06/02/business/media/tosca-musk-passionflix.html |archive-url=https://archive.today/20220602134058/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/06/02/business/media/tosca-musk-passionflix.html |archive-date=June 2, 2022 |access-date=April 28, 2025 |work=The New York Times}}</ref> Indeed, evolutionary psychologists have gained valuable insights into women's mate choice by studying romance novels popular among women, such as those sold by [[Harlequin Enterprises|Harlequin]].<ref>{{Cite news|last=Flood|first=Allison|date=4 March 2010|title=Evolutionary psychologists turn attention to romantic fiction|work=The Guardian|department=Books|url=https://www.theguardian.com/books/2010/mar/04/evolutionary-psychologists-romantic-fiction|access-date=16 June 2021}}</ref> Popular contemporary female romance novels conform to strategies common among women, for example by avoiding short-term relationships, and as such pertain to their genetic interests.<ref name="Cox-2009">{{Cite journal|last1=Cox|first1=Anthony|last2=Fisher|first2=Maryanne|date=2009|title=The Texas billionaire's pregnant bride: An evolutionary interpretation of romance fiction titles|journal=Journal of Social, Evolutionary, and Cultural Psychology|volume=3|issue=4|pages=386–401|doi=10.1037/h0099308|doi-access=free}}</ref> Five of the most common words in such novels are, in order of decreasing frequency, ''love'', ''bride'', ''baby'', ''man'', and ''marriage''; while the most common themes are commitment, reproduction, high-value—i.e. masculine—males, and resources.<ref name="Cox-2009" /><ref name="Hobbs-2011" /> Romantic novels sell rather well, with around 10,000 new titles appearing each year in the U.S. alone.<ref name="Soloski-2021" /> Romance is also one of the key themes in [[Young adult literature|young-adult literature]].<ref name=":1" /> Furthermore, despite what people say in [[Etiquette|polite society]], there is substantial interest in erotic literature featuring bondage, dominance, and sadomasochism ([[BDSM]]), as sales figures of novels such as ''[[Fifty Shades of Grey|Fifty Shades of Gray]]'' (2011) reveal.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|page=157}}

[[Fan fiction]] is the online equivalent of romantic novels.<ref name="Yang-2011">{{Cite news|last=Yang|first=Wesley|date=29 July 2011|title=Sex, Lies and Data Mining|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2011/07/31/books/review/a-billion-wicked-thoughts-by-ogi-ogas-and-sai-gaddam-book-review.html|access-date=16 June 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210603222609/https://www.nytimes.com/2011/07/31/books/review/a-billion-wicked-thoughts-by-ogi-ogas-and-sai-gaddam-book-review.html|archive-date=3 June 2021}}</ref> During the first two decades of the 21st century, writing and reading fan fiction became a prevalent activity worldwide. Demographic data from various depositories revealed that those who read and wrote fan fiction were overwhelmingly young, in their teens and twenties, and female.<ref name="Anderson-2017">{{Cite news|last=Anderson|first=Porter|date=25 October 2017|title=YA Reading and Writing Trends from Wattpad's 60 Million Users|work=Publishing Perspectives|url=https://publishingperspectives.com/2017/10/watpad-ya-trends-publishing-insights-millennials/|access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref><ref name="Clements-2018">{{Cite news |last=Clements |first=Mikaella |author-link=Mikaella Clements |date=8 August 2018 |title=From Star Trek to Fifty Shades: how fanfiction went mainstream |url=https://www.theguardian.com/books/2018/aug/08/fanfiction-fifty-shades-star-trek-harry-potter |access-date=29 December 2020 |work=The Guardian}}</ref><ref name="Aragon-2019">{{Cite news|last=Aragon|first=Cecilia|date=27 December 2019|title=What I learned from studying billions of words of online fan fiction|work=MIT Technology Review|url=https://www.technologyreview.com/2019/12/27/131111/online-fan-fiction-learning-communities/|access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> For example, an analysis of the site [[FanFiction.Net|fanfiction.net]] published in 2019 by data scientists Cecilia Aragon and Katie Davis showed that some 60 billion words of contents were added during the previous 20 years by 10 million English-speaking people whose median age was 15½ years.<ref name="Aragon-2019" /> Much of fan fiction concerns the "[[Shipping (fandom)|shipping]]" or romantic pairing of fictional characters of interests.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Knorr|first=Catharine|date=5 July 2017|title=Inside the racy, nerdy world of fanfiction|work=CNN|url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/07/05/health/kids-teens-fanfiction-partner/index.html|access-date=4 January 2021}}</ref> Fan fiction writers base their work on various internationally popular cultural phenomena such as [[K-pop]], ''[[Star Trek]]'', ''[[Harry Potter]]'', ''[[Doctor Who]]'', and ''[[My Little Pony: Friendship Is Magic|My Little Pony]]—''known as "[[Canon (fiction)|canon]]"—as well as other things they considered important to their lives, like natural disasters.<ref name="Anderson-2017" /><ref name="Clements-2018" /><ref name="Aragon-2019" /> Socially dominant men—the so-called "alpha males"—are the most popular among women.<ref name="Yang-2011" />
[[File:2 Erotic Kama statues of Khajuraho Hindu Temple de Lakshmana Khajurâho India 2013.jpg|thumb|Erotic artworks—such as this sculpture from a 10th-century temple in [[Khajuraho]], India—have been [[History of erotic depictions|made throughout human history]].|left]]
Males, by contrast, are generally more interested in pornography because it carries the same cues to female fertility they look for under mating conditions. In particular, the absolute majority of those who watch [[hardcore pornography]] are men, both straight and gay; women prefer "positive" or [[Softcore pornography|softcore]] materials instead.<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|page=247}} Many new technological inventions—notably, the [[printing press]], [[videotape]]s, [[cable television]], and the [[Internet]]—were used to produce and distribute erotica soon after they became available, and the same thing is happening with ([[Generative artificial intelligence|generative]]) artificial intelligence (AI) in the twenty-first century.<ref name=":25">{{Cite news |date=November 27, 2025 |title=AI is upending the porn industry |url=https://www.economist.com/international/2025/11/27/ai-is-upending-the-porn-industry |archive-url=https://archive.today/20251227080222/https://www.economist.com/international/2025/11/27/ai-is-upending-the-porn-industry |archive-date=December 27, 2025 |access-date=January 4, 2026 |work=The Economist}}</ref> Online pornography is now ubiquitous and widely consumed. By the mid-2020s, of the 50 most visited websites, five were pornographic in nature. During the same period, the total revenue of the global pornographic industry was around US$100 billion, or twice that of AI, which was booming.<ref name=":25" />

In their book ''A Billion Wicked Thoughts'' (2011) analyzing search-engine results, cognitive scientists [[Ogi Ogas]] and Sai Gaddam wrote, "Men's brains are designed to objectify females. The shapely curves of female ornamentation indicate how many years of healthy childbearing remain across a woman's entire lifetime."<ref name="Yang-2011" /> The top search categories included ''youth'', ''breasts'', ''cheating wives'', ''penises'', and ''gay'';<ref name="Yang-2011" /> ''mom'' is more commonly searched for than ''teens'' or ''cheerleaders''.<ref name=":6">{{Cite news |last=Bennett |first=Jessica |date=April 24, 2011 |title='A Billion Wicked Thoughts': What the Internet Can Teach Us About Sex: Review |url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/a-billion-wicked-thoughts-what-the-internet-can-teach-us-about-sex-review/ |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250808211043/https://www.thedailybeast.com/a-billion-wicked-thoughts-what-the-internet-can-teach-us-about-sex-review/ |archive-date=August 8, 2025 |access-date=November 2, 2025 |work=Daily Beast}}</ref> Data scientist [[Seth Stephens-Davidowitz]], who also examined search results, found that ''babysitter'', ''teacher'', and ''cheerleader'' were the top search terms for pornography among men of all age groups.<ref name=":7">{{Cite book |last=Stephens-Davidowitz |first=Seth |author-link=Seth Stephens-Davidowitz |title=Everybody Lies: Big Data, New Data, and What the Internet Can Tell Us About Who We Really Are |publisher=HarperCollins |year=2017 |isbn=978-0-06-239085-1 |location=New York}}</ref>{{Reference page|page=52}} He also discovered a high level of interest in incest-themed pornographic materials featuring opposite-sex siblings as well as parents and their opposite-sex children. In particular, men who search for these videos enjoy watching mothers and sons whereas women prefer fathers and daughters.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|page=50}} But having a sexual liaison with ''mom'' commands a greater number of searches on both Google and pornographic websites than any other socially taboo relationships, including ''boss'', ''employee'', ''student'', or ''daughter's best friend''.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|page=51}} In Bangladesh and India, there is considerable interest in pornographic materials involving a woman breastfeeding her male partner, more than any other country in the world.<ref name=":7" />{{Reference page|page=19}}

By letting her test subject watch erotic materials of various kinds—straight sex, gay sex, and bonobos—sexologist [[Meredith Chivers]] discovered an excellent agreement between the self-reported arousal of men and the amount of blood flow to their genitals. Men were only aroused by videos of straight sex. On the other hand, Chivers found a clear mismatch between the self-reports of women and what her devices measured. While women seemed easily aroused by videos of all three categories, increased blood flow alone was not enough to induce arousal, which explains why attempts to create the equivalent of [[Sildenafil|Viagra]] for women have failed.<ref name="Yang-2011" /> This seems to correspond with the different mating behaviors of men and women.<ref name="Yang-2011" /> The sight of a muscular or otherwise masculine man is not enough to sexually arouse a woman; she also needs to [[Body image|view herself]] as desirable and to feel safe, comfortable, and emotionally connected.<ref name=":6" />

=== Music, film, television, and graphic arts ===
[[File:Liebesfruehling stuck-1917.jpg|thumb|''Spring of Love'' (1917) by [[Franz von Stuck]]. While popular music is full of reproductive messaging, it remains debated whether or not musical talent is a sexually selected trait or a by-product of other evolved characteristics.]]
A 2011 study by Dawn R. Hobbs and Gordon G. Gallup of songs dating back over four centuries show that reproductive messaging has been a common theme among the most popular of songs. Hobbs and Gallup observe that their "content analysis of these messages revealed 18 reproductive themes that read like topics taken from an outline for a course on evolutionary psychology." An overwhelming majority (about 92%) of the songs that made it to the Billboard Top 10 in 2009 contained reproductive messages. In fact, "further analyses showed that the bestselling songs in all three charts featured significantly more reproductive messages than those that failed to make it into the Top Ten." Among contemporary English-language songs, [[country music]] tends to focus on commitment, parenting, and rejection; pop music on sex appeal, reputation, short-term strategies, and fidelity assurance; and rhythms and blues ([[Rhythm and blues|R&B]]) and [[hip hop music|hip hop]] on sex appeal, resources, sex act, and status.<ref name="Hobbs-2011">{{Cite journal|last1=Hobbs|first1=Dawn R.|last2=Gallup|first2=Gordon G.|date=1 July 2011|title=Songs as a Medium for Embedded Reproductive Messages|url=|journal=Evolutionary Psychology|volume=9|issue=3|pages=390–416|doi=10.1177/147470491100900309|pmid=22947982|doi-access=free|pmc=10426854 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Grandoni|first=Dino|date=30 September 2011|title=92% of Top Ten Billboard Songs Are About Sex|work=The Atlantic|department=Culture|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/culture/archive/2011/09/92-top-ten-billboard-songs-are-about-sex/337242/|access-date=16 May 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210516214635if_/https://www.theatlantic.com/culture/archive/2011/09/92-top-ten-billboard-songs-are-about-sex/337242/|archive-date=16 May 2021}}</ref>

Hobbs and Gallup classified the reproductive messaging of the songs into 18 categories, including genitalia (e.g. "[[Baby Got Back]]" (1992) by Sir Mix-A-Lot), courtship displays and long-term mating ("[[I Want to Hold Your Hand|I Wanna Hold Your Hand]]" (1963) by The Beatles), short-term mating ("[[LoveGame]]" (2009) by Lady Gaga), foreplay and arousal ("[[Sugar, Sugar]]" (1969) by The Archies), sex act ("[[Honky Tonk Women]]" (1969) by the Rolling Stones), sexual prowess ("[[Sixty Minute Man]]" (1951) by Billy Ward and the Dominoes), promiscuity, reputation, and derogation ("[[Roxanne (The Police song)|Roxanne]]" (1978) by the Police), commitment and fidelity ("[[Love Story (Taylor Swift song)|Love Story]]" (2008) by Taylor Swift), access to resources ("[[For the Love of Money]]" (1973) by the O'Jays), rejection ("[[Red Light (David Nail song)|Red Light]]" (2009) by David Nail), infidelity, cheater detection, and mate poaching ("[[I Heard It Through the Grapevine]]" (1966) by Marvin Gaye), and parenting ("[[It Won't Be Like This for Long|It Won't Be Like This For Long]]" (2008) by Darius Rucker).<ref name="Hobbs-2011" />

In many developed countries today, women possess much greater economic power than in generations past, giving them the ability to become much choosier than in generations past. This trend is reflected in many contemporary songs by female musicians, such as "[[Manchild (Sabrina Carpenter song)|Manchild]]" (2025) by Sabrina Carpenter or "[[If He Wanted to He Would]]" (2025) by Perrie Edwards.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news |date=September 8, 2025 |title=Why female pop stars are lambasting mediocre men |url=https://www.economist.com/culture/2025/09/08/why-female-pop-stars-are-lambasting-mediocre-men |archive-url=https://archive.today/20251003085333/https://www.economist.com/culture/2025/09/08/why-female-pop-stars-are-lambasting-mediocre-men |archive-date=October 3, 2025 |access-date=October 8, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref>

Nevertheless, the evolutionary purpose of music, if such exists, remains unclear. Some researchers like [[Charles Darwin]] and [[Geoffrey Miller (psychologist)|Geoffrey Miller]] propose that it is a form of courtship that has evolved by means of sexual selection<ref name="Hobbs-2011" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Miller|first=Geoffrey|url=https://mitpress.mit.edu/books/origins-music|title=The Origins of Music|publisher=MIT Press|year=2001|isbn=978-0-262-73143-0|editor-last=Wallin|editor-first=Nils L.|pages=329–360|chapter=Evolution of human music through sexual selection|editor2-last=Merker|editor2-first=Bjorn|editor3-last=Brown|editor3-first=Steven}}</ref><ref name="Markus-2012">{{Cite news|last1=Markus|first1=Gary|last2=Miller|first2=Geoffrey|date=16 April 2012|title=Did Humans Invent Music?|work=The Atlantic|department=Culture|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/entertainment/archive/2012/04/did-humans-invent-music/255945/|access-date=16 June 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419202511if_/https://www.theatlantic.com/entertainment/archive/2012/04/did-humans-invent-music/255945/|archive-date=19 April 2021}}</ref> whereas others, such as [[Steven Pinker]] and [[Gary Marcus|Gary Markus]], reject it as "auditory cheesecake"—no more than a purely cultural invention that is a by-product of evolved traits such as cognition and language.<ref name="Hobbs-2011" /><ref name="Markus-2012" /><ref>{{Cite book|last=Buss|first=David M.|title=The Evolution of Desire|title-link=The Evolution of Desire|publisher=Basic Books|year=2003|isbn=978-0-465-09776-0|edition=2nd|location=New York|pages=182–6|chapter=Chapter 5: Attracting a Partner}}</ref> More broadly, it has been hypothesized that cognitive traits pertaining to "[[high culture]]" in general, including music, the visual arts, conversational skills, or humor, have been sexually selected for. But this claim also lacks adequate evidentiary support.<ref name="Bainbridge-2009" />{{Reference page|pages=297-8}}
[[File:Château de Versailles, salon des nobles, Pygmalion priant Vénus d'animer sa statue, Jean-Baptiste Regnault.jpg|left|thumb|''Pygmalion Praying to Venus to Animate His Statue'' (1786) by [[Jean-Baptiste Regnault]]. The desire for the perfect mate is found in numerous stories from ancient to modern.]]
A similar pattern is found in popular movies, where themes of survival (fighting epic battles), reproduction (courtship), kin selection (treatment of family members), and altruism (saving a stranger's life) are ubiquitous.<ref name="Concordia University-2013" /> Indeed, as in the case with novels or mythology, the number of basic plots is rather small.<ref name="Delistraty-2014" /> One such example is the [[love triangle]], a common and, indeed, flexible [[dramatic device]]. It can be found not just in historical literature, such as the [[Trojan War]] in Greek mythology or [[Matter of Britain|Arthurian legend]] from the Middle Ages, but also in some modern television programs, like ''[[The Summer I Turned Pretty (TV series)|The Summer I Turned Pretty]]'' (2023–25), adapted from a novel series of the same name by Jenny Han.<ref>{{Cite news |date=September 16, 2025 |title=People are going pretty mad for "The Summer I Turned Pretty" |url=https://www.economist.com/culture/2025/09/16/people-are-going-pretty-mad-for-the-summer-i-turned-pretty |archive-url=https://archive.today/20250916145930/https://www.economist.com/culture/2025/09/16/people-are-going-pretty-mad-for-the-summer-i-turned-pretty |archive-date=September 16, 2025 |access-date=October 10, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Lim |first=Audrey |date=September 19, 2025 |title=Team Conrad v. Team Jeremiah: The History and Appeal of Love Triangles in Media |url=https://www.andrews.edu/life/student-movement/issues/2025-09-19/ae_lovetriangles.html |access-date=October 10, 2025 |work=The Student Movement |publisher=Andrews University}}</ref> However, even though the standard assumption in many movies and stories is that people are looking to get married or at least desire a long-term partner, this is not necessarily true in real life.<ref name="Ferdman-2016a" /> But finding—or even creating—the perfect mate, as can be seen films such as ''[[Blade Runner]]'' (1982) or ''[[Her (2013 film)|Her]]'' (2013), is a plot that can be traced back to antiquity in the form of the myth of [[Pygmalion (mythology)|Pygmalion]] the sculptor from ancient Greece.<ref name=":1" />

Popular culture can also shape public perception of mating and matrimony. For example, the American television program ''[[Sex and the City]]'' (1998–2004), whose protagonists are single professional young women living in New York City, has made the idea of remaining unmarried more acceptable, even appealing, to Middle Eastern women.<ref name=":9" />

Various individuals—such as painter [[Albrecht Dürer]], historian of art [[Kenneth Clark]], and anthropologist [[Donald Symons]]—have recognized the universal existence of beauty ideals in the human mind. For instance, [[Zeuxis (painter)|Zeuxis]] gathered five of the most beautiful living women in Croton as models for his painting of [[Helen of Troy]] from Greek mythology. Many famed beauties like Helen are not described in the original [[Source text|source materials]]. A modern example of a composite depiction of physical beauty is the film ''[[Flashdance]]'' (1983), in which close-up shots of the main character were not of her body. While the use of [[Double (occupation)|body doubles]] in case the main actor lacks the necessary athletic skills is nothing new, it is also common practice to match different bodies to different faces in order to create an ideal overall look.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|page=|pages=11-2}} Nevertheless, universal standards of beauty do not exist; as previously mentioned, they can change over time even within the same society or civilization.<ref name="Etcoff-1999" />{{Reference page|pages=15-8}}

=== Online dating ===
In the past, using dating services, including those available via a computer, was seen as a measure of last resort, but things started to change in the late twentieth century.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Morris |first=Desmond |author-link=Desmond Morris |title=The Human Sexes: A Natural History of Man and Woman |publisher=St. Martin's Press |year=1997 |isbn=0-312-18311-9 |location=New York}}</ref>{{Reference page|page=85}} Following the commercialization of [[smartphone]]s in the late 2000s, the number of online daters has grown dramatically in Western countries.<ref name=":24" /> [[Online dating service]]s have made it much easier for those who would otherwise never meet because their social circles do not intersect (perfect strangers) to find one another and pursue a romantic relationship together.<ref name="Ferdman-2016a">{{Cite news |last=Ferdman |first=Roberto A. |date=March 23, 2016 |title=How well online dating works, according to someone who has been studying it for years |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2016/03/23/the-truth-about-online-dating-according-to-someone-who-has-been-studying-it-for-years/ |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200507035950/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2016/03/23/the-truth-about-online-dating-according-to-someone-who-has-been-studying-it-for-years/ |archive-date=May 7, 2020 |access-date=October 12, 2024 |newspaper=The Washington Post}}</ref> They are especially useful for middle-aged individuals who have fewer options in real life, compared to those in their 20s,<ref name="Ferdman-2016a" /> as well as for those with highly unconventional lifestyle choices, small social circles, or extreme shyness.<ref name=":27">{{Cite news |date=February 12, 2026 |title=Need a bit of dating help? The caveman's guide to romance |url=https://www.economist.com/culture/2026/02/12/need-a-bit-of-dating-help-the-cavemans-guide-to-romance |access-date=March 13, 2026 |work=The Economist}}</ref> Compared to heterosexual couples, same-sex ones are much likely to have met online.<ref name="Ferdman-2016a" /> [[File:Allegory of Youth and Age (Louis Lagrenée).jpg|thumb|''Allegory of Youth and Age'' (1771) by [[Louis-Jean-François Lagrenée]]. As in real life, men of high status or great wealth typically look for much younger women on online dating platforms.]]
Such platforms offer goldmines of information for social scientists studying human mating behavior.<ref name="Whitehead-2017">{{Cite news|last=Whitehead|first=Kate|date=24 September 2017|title=Chinese online dating giant Zhenai.com shows money still driver in politically incorrect matchmaking world|work=South China Morning Post|department=Lifestyle|url=https://www.scmp.com/news/world/article/1426600/researchers-claim-have-method-improves-online-dating-odds|access-date=19 December 2019}}</ref><ref name=":11">{{Cite news|last=Meyer|first=Robinson|date=10 August 2018|title=Dude, She's (Exactly 25 Percent) Out of Your League|work=The Atlantic|department=Science|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/science/archive/2018/08/online-dating-out-of-your-league/567083/|access-date=1 December 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|date=15 July 2016|title=How Tinder "Feedback Loop" Forces Men and Women into Extreme Strategies|work=MIT Technology Review|department=Tech Policy|url=https://www.technologyreview.com/s/601909/how-tinder-feedback-loop-forces-men-and-women-into-extreme-strategies/|access-date=8 December 2019}}</ref> Nevertheless, as of 2017, no new pattern has been identified; to the contrary, scientists have only found the strengthening of gender stereotypes,<ref>{{Cite news|last=Purvis|first=Jeanette|date=10 February 2017|title=Why Tinder is so 'evilly satisfying'|work=The Conversation|url=https://theconversation.com/why-tinder-is-so-evilly-satisfying-72177|access-date=16 June 2021}}</ref> namely the attention to a prospective mate's socioeconomic status among women,<ref name="Jagannathan-20182" /><ref name="Whitehead-2017" /> and the preference for youth and beauty among men.<ref name="Salam-2018" /><ref name="Jagannathan-20182" /> In particular, exceptionally wealthy men usually look for much younger women,.<ref name="Whitehead-20173"/>  while women who date online are much more picky than in real life.<ref name=":27" />

While ancestral humans had evolve to select mates from a relatively small community, modern online daters face a myriad of potential matches, making it [[Overchoice|difficult for them to choose]]. In response, they impose many arbitrary criteria such as minimum heights or professions.<ref name=":27" /> Because online dating can be highly competitive,<ref name=":24">{{Cite news |date=November 6, 2025 |title=All over the rich world, fewer people are hooking up and shacking up |url=https://www.economist.com/briefing/2025/11/06/all-over-the-rich-world-fewer-people-are-hooking-up-and-shacking-up |archive-url=https://archive.today/20251109164340/https://www.economist.com/briefing/2025/11/06/all-over-the-rich-world-fewer-people-are-hooking-up-and-shacking-up |archive-date=November 9, 2025 |access-date=December 22, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref> both sexes commonly misrepresent themselves when it comes to attractive or undesirable characteristics,<ref>{{Cite news |last=Markowitz |first=David |date=22 August 2018 |title=The lies we tell on dating apps to find love |url=https://theconversation.com/the-lies-we-tell-on-dating-apps-to-find-love-101061 |access-date=26 August 2021 |work=The Conversation}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Markowitz |first1=David M. |last2=Hancock |first2=Jeffrey T. |date=June 2018 |title=Deception in Mobile Dating Conversations |url=https://academic.oup.com/joc/article/68/3/547/4986443 |journal=Journal of Communication |publisher=Oxford Academic |volume=68 |issue=3 |pages=547–569 |doi=10.1093/joc/jqy019 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> such as being to short or too heavy.<ref name=":24" /> Only a minority of online daters receive most of the matches. (See [[Pareto principle|Pareto's rule]].) Disclosing the number of matches received by other users was found to improve the [[supply and demand]] balance, that is, to reduce "congestion" and thus give users with lower mate value a greater chance of finding a match.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Huang |first1=Ni |last2=He |first2=Yumei |last3=Xu |first3=Xingchen |last4=Hong |first4=Yili |date=2019 |title=Designing Demand Information Disclosure in the Presence of Capacity Constraints: A Large-Scale Randomized Field Experiment on a Matching Platform |url=https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3514033 |journal=SSRN Electronic Journal |doi=10.2139/ssrn.3514033 |issn=1556-5068 |ssrn=3514033}}</ref> Social scientists have also been able to establish the existence of dating "leagues" (or hierarchies of mate value). People are unlikely to respond to suitors they deem to be less desirable than they are.<ref name=":11" /> As sociologist Mark Newman explains, "There is a [[trade-off]] between how far up the ladder you want to reach and how low a reply rate you are willing to put up with."<ref name="Davison-2018">{{Cite news |last=Davison |first=Angus |date=August 8, 2018 |title=Online dating: Aim high, keep it brief, and be patient |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-45114692 |access-date=December 2, 2019 |work=BBC News |department=Science and Environment}}</ref> No longer do people looking for a mate have to confine themselves to their own backgrounds, though in practice, the data still indicates assortative mating.<ref name="Ferdman-2016a" />

Concerns that online dating makes people more "superficial" by giving them an incentive to judge one another based on looks are unfounded, since this is how humans normally behave. Moreover, while there are online dating sites geared towards short-term sexual relationships (hookups), others are designed to help those looking for a long-term arrangement, including marriage. Individuals who pursue the latter option are no less successful in finding the right mates.<ref name="Ferdman-2016a" />

=== Politics ===
In general, the emotion of [[disgust]] can be divided into three categories: pathogen disgust, sexual disgust, and moral disgust. Sexual disgust leads to the avoidance of individuals and behaviors that jeopardizes one's long-term mating success. Moral disgust is being repelled by socially abnormal behaviors.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Tybur|first1=J. M.|last2=Lieberman|first2=D.|last3=Griskevicius|first3=V.|year=2009|title=Microbes, mating, and morality: Individual differences in three functional domains of disgust|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=97|issue=1|pages=103–122|citeseerx=10.1.1.186.6114|doi=10.1037/a0015474|pmid=19586243 |bibcode=2009JPSP...97..103T }}</ref>

Some evolutionary psychologists have argued that mating strategies can influence political attitudes. According to this perspective, different mating strategies are in direct strategic conflict. For instance, the stability of long-term partnerships may be threatened by the availability of short-term sexual opportunities. Therefore, public policy measures that impose costs on casual sex may benefit people pursuing long-term mating strategies by reducing the availability of short-term mating opportunities outside of committed relationships. One public policy measure that imposes costs on people pursuing short-term mating strategies, and may thereby appeal to sexually restricted individuals, is the banning of abortion. In a doctoral dissertation, the psychologist Jason Weeden conducted statistical analyses on public and undergraduate datasets supporting the hypothesis that attitudes towards abortion are more strongly predicted by mating-relevant variables than by variables related to views on the [[sanctity of life]].<ref>Weeden, Jason (2003). ''[https://repository.upenn.edu/dissertations/AAI3087480/ Genetic interests, life histories, and attitudes towards abortion]''. Scholarly Commons. University of Pennsylvania.</ref>

Weeden and colleagues have also argued that attitudes towards drug legalization are driven by individual differences in mating strategies. Insofar as sexually restricted individuals associate recreational drug use with promiscuity, they may be motivated to oppose drug legalization. Consistent with this, one study found that the strongest predictor of attitudes towards drug legalization was scores on the SOI.<ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1098/rspb.2010.0608 | title=Sex, drugs and moral goals: Reproductive strategies and views about recreational drugs | year=2010 | last1=Kurzban | first1=Robert | last2=Dukes | first2=Amber | last3=Weeden | first3=Jason | journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences | volume=277 | issue=1699 | pages=3501–3508 | pmid=20554547 | pmc=2982222 }}</ref> This relationship remained strong even when controlling for personality traits, political orientation, and moral values. By contrast, nonsexual variables typically associated with attitudes towards drug legalization were strongly attenuated or eliminated when controlling for SOI and other sexuality-related measures. These findings were replicated in Belgium, Japan, and the Netherlands.<ref>Quintelier, K.J., Ishii, K., Weeden, J., Kurzban, R., & Braeckman, J. (2013). [https://biblio.ugent.be/publication/3063186/file/6781623.pdf "Individual Differences in Reproductive Strategy are Related to Views about Recreational Drug Use in Belgium, The Netherlands, and Japan"]. ''Human Nature'', 24(2), 196–217.</ref> Weeden and colleagues have made similar arguments and have conducted similar analyses in regard to religiosity; that is, religious institutions may function to facilitate high-fertility, monogamous mating and reproductive strategies.<ref>Weeden, J., Cohen, A.B., & Kenrick, D.T. (2008). [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3161130/ "Religious attendance as reproductive support"]. ''Evolution and Human Behavior'', 29(5), 327–334.</ref>

With the development of the concept of privacy, legal arguments have been framed in favor of tolerance for, if not legalization of, abortion, homosexuality, and prostitution. In many jurisdictions today, adultery (by women) is no longer deemed a crime that warrants the death penalty.<ref name=":13" />

=== Religion ===
{{See also|Patriarchy}}
[[File:THE FAT LADY - GODDESS - TARXIEN TEMPLES (1745555567).jpg|thumb|Ruins of a mother goddess statue found at the [[Tarxien Temples]] (circa 3,000 B.C.) in what is now Malta|left]]
Prehistoric societies worshiped [[mother goddess]]es as symbols of fertility of the soil and of women. Figurines and even statues were made in honor of these deities. In the [[History of agriculture|early days of agriculture]], it was common for women to pray to mother goddesses in hope of bearing many children to till the land. Copulation was a feature in myths and legends from these ancient peoples.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=196-200}}

But since human societies became more urbanized following the [[Neolithic Revolution|Agricultural Revolution]], women have been commonly treated as possessions, a form of mate guarding frequently enforced by religion. In conservative Islamic countries, such as Afghanistan, women are expected to be veiled; they are strictly prohibited from showing their hair or skin in public. However, this cultural tradition actually came from the ancient [[Assyria]] and was used to label high-status females; slave girls were not allowed to be veiled.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=205}} In parts of the Middle East and North Africa today, [[female genital mutilation]], often done to children, is justified on the grounds of that the elimination of female sexual pleasure would prevent them from straying from their (future) husbands or being promiscuous. Today, a woman may still be [[Stoning|stoned to death]] for alleged adultery in some regions.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|page=|pages=208-11}}

In India, before it was banned by British colonial authorities in the early nineteenth century, there was a Hindu custom called "[[Sati (practice)|satee]]" for a widow to be burned alive at the funeral pyre of her husband in order to keep him company in the after life. Many women voluntarily jumped in, but others were thrown in by their own families. Those who successfully avoided immolation were socially ostracized. On the other hand, men were not expected to show such devotion to their wives.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=165-7}} 
[[File:Louis-Jean-François Lagrenée - Sarah présente Agar à Abraham.jpg|thumb|''[[Sarah]] Presenting [[Hagar]] to [[Abraham]]'' (circa 1781) by [[Louis-Jean-François Lagrenée]]. There are numerous examples of high-status men acquiring multiple mates in the Abrahamic religions.]]
In the [[Old Testament]], there are numerous examples of powerful men—kings, judges, and patriarchs—taking advantage of their high social status and their religion to acquire multiple mates, namely, wives, concubines, slaves, and other men's wives and consequently fathered many children.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Betzig |first=Laura |date=2005 |title=Politics as Sex: The Old Testament Case |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |volume=3 |issue=1 |article-number=147470490500300122 |doi=10.1177/147470490500300122|doi-access=free }}</ref> In Orthodox Jewish communities, the word ''scholar'' is preserved not for mathematicians, scientists, or historians, but rather those dedicated to the study of the Torah and the Talmud, who have access to the most beautiful women.<ref name=":4" />{{Reference page|pages=174-5}} In [[medieval Europe]], Christianity, the supreme cultural force, required women to be [[Modesty|modest]] and viewed sexual intercourse as sinful except for the purposes of procreation, in which case it was deemed a necessary evil. Indeed, women were treated as below men and potentially dangerous because of their carnal appeal.<ref name=":0" />{{Reference page|pages=236-7}}

On the other hand, there is evidence that as a society becomes wealthier, more urbanized, and more secular, religion is becoming increasingly irrelevant in the role of matchmaking or encouraging matrimony.<ref name="Ferdman-2016b" /> For instance, in Iran, an otherwise conservative Islamic theocracy, many women are prioritizing their education and autonomy.<ref name=":9">{{Cite news |date=August 25, 2012 |title=The attraction of solitude |url=https://www.economist.com/international/2012/08/25/the-attraction-of-solitude |archive-url=https://archive.today/20240722143614/https://www.economist.com/international/2012/08/25/the-attraction-of-solitude |archive-date=July 22, 2024 |access-date=November 7, 2025 |newspaper=The Economist}}</ref>
